source.title: Heterogeneous GPU headroom miscount causing silent dedicated shortfalls
source.creator: Priya Natarajan
source.description: Multiple enterprise customers observed silent capacity shortfalls in Reserved Pools which manifested as increased cold-starts and higher p95 latency during burst traffic windows. Initial triage showed autoscaler did not add headroom quickly enough for tenants using GPU variants with lower vCPU equivalence, causing the scheduler to under-allocate across heterogeneous pools. This ticket tracks the investigation, immediate mitigations, hotfix rollout, and follow-up reliability work.
timeline: 2026-03-09 09:12 - Pager alert: Reserved-pool-az-3 utilization hit 98% for reserved-pool-id rp-84; spike in tenant error rate reported by monitoring (acme-health)
2026-03-09 09:25 - Customer support escalated to SRE and PM; initial mitigation: increase tenant burst fallback timeout to 30s
2026-03-09 10:40 - Engineering rollout: emergency routing rule enabled to route top-tier tenants to cold fallback pool; short-term relief observed
2026-03-10 14:00 - Deeper investigation started by autoscaler team; repro tests run against sim cluster with heterogeneous GPU types
2026-03-11 16:30 - Identified headroom calc uses normalized vCPU heuristics that assume homogeneous GPU-vCPU mapping; miscount occurs when heterogenous GPU types mixed in same AZ
2026-03-12 08:20 - Hotfix branch created to include GPU-type weight adjustment and per-GPU-type headroom accounting (PR: github.com/redwood-inference/autoscaler/pull/1297)
2026-03-13 20:10 - Canary rollout to us-west-2 reserved pools; metrics show 45% reduction in coldstarts for affected tenants
2026-03-14 09:00 - Post-canary rollout to remaining regions with slow ramp and feature flag; customer comms prepared for impacted accounts
source.investigation_notes: Root analysis combined telemetry from reserved-pool allocator, lease table, and VM/GPU capacity exporter. Two compounding failures found: (1) headroom estimator normalized all GPU SKUs to a single vCPU-equivalent value derived from an outdated SKU map, causing underestimation for older high-memory GPUs; (2) burst-credit reconciler omitted pending preemptible vCPU reservations during a scale-up window, so autoscaler believed more free capacity was available than actually committed. Additionally, KV cache pressure caused eviction spikes which increased request latencies in a feedback loop.
source.root_cause: Autoscaler headroom estimator used a stale GPU-to-vCPU normalization map and lacked per-GPU-type accounting. This led to underprovisioning when heterogeneous GPU SKUs were present. The burst-credit reconciler race allowed temporary double-counting of available headroom. Together these caused reserved pools to appear healthier than they were and delayed scale-ups until backpressure had already impacted customers.
mitigation: Immediate: enable safe fallback policy that routes high-priority tenants to dedicated cold fallback pools and increases burst timeout for enterprise plans
Hotfix: update headroom estimator to use live SKU weights and per-GPU-type buckets (PR: github.com/redwood-inference/autoscaler/pull/1297)
Hotfix: add short-circuit in scale controller to trigger emergency scale-ups when p95 latency or queue depth thresholds breached despite headroom > 0
Telemetry: instrument lease-table commit traces and add alerts for discrepancy between reported and committed capacity
Customer comms: notify impacted customers with timeline and mitigation steps; offer credits if SLA breach confirmed
action_items: Marco Ruiz - merge and monitor PR 1297 and roll out feature-flagged across regions
Priya Natarajan - coordinate customer comms and SLA triage for Acme Health and two other accounts
Jing Zhao - add unit + integration tests for per-GPU-type headroom logic in autoscaler
SRE - add new runbook entry for headroom drift and emergency fallback activation
Telemetry - enrich reserved-pool dashboard to show SKU mix, lease commits vs reported headroom
source.postmortem_summary: Contributing factors included stale SKU normalization, burst-credit reconciler race, and insufficient telemetry around committed vs available capacity. The hotfix (autoscale-2.4.0-hotfix) restores per-GPU-type accounting, adds emergency scale triggers, and improves telemetry. Canary results are positive; we will monitor for three full traffic cycles before closing the incident and will schedule a follow-up to harden unit tests and add cross-region chaos tests.
source.comms: Prepared customer-facing post explaining root cause and mitigations. Sent initial notification to impacted accounts on 2026-03-13 and follow-up on 2026-03-14 with expected timeline for full rollout. Internal all-hands summary posted to #incidents channel.

source.title: Tenant prefetch circuit breaker and jittered scale controls
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Follow-up action item from recent hosted API GPU exhaustion incident where tenant prefetch and aggressive priming caused rapid queue buildup and cascading throttles. This ticket tracks design, implementation, testing, and rollout of a multi-pronged mitigation: a tenant-level prefetch circuit breaker, request admission safeguards that cap prefetch concurrency, and jittered scale-up to avoid synchronized priming across multiple tenants. Goal: prevent multi-tenant cascade events that saturate GPU pools while preserving tail latency for interactive customers.
source.incident_summary: On 2026-03-05 an increase in tenant prefetch traffic caused a coordinated priming wave across multiple hosted GPU pools. Autoscaler primed capacity in large bursts while admission logic allowed uncontrolled background prefetches, leading to queue amplification and eviction of active inference work. The incident resulted in elevated 95/99 latency and several customer complaints about dropped requests.
timeline_highlights: 2026-03-05 02:14 UTC: surge of concurrent prefetch jobs from three tenants observed
2026-03-05 02:18 UTC: autoscaler triggered broad prewarm across affected pools
2026-03-05 02:21 UTC: admission controller failed to limit prefetch concurrency; GPU queue grew > 5x baseline
2026-03-05 02:30 UTC: cascading throttles and soft-evictions started impacting interactive routes
2026-03-05 03:05 UTC: mitigation: emergency manual cap on prefetch configured; queue growth slowed
2026-03-05 05:00 UTC: full recovery after capacity redistribution and tenant coordination
root_causes: Unbounded tenant prefetch/concurrent priming allowed large background jobs to occupy GPU scheduler slots
Autoscaler priming logic lacks jitter and can trigger across many pools simultaneously
Admission controller admission thresholds were tuned for steady-state throughput, not transient prefetch spikes
Insufficient telemetry on prefetch vs live inference token-level queues prevented early detection
acceptance_criteria: New prefetch circuit breaker prevents more than N concurrent prefetch tasks per tenant (configurable)
Autoscaler priming is jittered and rate-limited; we measure a 5x reduction in synchronized priming events in chaos tests
Admission controller enforces a soft cap that preserves <5% latency SLO degradation for interactive routes under prefetch bursts
Dashboards show separate signals for prefetch queue occupancy and live-inference queue occupancy with alerts
source.design_notes: Proposed approach: implement tenant_prefetch_score that weights recent live inference rate and token spend; if score below threshold, route prefetch to delayed queue. Circuit breaker state machine: CLOSED -> PROBING -> OPEN with exponential backoff; probes allow small sample prefetches. Autoscaler jitter: randomize priming across pool boundaries within a 60-180s window and apply per-tenant prewarm quotas. Admission change required: decrement prefetch admission priority by one tier, and add a fast-fail path for prefetch requests if pool slot usage > 80%.
implementation_tasks: Add tenant_prefetch_score and config knobs to admission service (ENG)
Implement prefetch circuit breaker state machine and metrics (ENG)
Modify autoscaler priming behavior to include jitter and per-tenant prewarm quotas (SRE/ENG)
Add soft-eviction tiers and priority-preserving ejection logic (ENG)
Build telemetry: prefetch-queue occupancy, prefetch:live ratio, per-tenant priming events (Observability)
End-to-end tests and chaos experiments to simulate simultaneous tenant priming (QA/ENG)
Update runbook and oncall playbooks with prefetch-enforcement steps (SRE)
Rollout plan with canary on 5% of pools then 25% then global (ENG/SRE)
Customer communication template for tenants with heavy prefetch behavior (Support/PM)
risks_and_mitigation: Risk: over-restricting prefetch could degrade throughput for legitimate batch workloads; mitigation: make circuit breaker thresholds tenant-configurable and opt-in for batch tiers
Risk: autoscaler jitter increases latency of capacity availability; mitigation: tune jitter window and allow emergency on-demand burst for latency-critical routes
Risk: rollout complexity across Hosted/Dedicated/Private; mitigation: implement feature-flag gating and disable in Private by default
source.telemetry_and_alerting: Create two new Grafana dashboards: Prefetch Insights (per-tenant) and Priming Health (pool synchronization). Alerting: page if prefetch_queue_occupancy > 20% of pool capacity for > 2 minutes or priming_synchronization_index > 0.6 for any region.
source.testing_plan: Unit tests for circuit breaker state machine, integration tests for admission service modifications, and chaos test simulating 30 tenant prefetch jobs across 10 pools. Define SLIs: prefetch-to-live ratio not exceeding 0.3 during blasts and 99p latency within SLO for interactive routes.
source.rollout_plan: Feature-flagged rollout: enable for non-critical pools first (5%), monitor for 48h, then 25%, then 100%. Have immediate rollback flag to disable prefetch circuit breaker and revert autoscaler jitter. Coordinate with Support to notify top 20 tenants potentially impacted.
owners_and_stakeholders: Eng Owners: Diego Morales (runtime), Priya Nair (admission)
action_items: (ENG) Implement circuit breaker prototype and expose metrics - owner: Diego Morales - ETA: 2026-03-26
(SRE/ENG) Add jitter to priming and implement per-tenant prewarm quotas - owner: Liam O'Connor - ETA: 2026-03-29
(OBS) Create dashboards and alerts for prefetch signals - owner: Sofia Martinez - ETA: 2026-03-19
(QA) Run chaos experiments and validate SLO preservation - owner: QA Team - ETA: 2026-03-31
(PM/Support) Prepare tenant communications and usage guidance - owner: Rahul Kapoor / Elena Petrova - ETA: 2026-03-20
(ENG) Update admission controller and add soft-fail path for prefetch - owner: Priya Nair - ETA: 2026-03-30
(SRE) Update runbook and oncall playbook with emergency cap procedure - owner: Liam O'Connor - ETA: 2026-03-18
comments: 2026-03-12 - Ava Chen: Kicked off initial design session with runtime and SRE. Focus on minimizing customer-visible latency hits while preventing queue amplification.
2026-03-13 - Diego Morales: Prototype state machine implemented locally; need telemetry hooks before QA tests.
2026-03-14 - Priya Nair: Admission changes ready for code review; will add configuration flags for per-tenant thresholds.
2026-03-14 - Sofia Martinez: Dashboards drafted; need metric names confirmed before wiring alerts.
source.notes: This ticket focuses specifically on the prefetch-induced amplification vector observed in the incident. Separate follow-ups will address unrelated admission fairness tuning and long-term autoscaler policy redesign.

source.title: Warp-tiler slab-wrap and prefetch index-fence leading to KV overwrite and subtle numeric skew
source.creator: Mateo Alvarez
source.description:
Summary: A concurrency bug in the warp-tiler prefetch path can cause an off-by-one slab wrap combined with an index-fence ordering violation. That results in KV slab slots being overwritten during concurrent prefill+decode windows. Symptoms include rare token duplication, subtle numerical drift in softmax accumulators under mixed-precision, and occasional transient degradation in prompt continuation quality.

Steps to reproduce (intermittent):
1) Start a dedicated workload with adaptive-kernel-routing enabled (--adaptive-kernel=true) on an 8-GPU pool and model variant 'redwood-open-16k-quant'.
2) Send a burst of interleaved prefill requests (batch_size=4, prefix lengths varying 512-1024) while simultaneously issuing low-latency decode continuations on other shards.
3) Observe error rate increase and rerun with debug KV instrumentation enabled (RUNTIME_KV_TRACE=1) to capture slab indices.

Observed:
- Rare collisions where slab index wraps to a previously active region (frequency ~1/10k requests under stress).
- Token duplication or out-of-order replay in ~0.2% of affected sessions.
- Numerical drift manifests as small 1e-4 shifts in softmax logits when mixed-precision accumulation (fp16+bf16 fast-path) is used; drift accumulates on long generations.

Suspected root cause:
- The warp-tiler prefetch scheduler uses a compacted index encoding and an index-fence check that relies on a per-warp epoch. Under certain scheduling interleavings, a prefetch completion races with an eviction path that advances the slab cursor; the cursor wraps without the fence observing the wrap, causing a slot to be treated as free when it is still referenced by an in-flight decode kernel.
- Mixed-precision accumulation exacerbates correctness because stale vs freshly written KV slices are combined by accumulation kernels optimized for throughput, producing rounding bias that diverges over many steps.

Impact:
- High for real-time chat and long-generation workloads (token duplication and subtle quality regression).
- Low syscall/crash risk, but compromises determinism and regression guarantees across model variants and hardware.

Immediate mitigations: 
- Add a conservative global fence for prefetch completion on the tile allocator to serialize slab-wrap events (hotfix branch: fix/warp-tiler-fence).
- Disable speculative mixed-precision accumulation for kernels touching the KV slab (flag: disable_kv_spec_accumulation=true) as a short-term guard.

Proposed long-term fix:
1) Rework the warp-tiler index encoding to include an explicit wrap counter and make slab-slot checks atomic with a compare-and-swap on the slot header.
2) Introduce per-request KV epoch stamping so decoders validate that referenced slots match epoch before reading.
3) Add a kernel-level regression test that fuzzes concurrent prefill/decode ordering under representative scheduling.

Next steps:
- (Sofia) finish microbench and stress harness to reproduce reliably by 2026-03-14.
- (Mateo) land hotfix fence and run canary on staging on 2026-03-13.
- Add unit/integration tests and schedule postmortem with reliability and kernel teams by 2026-03-18.
comments: 2026-03-10 Mateo Alvarez: Initial report from canary run in staging. Observed two sessions with duplicate tokens after a high-concurrency prefill burst. Attached KV trace to this ticket.

2026-03-11 Sofia Chen: Reproduced locally with stress harness (seed=7632). Frequency increased when adaptive_kernels=true and batch_coalescing=on. Beginning bisect to isolate allocator vs scheduler contribution.

2026-03-12 Sofia Chen: Identified slab cursor wrap window where allocator advances without writer fence. PoC branch created: fix/warp-tiler-fence. Hotfix passed unit tests but still running longer stress on staging.

2026-03-13 Mateo Alvarez: Deployed hotfix to staging (runtime-1.22-rc); found 10x reduction in collisions but one instance remained. Instrumentation logs show epoch mismatch on that run; suspect slow thread preemption interacting with kernel fallback path.
2026-03-14 Reliability Team (DM): Recommended disabling speculative accumulation on KV path until epoch-stamp fix is implemented. Created follow-up ticket ENG-101002 for epoch-stamping design.
2026-03-14 Sofia Chen: Scheduled pair design review with kernel team for 2026-03-16. Working on additional fuzz targets for CI integration.

Links: PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/6142, Playbook: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/RUNTIME/Warp-Tiler+Prefetch+Investigation, Canary dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/RUNTIME/canary-prefetch-2026

source.title: Adaptive batcher hang analysis and mitigations
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Summary: On 2026-03-09 at ~03:18 UTC we observed a multi-hour regression where the adaptive continuous batcher entered a state where new batches failed to form for particular traffic mixes, causing 95th/99th token latency to spike and sustained throughput degradation for affected tenants. This ticket captures the incident analysis, short-term mitigations we pushed, and the longer-term action plan to prevent recurrence.

What happened: High proportion of long-sequence, low-priority requests mixed with a burst of short, high-priority requests exposed a race condition in the batch formation state machine. The adaptive timer that delays batch finalization to improve throughput was repeatedly re-armed under a specific admission/eviction interplay. Combined with a KV-cache hotlock and worker recycling during a burst of autoscaler activity, many requests were repeatedly queued but never committed into batches for up to several seconds. Services experienced elevated tail latency and customer-facing timeouts.

Impact: For the tenant group routed through region-us-west-2: 95p token latency rose from baseline ~160ms to 4.2s, 99p to >9s for a 45-minute window. Inflight request dropouts grew ~3.8x during the peak. Overall cluster throughput for the affected model family fell ~18% while CPU and GPU utilization stayed high on some hosts due to stuck batch state. Several customers filed support tickets and some production routes hit automatic SLA fallbacks.

Scope: Incident was limited to clusters running the adaptive continuous batcher in runtime-1.20 with the adaptive-timer feature flag enabled. Not observed on dedicated reserved clusters that use the fixed-batcher path.

Evidence artifacts (links below) include Grafana dashboards, tracing spans showing repeated timer re-arm traces, and a heap of log fragments where batch-finalizer goroutines contended on the same atomic state.
timeline: 2026-03-09 03:10 UTC - small spike in long-sequence low-priority traffic begins
2026-03-09 03:18 UTC - first customer alert for elevated tail latency
2026-03-09 03:22 UTC - SRE notified, initial hypothesis: autoscaler churn
2026-03-09 03:35 UTC - trace samples show re-armed batch timers and no batch finalization for specific queue ids
2026-03-09 03:50 UTC - temporary mitigation (force-flush timer) rolled to two low-traffic clusters, latency improves locally
2026-03-09 04:30 UTC - mitigation validated and rolled cluster-wide for runtime-1.20 with feature-guard
2026-03-09 06:00 UTC - traffic normalizes; incident declared resolved for most tenants; post-incident investigations started
findings: Race in batch-formation state: the adaptive timer could be re-armed after a cancel path under specific interleavings, leaving no path to finalize the batch until an external flush happened
Admission controller and autoscaler interaction: worker eviction during high queue depth caused requests to be requeued without resetting timer state consistently
Fairness policy degraded latency: the throughput-first heuristic allowed long sequences to dominate slot reservation, starving short requests and increasing tail latency
Insufficient observability: we lacked a histogram metric for 'time-to-first-batch-finalize' and per-priority batch formation heatmaps, delaying diagnosis
Mitigation surface area: a force-flush endpoint existed but was not wired into alerting or automated rollback mechanics
mitigations_deployed: Hotfix v1: added a max-wait cap for adaptive batch timers (25ms default) and disabled auto-rearm on cancel paths - rolled to runtime-1.20 on 2026-03-09
Hotfix v2: emergency operator runbook step added to trigger force-flush on suspected hang (docs/runbooks/force-flush.md) - executed twice during incident
Routing fallback: for affected customers we temporarily shifted traffic to fixed-batcher nodes to restore tail latency SLAs
Observability: added temporary Grafana panel with batch-formation-time and per-queue stuck-counts
action_items: ENG-19841: Implement atomic batch state machine with explicit 'finalize' vs 'cancel' states to prevent rearm races - owner: Ravi Patel - due: 2026-03-21 - status: in progress
ENG-19842: Add per-priority fair-queueing (deficit round robin) to prevent starvation from long sequences - owner: Priya Rao - due: 2026-04-04 - status: planned
ENG-19843: Instrument and export 'time_to_batch_finalize' histogram and per-priority batch success rate to Prometheus - owner: Marcus Li - due: 2026-03-28 - status: in progress
ENG-19844: Add chaos test that simulates worker eviction + skewed traffic mix to CI perf/regression suite - owner: Sophie Martinez (SRE) - due: 2026-04-11 - status: planned
ENG-19845: Add automated alert that fires when >10 queues have 'stuck_count' > 0 for >30s and trigger self-healing flush - owner: Maya Thompson - due: 2026-03-30 - status: in progress

ENG-19846: Post-incident review to update runbooks, include checklist to escalate to platform on-call and perform immediate routing fallback - owner: Ravi Patel - due: 2026-03-18 - status: in progress
followups: Design long-term admission controller rewrite to separate token accounting from batch formation and support per-tenant backpressure profiles (quarterly roadmap item)
Evaluate making max-wait cap adaptive per model class (short-chat vs long-completion) and expose as tunable in Dedicated configs
Investigate kernel-level optimizations so that small batches are cheaper and we can bias towards latency without large throughput regressions
Run a customer-impact review to determine if any SLA credits are appropriate for affected tenants and coordinate with support
notes: SRE feedback: keep force-flush gated behind alerting; avoid operator misuse by adding confirmation and rate-limiters
PM input: expose a console toggle for 'latency-first' vs 'throughput-first' batching strategy for Dedicated customers
Design comment: prefer composable fairness primitives to monolithic heuristics to make reasoning about trade-offs easier
QA: add regression tests that assert no batch remains unfinalized for >200ms under mixed workload emulation
links: https://confluence.redwood.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=987654321 - incident writeup and raw trace artifacts
docs/runbooks/force-flush.md - operator runbook for batcher force flush

This ticket captures the incident analysis, short-term mitigations we pushed, and the longer-term action plan to prevent recurrence.

What happened: High proportion of long-sequence, low-priority requests mixed with a burst of short, high-priority requests exposed a race condition in the batch formation state machine. The adaptive timer that delays batch finalization to improve throughput was repeatedly re-armed under a specific admission/eviction interplay. Combined with a KV-cache hotlock and worker recycling during a burst of autoscaler activity, many requests were repeatedly queued but never committed into batches for up to several seconds. Services experienced elevated tail latency and customer-facing timeouts.

Impact: For the tenant group routed through region-us-west-2: 95p token latency rose from baseline ~160ms to 4.2s, 99p to >9s for a 45-minute window. Inflight request dropouts grew ~3.8x during the peak. Overall cluster throughput for the affected model family fell ~18% while CPU and GPU utilization stayed high on some hosts due to stuck batch state. Several customers filed support tickets and some production routes hit automatic SLA fallbacks.

Scope: Incident was limited to clusters running the adaptive continuous batcher in runtime-1.20 with the adaptive-timer feature flag enabled. Not observed on dedicated reserved clusters that use the fixed-batcher path.

Evidence artifacts (links below) include Grafana dashboards, tracing spans showing repeated timer re-arm traces, and a heap of log fragments where batch-finalizer goroutines contended on the same atomic state.

source.title: Sequenced Release Safety Thermostat and Automated Rollback Orchestra
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design and implement a 'thermostat' control loop for sequenced progressive delivery that dynamically adjusts percentage ramps, enforces multi-signal gates (service SLOs, error budget burn, anomaly score, infra utilization), and coordinates an automated rollback orchestra which executes safe, observable reversion steps across model variants, feature flags, and routing layers. This ticket covers design, simulation, a staging run, and an initial production pilot for Redwood's hosted and dedicated deployments.
acceptance_criteria: Thermostat controller deployed to staging and configurable via configmap/console
Controller uses at least three signals: p95 latency, error-rate (5xx/resp-level), and anomaly-score from evals-dashboard
Automatic ramp adjustments (increase/hold/decelerate/reverse) operate without human intervention during pilot windows
Rollback orchestra can revert model routing, flip feature flags, and fall back to golden runtime with no customer-facing errors > 0.5% for 30 minutes
Audit trail recorded for each decision and surfaced in Console (rollout timeline + rationale)
End-to-end sim harness reproduces 4 common failure modes and triggers intended responses
implementation_steps: Design thermostat policy DSL (velocity coeff, max_step_pct, hold_window_sec, cool_down_sec)
Instrument telemetry adapter to ingest p95/p99 latencies, 5xx rates, resource pressure (GPU mem/CPI), and anomaly-score from evals service
Implement decision engine with hysteresis and stochastic dampening to avoid oscillation
Integrate with feature-flag system to atomically change release cohorts
Build rollback orchestra sequence templates (model-variant-revert, flag-toggle, routing-fallback, circuit-breaker-enable)
Create staging simulation suite and run fault-injection scenarios
Add Console UI to visualize thermostat decisions and allow manual override
Run a controlled pilot (10% -> 25% -> 50%) for one non-critical model and validate metrics
risk_mitigation: Start pilots on low-traffic models and non-critical tenants
Shadow traffic runs for 48h before enabling active traffic ramps
Require human approval to promote to LTS channel regardless of thermostat status
Rate-limit maximum auto-increase per hour and cap to 50% unless explicitly waived
Maintain a pre-warmed golden fallback pool to avoid cold-start regressions during rollback
metrics_and_slos: Primary SLOs observed: p95 latency (target < 120ms), error-rate (target < 0.5%)
Auto-rollback triggers: sustained error-rate > 1.0% for 3 minutes OR p95 > 300ms for 5 minutes OR anomaly-score > 0.85
Throttle rules: max_increase_step = 10% of traffic per 10 minutes, deceleration_factor = 0.5 when infra_pressure > 0.7
Pilot success criteria: stable or improving SLOs across two consecutive 24h windows at 50% traffic
testing_plan: Unit tests for DSL parsing and decision rules
Integration tests for telemetry ingestion with synthetic signals
Chaos tests: introduce latency spike, partial GPU OOM, and model perf regressions in staging
End-to-end rehearsals: execute rollback orchestra from Console and measure customer-facing error delta
Canary-to-production dry runs using shadow traffic for 72h
comments: 2026-03-02 - Ava Chen: Initial design draft posted to Confluence: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/REL/thermostat-design (see 'control-loop-v1')
2026-03-04 - Ravi Patel: Spike complete. Implemented basic DSL and simulated PID-like controller in a Jupyter runbook: https://drive.redwood.ai/drive/spikes/thermostat-sim.ipynb
2026-03-06 - Morgan Lee (SRE): Concern about oscillation when two orthogonal signals cross thresholds; suggested adding stochastic damping and minimum hold window
2026-03-08 - Design Review: Approved UI wireframes for Console timeline. PR for UI components opened: https://github.com/redwood-inference/console/pull/1482

2026-03-10 - QA: Simulation results show controlled deceleration on synthetic infra pressure; however, rollback path had a 30s window where traffic switched but instances were not drained — added 'drain timeout' step to orchestra
2026-03-12 - Security: Confirmed no extra privileges are needed beyond existing rollout-role for the controller. Audit logging requirement noted.
2026-03-13 - Product (Lina Morales): Request to ensure LTS promotions are explicit and not automated; thermostat may suggest promotion but must not auto-publish to LTS channel
2026-03-14 - Ravi Patel: Staging pilot scheduled for 2026-03-18. Checklist linked: https://docs.redwood.ai/runbooks/thermostat-pilot
notes_and_decisions: Decision: Thermostat will be advisory by default for first two pilots; auto-enact for deceleration/hold decisions only (no automatic 0->100 promotions)
Decision: Multi-signal gating requires both service and infra signals to pass OR a human override; anomaly-score has veto power
Trade-off: Tighter thresholds reduce blast radius but increase manual work; we favor conservative defaults for public tenants
Follow-up: Build a lightweight simulation dashboard to tune velocity_coeff and deceleration_factor per model family

source.title: KV cache placement and compaction heuristics for sustained throughput
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Problem: As sustained continuous-batching workloads run on long-lived sessions (chat, multi-turn generation), KV cache entries show two failure modes: (1) small-variable sized segments cause internal fragmentation on GPU allocator leading to increased OOM risk under peak load; (2) uneven placement of KV slabs across GPUs increases cross-GPU memory pressure and reduces available headroom for batching spikes. Goal: Design and implement a lightweight placement + compaction heuristic that minimizes fragmentation and keeps headroom for high-concurrency bursts without adding significant CPU overhead or serialization pauses. Requirements: compatible with existing KV slab allocator and defrag tooling, work with dynamic sequence length distributions, preserve KV cache correctness across model warm-starts and rollouts, and support online metrics for fragmentation and compaction effectiveness.
source.acceptance_criteria: 1) New placement heuristic reduces measurable internal fragmentation metric by >= 30% on benchmark traces compared to baseline slab allocator; 2) Compaction runs do not increase p95 latency by more than 5% under median load; 3) No data loss observed across simulated canary rollouts and model swaps; 4) Telemetry added (fragmentation ratio, compaction count, reclaimed_bytes, compaction_latency) and surfaced in Console dashboard; 5) Automated e2e test that injects adversarial small-chunk KV patterns and validates memory stability for 30 minutes.
updates: 2026-03-01 - Ticket created by Ava Chen. Initial problem statement and goals recorded. Linked to ENG-4729 and ENG-8831 for allocator context.

2026-03-03 - Spike completed: profiled KV allocation patterns on three customer traces (chatbot, summarization, and streaming QA). Observed ~42% of allocations are < 1KB which dominate fragment churn. Documented findings in internal doc: drive.google.com/drive/fake-link-kv-spike.

2026-03-06 - Design review with runtime and infra: proposed hybrid placement that groups small KV entries into shared micro-slabs and pins micro-slab ranges to GPUs based on load heatmaps. Decided to prototype with a lock-free micro-slab header to avoid contention.

2026-03-08 - Prototype implemented: micro-slab allocator + background compaction thread that runs adaptive policies (idle-based and pressure-based). Prototype reduces fragmentation by 28% on lab traces but increases compaction CPU by ~4%.

2026-03-10 - Performance safety meeting: agreed to limit compaction concurrent work to 1 worker per GPU and backpressure compaction if p95 latency breach detected. PR opened: github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/2234.

2026-03-12 - Integration tests: verified correctness across model rollouts with pinned KV versions; added canary job that simulates model swap during compaction (no observable data corruption). Test run logs uploaded: s3://redwood-artifacts/eng/kv-9150/test-run-20260312.log.
2026-03-14 - Current: tuning placement thresholds and adding Console telemetry panels. Preparing for QA sign-off and a limited canary on dedicated staging cluster next week.
Follow-ups: evaluate long-term benefit of integrating with GPU driver allocator hooks; consider an optional aggressive compaction mode for offline batch inference windows.

source.title: Kernel pulse signature and span anchoring for prefill/decode telemetry
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: We lack a low-noise, high-fidelity signal that ties per-kernel GPU execution to high-level request phases (prefill vs decode) and scheduler decisions. Existing NVTX ranges and execid spans surface latency spikes, but we cannot reliably detect microburst kernel-path divergence caused by attention-path heuristics or KV-cache reuse without heavy sampling.

Proposal: Introduce a lightweight 'kernel pulse' marker emitted by the runtime around each kernel submission that contains a compact signature (string tags) describing the execution context. Anchor those pulses to trace spans so that backends (Tempo/Honeycomb) can reconstruct the full per-request kernel timeline without requiring full-trace capture every time. Pulse fields: kernel_name, kernel_variant, seq_len_bucket, kv_cache_hit, quant_path, stream_id, scheduler_decision, exec_id_short, bytes_read, bytes_written, timestamp_us. Emit as NVTX push/pop or a minimal perf event depending on build.

Goal: Give SRE, runtime, and profiling engineers the ability to: 1) attribute 95th/99th decode latency regressions to specific kernel-path signatures; 2) detect attention-pathway divergence cohorts; 3) drive scheduler and kernel-selection micro-optimizations with grounded telemetry.

Non-goals: This ticket does not change kernel selection logic itself or add new kernel implementations. It focuses only on telemetry footprint, ingestion, and dashboards needed to inform future changes.
decisions: Use compact ASCII signature to avoid binary payloads in NVTX (reduce parsing complexity)
Emit pulses at submission time; attach exec_id_short (8 chars) for cross-correlation with existing GPU exec traces
Two-mode capture: continuous low-overhead pulses (dev/prod) + burst full-span capture triggered on SLO breach or manual toggle
Sampling policy: default 0.5% continuous, 1% for dedicated clusters, and 100% in nightlies/dev for debugging
Backwards compatibility: make instrumentation opt-in via REDWOOD_KERNEL_PULSE env var and runtime flag
Privacy: never include request payload or prompt text in pulses (only structural metadata)
tasks: Runtime: add kernel_pulse emit hook in submit path (C++), populate compact signature fields
NVTX integration: create human-readable envelope and a short binary tag option for performance builds
Tracing: attach pulse markers to top-level request span (trace_id and span_id) in the trace manager
Ingestion: add parser to the telemetry pipeline to expand signature into indexed fields (backend change)
Dashboards: new per-kernel-signature dashboard showing p50/p95/p99 latency, token throughput, and kv_cache_hit rate
Sampling infra: add a low-overhead sampler + burst capture trigger (SLO/trace-based) with toggles in Console
Storage: define schema for hourly aggregation and a retention policy (30d raw, 365d aggregated cohorts)
E2E test: synthetic harness that reproduces attention-path divergence and validates pulses are correlated with kernel execids
Bench: measure overhead (latency + CPU + telemetry volume) and ensure <1% tail latency increase under 99th percentile load
Docs: update runtime observability docs with pulse schema, toggles, and querying examples
acceptance_criteria: Pulses are emitted for >99% of GPU kernel submissions in dev with REDWOOD_KERNEL_PULSE=1
Parser in telemetry stack decodes pulse signatures into indexed fields and populates dashboard within 5 mins of ingestion
Dashboards show kernel-signature cohorts with p50/p95/p99 breakdowns and kv_cache_hit correlations
Sampling/burst mode can be enabled from Console and triggers full span capture on SLO breach
End-to-end overhead measured <=1% added tail latency at p99 in controlled perf run
PR merged and feature gated; rollout plan documented with canary and rollback steps
comments: 2026-03-01 Ava Chen: Drafted initial proposal after postmortem on 2026-02-24 decode spike. Included initial signature schema and goals.

2026-03-03 Diego Morales: Reviewed runtime hook placement. Concern: ensure NVTX string length remains <256 bytes to avoid driver truncation. Action: use short keys and enumerated buckets for seq_len and quant_path.
2026-03-05 Priya Rao (SRE): Suggested sampling budget — keep continuous sampling <=0.5% on hosted to limit telemetry egress costs. Added burst trigger on SLO and manual console toggle.

2026-03-07 Marco Silva (Telemetry): Will add parser in pipeline; recommended emitting a secondary compact binary tag for high-throughput clusters. Risk: increases parsing work — we will gate binary tag behind runtime flag.
2026-03-09 Security review note (Elena Park): Confirmed no PII or prompt content will be included. Recommend adding an allowlist for customer-specific opt-outs for Private deployments.

2026-03-11 Diego Morales: First dev implementation landed in branch feat/kernel-pulse (PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/4123). Initial unit tests added. Observed ~0.3% CPU overhead in dev harness.

2026-03-13 QA (Samir Gupta): E2E harness run reproduces attention-path divergence; pulses correlate with execids in majority of traces. Follow-up: increase continuous sample rate in dedicated clusters to 1% for better coverage.
2026-03-14 Ava Chen: Merged pipeline parser PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/telemetry/pull/2191. Dashboards prototype: https://grafana.redwood.company/d/abcd/kernel-pulse-signatures

source.title: Throughput certification runbook for Dedicated capacity (benchmark harness & acceptance tests)
source.creator: Priya Natarajan
source.description: Create an operational runbook and test artifacts to certify Dedicated capacity SLAs and throughput guarantees. The goal is a repeatable, customer-facing acceptance test suite and an internal benchmark harness that measures sustained and burst throughput, autoscaling behavior, and graceful degradation across model variants and instance types. This ticket covers the benchmarking methodology, harness implementation notes, accepted measurement tools, and concrete acceptance criteria for customer handoff.
scope: Define traffic shapes: sustained QPS, burst profiles, spike-and-steady, tail-latency floods
Specify measurement windows and warm/cold KV-cache states
Implement harness extensions for multi-client synthetic load (sequenced fanout across regional nodes)
Benchmark permutations: model-size, quantization (q8/q4), sequence length distributions, batcher configs
Autoscaling policy verification: scale-up latency, scale-down hysteresis, and headroom validation
Acceptance test scripts and checklists for customer handoff and SRE runbooks
measurement_tools: prometheus + grafana exporters for token/sec and p95/p99 latency
test_plans: Sustained throughput test: run 60m at target QPS with KV cache warm; measure p50/p95/p99 and token/sec stability
Burst recovery test: inject 3x QPS for 30s then return to baseline; measure error rate and failover behavior
Autoscale ramp test: start at baseline, increase load until autoscaler adds nodes; verify observed capacity >= configured reserve
Model-variant fallback: saturate primary model then validate automatic fallback to smaller variant within SLO
Cold start & KV-warm scenarios: compare throughput with and without prefix cache pre-seeded
acceptance_criteria: For target Dedicated tier (e.g., d-capacity-4), 95% of requests must meet latency SLO (configured per-model) during 60m sustained test
No more than 0.1% 5xx error rate during steady-state with 3x sustained load ramped for burst test
Autoscaler must add capacity within configured scale-up window in >= 90% of runs and remove excess within configured hysteresis without oscillation
Fallbacks must not increase customer-visible 5xx rate above 0.5% for the duration of the test
Benchmark artifacts (raw traces, Prometheus exports, summary CSV) uploaded to the test run folder and linked in ticket
runbook_steps: Provision a dedicated test fleet using infra/test-dedicated-config
Start exporters and perf collectors (node/VM metrics, GPU metrics, per-request traces)
Execute baseline harness with single-client driver to verify environment health
Run permutation matrix (model x quant x batcher profile x seq-length) and store results
Run autoscale scenarios and capture timeline of replica changes and request latencies
Validate post-run artifacts and produce summary report with pass/fail per acceptance criteria
risks_and_tradeoffs: Measuring sustained throughput on multi-tenant infra introduces noisy neighbors; dedicated staging fleet required for reproducible baselines
Aggressive batching wins throughput but can harm tail latency; test matrix must surface tradeoffs for each workload profile
Autoscaler instrumentation currently coarse-grained; adding finer hooks increases risk surface but yields better observability
Full matrix (model sizes x quantization x hardware types) is combinatorial; initial scope prioritizes customer-used permutations
notes: Decided to extend redwood-load rather than adopt a fully external toolchain to keep token-level tracing and kernel-mapping intact - @Marcel reviewed and approved approach
We will publish a condensed customer acceptance checklist (one page) that maps to the full runbook
Plan to run the first certification set against customer-sim fleet on 2026-03-20 (internal dry-run scheduled)
Follow-up ticket will capture automation to run acceptance tests as part of Dedicated onboarding pipeline
comments: 2026-03-01 Priya Natarajan: Initial scope drafted. Will pair with SRE to identify staging fleet. Linking baseline perf paper.
2026-03-04 Ethan Morales: Prototype harness changes pushed to branch perf/eng-9853. Needs integration tests and CI gating.
2026-03-07 Marcel Liu: Reviewed methodology. Request to add KV-cache warm vs cold permutations and to include tokenization variance measurements.
2026-03-10 SRE Team: Provisioned dedicated staging-nodes-5 (GPUs: A10G). Please coordinate scheduling; added to infra calendar.
2026-03-12 QA: Smoke tests passed on sustained 30m run at 50% target QPS; capturing artifacts to S3. Scheduling full 60m run next week.
2026-03-14 Ethan Morales: Updated acceptance criteria after drying runs; bumped estimate to include autoscaler instrumentation work. Planning customer acceptance dry-run on 2026-03-20.
next_steps: Complete automation of result ingestion into perf dashboard (ETL job)
Run full matrix for critical customers and tag any performance regressions
Prepare customer-facing acceptance checklist and walkthrough playbook
Create follow-up task to integrate acceptance run into onboarding flow (ENG-9901)

source.title: Canonicalize model variant identifiers and publish performance profile guides
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Problem: Newly ingested models and third-party variants arrive with inconsistent ID formats (vendor-flavor, model-vX, quantized tags, internal hash). This causes: confusion in pinning workflows, duplicate entries in the catalog, and errors when publishing performance profiles to the console. Goal: define a canonical variant identifier scheme, implement a lightweight normalizer in the onboarding pipeline, and update docs so engineers and integrators can reliably pin specific variants and read precise performance profiles. Scope: - canonical ID spec (human-readable + stable fingerprint) - onboarding normalizer + lint step - profile publish workflow that records provenance + runtime signature - docs and console help text updates - migration strategy for existing catalog entries
acceptance_criteria: A canonical identifier spec is documented and approved by infra and applied-ml owners
Onboarding pipeline normalizer runs as a pre-commit lint and converts common incoming patterns into canonical IDs
Published performance profiles reference canonical ID and include a reproducible fingerprint and hardware signature
Console catalog pages show canonical ID, upstream name, pinned variants list, and link to detailed perf guide
Backfill plan for existing ambiguous entries is defined and non-destructive (read-only until approval)
notes: Design decision: canonical ID = <vendor>_<model>_v<semver>_q<quant>-<shorthash>. e.g. meta_llama3_v1.1_q4-3f2a
We will keep upstream_name and source_manifest fields for traceability and do not intend to overwrite upstream metadata
Performance profiles will include: seq_length_distribution, latency_p50/p90/p99, throughput_tokens/s, quantization flags, hardware family, and kernel hints
Normalization will be implemented as a standalone Golang binary run by the ingestion lambda; unit tests cover 95% of known variant patterns
Migration approach: create mapping table and expose preview CLI that shows proposed canonical IDs; require two approvals before write-back
comments: 2026-03-11 - Diego Martinez: Implemented the first pass of the normalizer and added unit tests for vendor prefixes. Found ambiguous tags from a community model repo; created ENG-16235 to track exceptional mapping rules.
2026-03-12 - Priya Nair (Applied ML): Reviewed canonical ID spec. Requested that the shorthash be derived from model file manifest + tokenizer checksum to avoid drift.
2026-03-13 - Diego Martinez: Updated normalizer to use manifest+tokenizer checksum. Pushed branch feature/eng-16234-normalizer. Opening PR to infra pipeline for pre-commit hook integration.

2026-03-13 - PM review (Liam O'Connor): Please add a line in docs reinforcing that pinning to a canonical ID will always resolve to the pinned fingerprint even if upstream naming changes. This is critical for customer contracts.
2026-03-13 - QA (Marina Lopez): Added test plan items covering console rendering of canonical IDs and mismatched upstream names. Noted one edge case for models with multiple tokenizer files.
next_steps: Finalize canonical ID spec and capture in Confluence (owner: Ava) - target 2026-03-14
Merge normalizer PR and add to ingestion CI pipeline (owner: Diego) - target 2026-03-16
Update perf publish pipeline to emit canonical_id + fingerprint (owner: Priya) - target 2026-03-18
Draft console copy and wireframe for catalog pages (owner: Design) - target 2026-03-17
Run backfill preview across staging catalog and collect false positive rate (owner: Data Ops) - target 2026-03-19

source.title: Call Interoperability Liaison Bridge: reconcile OpenAI-style tools with legacy function surface
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Goal: Implement a lightweight, production-safe compatibility layer (Call Interoperability Liaison, CIL) that lets Redwood route and execute both OpenAI-style "tools" and our legacy "function" call surface with minimal client changes. The CIL will: 1) normalize names and parameter shapes, 2) preserve invocation provenance and deterministic fallback behavior, 3) support streaming responses and partial tool/function returns, and 4) emit telemetry to detect regressions during migration.

Background: We have multiple customers that rely on older function-style integrations while a growing number adopt the OpenAI 'tool' contract. Current runtime has ad-hoc mappings which cause name collisions, lost params, and inconsistent streaming semantics. This ticket scopes the runtime bridge, a stable compatibility header, a per-customer alias registry, and a rollout plan.

Non-goals: This ticket does not redesign tool schema semantics or add new tool-safety policies. It also does not change billing semantics for tokens.

Design summary:
- Name resolution: canonical_name = sandboxed lookup(caller_namespace, provided_name) with deterministic tie-breaks for collisions (priority: explicit alias > customer namespace > global catalog).
- Parameter mapping: configurable marshallers that convert legacy flat args to typed tool params and vice-versa; simple heuristics for common types (string->text, object->json, arrays).
- Streaming: preserve chunk boundaries and stream-invocation events. For function-style calls that previously returned a complete payload, provide an opt-in streaming wrapper that emits partial results and a final sentinel event.
- Fallbacks: automatic fallback policy that can route to a compatible variant (smaller model, cached response, or legacy stub) with clear provenance in telemetry.
- Telemetry: add invocation tags (orig_surface, canonical_name, alias_id, resolution_path) and histograms for resolution latency, mapping errors, and fallback rate.
- Aliases registry: small datastore (KV) with write-protected backfill endpoints; admin UI hooks for Console (separate PR).

Acceptance criteria:
- Runtime resolves names for a mixed suite of test cases with >95% deterministic success (see e2e tests).
- Streaming semantics match contract: clients receive partial frames and final sentinel in both tool and legacy function paths.
- No regression in p99 latency beyond 5% for typical short calls (under 200ms baseline).
- Telemetry flows into console dashboards and alerts if fallback rate > 1% for a customer.
- Migration guide (docs) and a small canary rollout plan documented in the PRD.

Artifacts/links: design doc: https://confluence.redwood.ai/t/tool-interop-liaison-cil, GH draft PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/8221, Console story placeholder: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/PM-732, smoke test dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/interop/overview
comments: 2026-03-10 - Ava Chen: Created ticket and sketched initial design in the design doc. Pushed draft marshaller interface to the runtime repo branch 'cil/marshaller-shim'.

2026-03-11 - Sofia Martinez: Implemented name resolution prototype and ran it against 1200 historical invocations. Found 28 collisions that require explicit aliasing. Added a conflict-resolution heuristic and updated design doc.

2026-03-12 - Marcus Lee: Reviewed prototype; flagged streaming sentinel format mismatch with client SDKs. Suggested adding a compatibility header 'X-Redwood-Invocation-Surface' and a version field. Also requested telemetry tags for alias_id.

2026-03-13 - Sofia Martinez: Updated marshaller to emit alias_id and added unit tests for parameter coercion. Opened PR #8221 for initial runtime changes and included smoke test instructions. Next: add KV-backed alias registry and create canary rollout ticket in PM board.

title: Call Interoperability Liaison Bridge: reconcile OpenAI-style tools with legacy function surface
preview:
Goal: Implement a lightweight, production-safe compatibility layer (Call Interoperability Liaison, CIL) that lets Redwood route and execute both OpenAI-style "tools" and our legacy "function" call surface with minimal client changes. The CIL will: 1) normalize names and parameter shapes, 2) preserve invocation provenance and deterministic fallback behavior, 3) support streaming responses and partial tool/function returns, and 4) emit telemetry to detect regressions during migration.

Background: We have multiple customers that rely on older function-style integrations while a growing number adopt the OpenAI 'tool' contract. Current runtime has ad-hoc mappings which cause name collisions, lost params, and inconsistent streaming semantics. This ticket scopes the runtime bridge, a stable compatibility header, a per-customer alias registry, and a rollout plan.

Non-goals: This ticket does not redesign tool schema semantics or add new tool-safety policies. It also does not change billing semantics for tokens.

Design summary:
- Name resolution: canonical_name = sandboxed lookup(caller_namespace, provided_name) with deterministic tie-breaks for collisions (priority: explicit alias > customer namespace > global catalog).
- Parameter mapping: configurable marshallers that convert legacy flat args to typed tool params and vice-versa; simple heuristics for common types (string->text, object->json, arrays).
- Streaming: preserve chunk boundaries and stream-invocation events. For function-style calls that previously returned a complete payload, provide an opt-in streaming wrapper that emits partial results and a final sentinel event.
- Fallbacks: automatic fallback policy that can route to a compatible variant (smaller model, cached response, or legacy stub) with clear provenance in telemetry.
- Telemetry: add invocation tags (orig_surface, canonical_name, alias_id, resolution_path) and histograms for resolution latency, mapping errors, and fallback rate.
- Aliases registry: small datastore (KV) with write-protected backfill endpoints; admin UI hooks for Console (separate PR).

Acceptance criteria:
- Runtime resolves names for a mixed suite of test cases with >95% deterministic success (see e2e tests).
- Streaming semantics match contract: clients receive partial frames and final sentinel in both tool and legacy function paths.
- No regression in p99 latency beyond 5% for typical short calls (under 200ms baseline).
- Telemetry flows into console dashboards and alerts if fallback rate > 1% for a customer.
- Migration guide (docs) and a small canary rollout plan documented in the PRD.

source.title: Adaptive onboarding checklist and pinning policy for experimental quantized models
source.creator: Priya Sharma
source.description: Establish an adaptive metadata schema, onboarding checklist, and a conservative pinning policy for newly-ingested experimental quantized variants (Q4/Q8/Q5) so that performance profiles are published reliably and runtime fallbacks behave predictably. This ticket covers schema additions, validation tooling, documentation updates for the catalog, and a short-term rollout policy that balances dev velocity with safety for production customers.
acceptance_criteria: New metadata fields (quantization_scheme, calibration_dataset, fallback_variant, kernel_profile_signature, pinned_digest) are added to model catalog schema and linted by ingest pipeline
A lightweight metadata validator runs in the onboarding flow and fails the ingest for missing required fields for experimental quantized variants
Performance profile for each onboarded quantized variant is published to the model catalog UI and API with a performance_signature_id that links to the benchmark job
Pinning policy implemented: experimental quantized variants default to 'soft-pin' (auto-fallback enabled) and require manual 'hard-pin' sign-off for Dedicated/Private release

End-to-end compatibility test added that loads the pinned digest against the runtime kernel matrix for the target GPU (A100/NVIDIA H100/AMD MI250) and passes baseline latency and correctness thresholds
Catalog docs and quickstart updated with an onboarding checklist and example PRD/PR templates
Migration script for previously ingested quantized models created and run in dry-mode with a report generated
progress_updates: 2026-03-10: Kickoff with PM and Runtime leads. Agreed need for explicit quantization metadata and a conservative default pin policy.
2026-03-11: Prototype metadata validator implemented and added to ingest pipeline; validator emits warnings for optional fields, errors for required fields when variant is marked experimental-quant.

2026-03-12: Design review feedback from Design (Alicia Chen): add UI hints in catalog to show 'experimental' badge and fallback behavior; PM asked for opt-in telemetry to measure real-world fallback rates.
2026-03-12: Trade-off discussion: conservative 'soft-pin' default vs developer friction. Decided soft-pin default with telemetry + manual hard-pin sign-off for Dedicated customers.
2026-03-13: Opened PR for schema changes and validator: https://github.com/redwood-inference/model-catalog/pull/821; runtime compatibility tests stubbed in runtime repo.
Next steps: finish runtime e2e test, update console docs, schedule canary onboarding run with two internal experimental quantized variants, and prepare release notes for catalog-2026.03
review_feedback: Alicia Chen (Design): Add an experimental badge and collapsible details on the catalog entry for quantized variants.
Samir Kulkarni (Runtime): Require kernel_profile_signature in metadata to map to optimized kernels; added request for auto-scan job to verify kernel availability.
Leah Thompson (Product): Telemetry must include fallback counts and token-cost deltas; prefer soft-pin default to avoid regression exposure for Hosted tier.
follow_up_tasks: Run canary onboarding with internal models and collect fallback telemetry (owner: Ethan Park)
Add migration dry-run for existing quantized entries and provide CSV of missing fields (owner: Priya Sharma)
Coordinate with Dedicated sales ops to document hard-pin process and approval workflow (owner: Samir Kulkarni)
Publish catalog docs and record a 30-minute demo for ENG/SEs (owner: Alicia Chen)

source.title: Dynamic slew-rate limiter with progressive KV compaction to mitigate queue pileup at sustained high QPS
source.creator: Maya Chen
source.description:
Background: During recent weekend load tests and two customer incidents we observed sustained high QPS (50k+ req/s across the cluster) producing prolonged microbatch queue growth, increased tail latency, and KV cache pressure that eventually triggers out-of-memory (OOM) evictions and degraded throughput. Existing admission heuristics (static rate caps + simple headroom checks) react too late or oscillate under nonstationary traffic patterns.

Goal: Implement a coordinated admission and memory-reclamation strategy that prevents queue pileups before OOMs occur while preserving throughput and KV hit rate. Specifically: introduce a 'slew-rate limiter' to throttle the entrance of new microbatches smoothly and a progressive KV compaction policy to reclaim low-value cache entries when memory headroom falls below safe thresholds. The two mechanisms should be tenant-aware and integrate with existing autoscaling and fallback routing policies.
source.problem_statement: Sustained high QPS leads to long-lived queue growth because admission control admits bursts faster than compaction and GPU reclaim can free memory. Result: GPU OOMs, heavy KV churn, increased 95/99/99.9 tail latencies, and tenant unfairness. We need a stable, low-oscillation controller that acts proactively based on short-term forecasts and multiple memory/latency signals.
source.proposed_solution:
1) Slew-rate limiter: implement a rate-limiter at the microbatch entrance that limits the increase in admitted microbatches per second (MB/s) using a configurable ‘dS/dt’ (slew-rate). The limiter uses an exponential moving average of admitted MB/s and only allows additive increases up to the slew-rate; decreases can be immediate when signals indicate danger.

2) Signal set for controller: GPU_free_memory_ratio, cluster_queue_length, per-tenant_kv_hit_rate, tail_latency_p95/p99, recent_gpu_oom_count, and predictive forecast (5s EWMA on incoming QPS).

3) Progressive KV compaction: when headroom < 25% start a low-cost compaction pass that probabilistically compacts buckets of low-recently-used values (using sampled LRU weights) and lowers per-tenant TTLs by a small factor. If headroom < 12% escalate compaction aggressiveness and enable ephemeral spill-to-host for rarely-hit KV entries. Compaction runs on CPU background threads with rate limits to avoid impacting serving threads.

4) Coordinated backpressure policy: combine slew-rate output and compaction state into an admission score. When compaction is running aggressively, admission accepts fewer MB/s; when compaction has recovered headroom, admission ramps up respecting slew-rate increments. Add hysteresis windows to prevent oscillations.

5) Tenant fairness: add per-tenant share buckets so a single tenant cannot starve others; allow emergency overrides for enterprise tenants under contract (configurable).
source.algorithm_details:
- Slew-rate defined per-shard and per-tenant: default 10% increase per 1s, configurable.
- EWMA alpha=0.3 for short-term admitted MB/s forecast.
- Admission decision: if (predicted_free_memory < memory_threshold) => apply immediate cap = current_admitted_MB/s * backoff_multiplier (0.6 default). Otherwise allow ramp by min(current_admitted + slew_rate, max_allowed_by_headroom).
- Compaction tiers: TIER1 (gentle): sample-based TTL decay and 5% eviction of cold slots per minute; TIER2 (aggressive): 20% eviction, host-spill enable; TIER3 (critical): disable nonessential caches and trigger per-model light-weight checkpoint/cache-write.
- Metrics emitted for each decision: allowed_MB/s, slew_rate_value, compaction_tier, estimated_reclaimed_bytes, per-tenant_kv_hit_rate_delta.
source.tradeoffs:
- Pros: proactive protection against OOMs, smoother ramp-ups, lower tail latency and more predictable service.
- Cons: increased CPU cost for compaction, potential temporary drop in KV hit rate (more host fetches), and slightly elevated median latency for affected requests when spill-to-host is used. Complexity in tuning slew-rate and compaction thresholds; initial tuning required per-cluster profile.
source.acceptance_criteria:
- Under synthetic sustained load (cluster-level QPS 3x baseline) the controller keeps GPU OOMs at 0 in canary region.
- 99th percentile latency improves or does not regress more than 5% compared to current emergency eviction behavior during the same load.
- KV local hit rate drop limited to <= 10% during compaction windows and recovers to within 3% of baseline within 60s after ramp-down.
- No customer-facing error spikes during canary rollout; feature flag enables rollback within 2 minutes.
source.rollout_plan:
1) Unit tests + microbench harness for admission logic (branch).
2) Simulation job (k8s job) replaying production QPS traces with multiple tenant mixes; tune defaults.
3) Canary rollout to one shard in us-west-2 with telemetry gates: device_memory_pressure, tail_latency_p99, kv_hit_rate.
4) Expand to 25% of shards if canary stable for 12 hours, then full rollout.
5) Postmortem window: 72 hours after full rollout for telemetry review and tuning.

Feature flagging: runtime flag per-cluster and per-tenant; emergency kill switch in Console.
source.testing_plan:
- Unit tests for EWMA and admission math.
- Integration tests with in-memory KV to validate compaction tiers.
- Load tests: replay of 7-day traffic trace scaled up to 3x QPS in staging.
- Chaos tests: introduce pod eviction / slower disk to emulate host-spill penalty.
- Regression suite to ensure no increase in median latency > 5% for normal loads.
source.notes:
Related design doc: https://confluence.redwood.example/display/SERVE/Slew+Rate+Limiter+and+KV+Compaction.
Proto PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving/pull/4239.
Perf dashboard (staging canary): https://datadog.com/dash/serving/slew-rate-canary-1000453.
Customer incident references: INC-2026-017 and internal postmortem: https://drive.redwood.example/pm/postmortems/2026-02-surge.pdf
comments: 2026-03-02 - Maya Chen: Created ticket after weekend load test. Initial simulation shows queue growth precedence over memory reclaim by ~4s.
2026-03-04 - Ethan Park: Prototype EWMA + slew-rate implemented in admission_controller.go. Running unit tests locally; found off-by-one in rate window, fixed. PR draft available.
2026-03-06 - Priya Raman (PM): Reviewed and requested explicit tenant fairness section and emergency enterprise override. Added to proposed_solution.

2026-03-08 - Charlie Nguyen (SRE): Ran staging replay; with default parameters compaction tier transitions reduced OOMs but increased host spill metrics. Suggested softer initial TTL decay and longer hysteresis (10s).

2026-03-10 - Ethan Park: Integrated hysteresis and per-shard cap. Simulation results: p99 latency down 14% vs previous emergency eviction; kv_hit_rate transient drop 7% during aggressive compaction windows. Uploading detailed logs to Datadog.
2026-03-12 - Security Review Bot: No direct data exfiltration risk identified; host spill touches unencrypted memory briefly — add KMS note to follow-up.

source.title: Workload Signal Assembler & Continual Batching Advisor
source.creator: Maya Chen
source.description: Build a recommendation engine that synthesizes workload signals (request size distribution, repeat prefixes, KV cache hit patterns, latency tail behavior) and outputs conservative continuous-batching parameters plus a safe apply/rollback plan. This ticket focuses on the signal assembler, heuristics for safe step sizes, and the shadow-apply telemetry needed for automatic rollback triggers.
acceptance_criteria: Signal assembler ingests request traces and produces normalized workload fingerprints (slot histograms, prefix reuse rate, KV-hit proxy) for a given route.
Advisor outputs deterministic parameter proposals: target microbatch size, coalescing window ms, prefix-cache TTL, and suggested conservative step sizes per apply stage.
Shadow-apply mode can run proposed config on 1% of traffic with no customer-visible routing changes and emits rollout metrics to the rollout dashboard.

Rollback triggers defined and tested: >15% P95 latency degradation, >10% token-cost increase, or >0.5% error-rate increase over baseline within 30m window should automatically flag and mark config for rollback.

End-to-end integration test that simulates bursty and low-QPS workloads validates that advisor chooses different strategies (small microbatch + short window for latency-sensitive routes; larger microbatch + longer window for throughput-heavy routes).
tasks: Define schema for normalized workload fingerprint (histogram buckets, KV proxy fields, prefix-entropy)
Implement extractor that reads traces from telemetry and produces a per-route fingerprint hourly
Prototype heuristics engine mapping fingerprints to parameter suggestions
Add shadow-apply plumbing and metric tagging in runtime for 1% traffic experiments
Create rollout dashboard panels and alert rules for rollback triggers
Run safety chaos tests with synthetic replay harness and document results
Write runbook for manual rollback and emergency revert procedure
comments: 2026-02-20 Maya Chen: Initial ticket created after spike meeting. Motivation: customers see cost variance across routes; need automated, safe recommendations.

2026-02-23 Ethan Park: Spiked on fingerprint schema. Proposed fields: size_percentiles (P10,P50,P90,P99), prefix_reuse_rate, avg_kv_hits_per_request, burst_index (5m/1h ratio). Putting draft at /docs/batching/fingerprints.md.
2026-02-26 Design review (Priya Rao): Recommend exposing 'latency_sensitivity' label from product to bias advisor away from aggressive batching on certain routes.

2026-03-02 Engineering sync: Decided to implement conservative step size schedule: 10% microbatch increase or 5ms coalescing window per stage, max 3 stages; rollback criteria must be met for continuous apply to stop.

2026-03-05 QA (Samir Gupta): Added synthetic replay harness that can replay production trace subsets with injected latency/error faults. Found one edge case: small QPS routes with high prefix reuse get misclassified; added a minimum-sample threshold.

2026-03-09 Infra (Lena Ortiz): Shadow-apply deployed to staging runtime. Metric tagging works but discovered telemetry cardinality spike when annotating trace-level KV fields; switched to hourly aggregated fingerprints to avoid high-cardinality metrics.

2026-03-12 Ethan Park: Began running 1% shadow experiments on internal routes. First results: for route /chat-stream, advisor suggested microbatch 3 -> 4 and coalescing window 5ms -> 8ms with estimated token-cost -7% and negligible latency change. Preparing canary for 5% traffic next week.
2026-03-12 Product (Aisha Khan): Requested adding a 'safety multiplier' per-customer to scale down recommended batch sizes for tiered SLAs. Adding as follow-up P2.
2026-03-12 Security (N/A): No PII in fingerprints; KV-proxy is aggregated. Security review flagged KMS usage when storing synthetic replay traces — follow-up to redact traces.

source.title: Central policy proxy and audit pipeline for RBAC v2
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Summary:
Build a central policy evaluation proxy and an immutable audit pipeline to support RBAC v2 across the API surface (hosted API), the serving runtime, and the Redwood Console. This ticket covers the core engine (fast, deterministic policy evaluation), the authorization middleware to call it from different runtimes, audit event schema and transport, SSO/SAML role-to-policy mapping hooks, and the admin controls for policy editing and rollout.

Goals:
- Implement a lightweight, horizontally scalable policy evaluation service (gRPC + HTTP sidecar) capable of evaluating token and context attributes within 1ms P50 for typical requests.
- Provide language-agnostic middleware libraries for the API gateway, runtime request pipeline, and console backend so all requests go through a consistent authorization decision path.
- Emit structured, tamper-evident audit events for every allow/deny decision and admin policy change; deliver to our central Kafka topic and an append-only object store for long-term retention.
- Integrate SAML/SSO role mappings into the evaluation path so identity assertions map cleanly to RBAC v2 roles and policy attributes.
- Admin UX hooks: policy preview, dry-run mode, canary split by percentage, policy diff and rollback controls in the Console.

Acceptance criteria:
1) Policy evaluation service running in staging, reachable via gRPC and HTTP, with a documented protobuf schema and API contract.
2) Middleware implemented for gateway, runtime, and console; end-to-end tests demonstrating deny/allow flows and attribute-based decisions.
3) Audit events emitted for every decision and admin change; events validated by schema; consumer (kafka topic) receives events with <100ms end-to-end latency under 500 req/s.
4) SSO/SAML mapping hooks deployed and exercised in a partner test tenant; mapping documented in Confluence.
5) Admin console features for canary/dry-run exist behind a feature flag; reviewers can promote policies to global in Console.
6) Security review completed and signed off; threat-model updated.

Implementation notes and trade-offs:
- We will implement the evaluator in Rust for performance and FFI-friendly integration. An HTTP gateway wrapper will provide backwards compatibility to services that can only speak REST.
- To avoid large coupling, middleware will be thin: it marshals request context, calls the evaluator, and enforces decision. No policy logic will live in callers.
- Audit events will include a signature (HMAC) and event sequence id to make tampering detectable; full immutability will be satisfied by writing to an object-store commit log plus streaming to Kafka for real-time consumers.
- KV cache of recent decisions will be provided at the middleware layer with a configurable TTL to reduce eval QPS for hot tokens, with cache-control headers to ensure freshness for admin-changed policies.

Docs & artifacts:
- Confluence: https://confluence.redwood.internal/display/SEC/RBAC-v2+Policy+Proxy
- Design spec (proto + schema): https://drive.redwood.internal/drive/folders/rbac-v2-protos
- Initial spike PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/1289
- Audit schema: https://gist.github.com/redwood-inference/audit-schema-rbac-v2

Rollout plan:
- Phase 1 (staging): deploy evaluator + gateway middleware for internal services and run tests (2 weeks).
- Phase 2 (canary): enable on 10% traffic for non-critical customers; enable dry-run logging for 100% (1 week).
- Phase 3 (gradual): increase traffic with health checks; enable Console admin controls and policy promotion (2 weeks).
- Phase 4 (global): turn on enforcement for all traffic after SRE SLA validation and security sign-off.

Risks & mitigations:
- Performance regression: add local decision-cache, benchmark harness, and optimize hot paths (Rust SIMD or JIT later).
- Incorrect SSO mappings: require test tenant validation and a mapping preview in Console.
- Audit log scale/cost: tier retention by customer and compress older events; provide sampling for high-volume non-security events.
comments: 2026-02-26 - Ava Chen: Kicked off design working group. Drafted protobuf and initial evaluator API. See design doc link in description.

2026-03-02 - Marco Rivera: Implemented evaluator skeleton in runtime repo. Basic gRPC endpoint up; benchmarking shows 1.8ms P50 on a c6i-like instance with no load. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/1289
2026-03-04 - Priya Singh (Security): Reviewed threat model. Requested HMAC signing for audit events and stricter policy-change audit fields. Marked security_review_required = true.

2026-03-06 - Marco Rivera: Added audit producer to Kafka; local end-to-end tests show <50ms from decision to kafka publish. Need to validate signature verification consumer. Open issue ENG-10037 for consumer verification.

2026-03-09 - Elena Park (SRE): Load test (5000 RPS) of sidecar + evaluator shows CPU bound at tail; investigating batching or horizontally autoscaling configs. Created sub-issue ENG-10045 for autoscaling policy tuning.
2026-03-11 - Design: Console mockups uploaded for policy dry-run and canary controls. Feedback: show policy diff and example affected principal counts before promote. Confluence: design page updated.

2026-03-12 - Marco Rivera: Updated updated_at and added rollout plan to description. Next steps: complete middleware builds for API gateway and runtime, finish security review, and schedule staging deploy with SRE.
