Acceptance criteria:
1) Policy evaluation service running in staging, reachable via gRPC and HTTP, with a documented protobuf schema and API contract.
2) Middleware implemented for gateway, runtime, and console; end-to-end tests demonstrating deny/allow flows and attribute-based decisions.
3) Audit events emitted for every decision and admin change; events validated by schema; consumer (kafka topic) receives events with <100ms end-to-end latency under 500 req/s.
4) SSO/SAML mapping hooks deployed and exercised in a partner test tenant; mapping documented in Confluence.
5) Admin console features for canary/dry-run exist behind a feature flag; reviewers can promote policies to global in Console.
6) Security review completed and signed off; threat-model updated.

Implementation notes and trade-offs:
- We will implement the evaluator in Rust for performance and FFI-friendly integration. An HTTP gateway wrapper will provide backwards compatibility to services that can only speak REST.
- To avoid large coupling, middleware will be thin: it marshals request context, calls the evaluator, and enforces decision. No policy logic will live in callers.
- Audit events will include a signature (HMAC) and event sequence id to make tampering detectable; full immutability will be satisfied by writing to an object-store commit log plus streaming to Kafka for real-time consumers.
- KV cache of recent decisions will be provided at the middleware layer with a configurable TTL to reduce eval QPS for hot tokens, with cache-control headers to ensure freshness for admin-changed policies.

Docs & artifacts:
- Confluence: https://confluence.redwood.internal/display/SEC/RBAC-v2+Policy+Proxy
- Design spec (proto + schema): https://drive.redwood.internal/drive/folders/rbac-v2-protos
- Initial spike PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/1289
- Audit schema: https://gist.github.com/redwood-inference/audit-schema-rbac-v2

Rollout plan:
- Phase 1 (staging): deploy evaluator + gateway middleware for internal services and run tests (2 weeks).
- Phase 2 (canary): enable on 10% traffic for non-critical customers; enable dry-run logging for 100% (1 week).
- Phase 3 (gradual): increase traffic with health checks; enable Console admin controls and policy promotion (2 weeks).
- Phase 4 (global): turn on enforcement for all traffic after SRE SLA validation and security sign-off.

Risks & mitigations:
- Performance regression: add local decision-cache, benchmark harness, and optimize hot paths (Rust SIMD or JIT later).
- Incorrect SSO mappings: require test tenant validation and a mapping preview in Console.
- Audit log scale/cost: tier retention by customer and compress older events; provide sampling for high-volume non-security events.
text:
Summary:
Build a central policy evaluation proxy and an immutable audit pipeline to support RBAC v2 across the API surface (hosted API), the serving runtime, and the Redwood Console. This ticket covers the core engine (fast, deterministic policy evaluation), the authorization middleware to call it from different runtimes, audit event schema and transport, SSO/SAML role-to-policy mapping hooks, and the admin controls for policy editing and rollout.

Goals:
- Implement a lightweight, horizontally scalable policy evaluation service (gRPC + HTTP sidecar) capable of evaluating token and context attributes within 1ms P50 for typical requests.
- Provide language-agnostic middleware libraries for the API gateway, runtime request pipeline, and console backend so all requests go through a consistent authorization decision path.
- Emit structured, tamper-evident audit events for every allow/deny decision and admin policy change; deliver to our central Kafka topic and an append-only object store for long-term retention.
- Integrate SAML/SSO role mappings into the evaluation path so identity assertions map cleanly to RBAC v2 roles and policy attributes.
- Admin UX hooks: policy preview, dry-run mode, canary split by percentage, policy diff and rollback controls in the Console.

Acceptance criteria:
1) Policy evaluation service running in staging, reachable via gRPC and HTTP, with a documented protobuf schema and API contract.
2) Middleware implemented for gateway, runtime, and console; end-to-end tests demonstrating deny/allow flows and attribute-based decisions.
3) Audit events emitted for every decision and admin change; events validated by schema; consumer (kafka topic) receives events with <100ms end-to-end latency under 500 req/s.
4) SSO/SAML mapping hooks deployed and exercised in a partner test tenant; mapping documented in Confluence.
5) Admin console features for canary/dry-run exist behind a feature flag; reviewers can promote policies to global in Console.
6) Security review completed and signed off; threat-model updated.

Implementation notes and trade-offs:
- We will implement the evaluator in Rust for performance and FFI-friendly integration. An HTTP gateway wrapper will provide backwards compatibility to services that can only speak REST.
- To avoid large coupling, middleware will be thin: it marshals request context, calls the evaluator, and enforces decision. No policy logic will live in callers.
- Audit events will include a signature (HMAC) and event sequence id to make tampering detectable; full immutability will be satisfied by writing to an object-store commit log plus streaming to Kafka for real-time consumers.
- KV cache of recent decisions will be provided at the middleware layer with a configurable TTL to reduce eval QPS for hot tokens, with cache-control headers to ensure freshness for admin-changed policies.

Docs & artifacts:
- Confluence: https://confluence.redwood.internal/display/SEC/RBAC-v2+Policy+Proxy
- Design spec (proto + schema): https://drive.redwood.internal/drive/folders/rbac-v2-protos
- Initial spike PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/1289
- Audit schema: https://gist.github.com/redwood-inference/audit-schema-rbac-v2

Rollout plan:
- Phase 1 (staging): deploy evaluator + gateway middleware for internal services and run tests (2 weeks).
- Phase 2 (canary): enable on 10% traffic for non-critical customers; enable dry-run logging for 100% (1 week).
- Phase 3 (gradual): increase traffic with health checks; enable Console admin controls and policy promotion (2 weeks).
- Phase 4 (global): turn on enforcement for all traffic after SRE SLA validation and security sign-off.

Risks & mitigations:
- Performance regression: add local decision-cache, benchmark harness, and optimize hot paths (Rust SIMD or JIT later).
- Incorrect SSO mappings: require test tenant validation and a mapping preview in Console.
- Audit log scale/cost: tier retention by customer and compress older events; provide sampling for high-volume non-security events.

source.title: Stream debug replay and cancellation-signal UX for Python SDK
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Problem: Developers building streaming chat integrations frequently hit hard-to-reproduce issues when streams are interrupted, events are reordered, or retries occur. Current patterns require application-level wiring for checkpointing, cancellation, and replay which leads to boilerplate and fragile integrations. Goal: Provide ergonomic, battery-included primitives in the Python SDK to (1) emit rich sideband telemetry and debug blobs for every streaming session, (2) expose a lightweight replay mode for local debugging and deterministic test harnesses, and (3) introduce a small cancellation/signal API usable from both sync and async code without forcing callback-heavy designs.
acceptance_criteria: New StreamSession context manager supports with / async with and yields a typed Event stream iterator
StreamSession automatically attaches a compact debug blob (events + timestamps + token watermarks) available via .debug_dump() and uploads to s3-compatible debug bucket behind a feature flag
SDK exposes StreamSession.replay(debug_blob) that can deterministically rehydrate the event iterator for local replay tests
Cancellation API: StreamSession.cancel(reason) and signal objects that integrate with asyncio.CancelledError and synchronous cancellation (thread-safe)
Minimal breaking changes: existing streaming helper helpers remain functional; new API is additive behind an opt-in kwarg 'session_mode=True'
End-to-end unit and integration tests cover interruption + retry + replay scenarios and preserve event ordering assertions
decisions: Use an opt-in StreamSession object instead of changing global stream primitives to minimize risk
Keep debug dump format as newline-delimited JSON (ndjson) with a small header for compatibility with log ingestion pipelines
Replay mode will be deterministic and single-pass; live streaming behavior (timeouts, network errors) is not simulated in replay
Telemetry: emit compact session-level metrics to existing SDK telemetry pipeline and include a link to debug blob when available
tasks: Design API sketch and public doc + examples (markdown in repo) - owner: Ravi
Implement StreamSession context manager with sync + async support
Add debug_dump() and local replay loader utility
Wire session-level telemetry (duration, final_status, bytes_in, events_count, debug_blob_url) behind feature flag
Add tests: unit for iterator behaviour, integration for end-to-end replay, fuzz test for reordering tolerance
Create migration notes and developer guide (how to enable session_mode)
comments: 2026-03-10 - Ava Chen: Filed. Sketching API that supports both 'with StreamSession(...) as s' and 'async with StreamSession(...) as s'. Prioritizing minimal friction for existing users.
2026-03-11 - Ravi Patel: Punted full network-simulator for replay — MVP will focus on deterministic event replay only. Added estimate 3pts.
2026-03-11 - Lucas Moreno: Recommended ndjson for debug dump and suggested adding a compact header with SDK + model version to aid parsing.
2026-03-12 - Priya Singh: QA automation note — ensure replay mode can run in CI with recorded debug blobs; otherwise flakes will proliferate.
2026-03-12 - Ravi Patel: Linking prototype PR and initial unit tests. Calling out that debug blob upload is behind a feature flag and controlled via env SDK_DEBUG_DUMPS=true.
notes: Backward compatibility: session_mode is opt-in. Existing examples continue to work.
Performance: debug_dump keeps a rolling buffer capped at 1MB by default to avoid unbounded memory growth; uploads are asynchronous and can be disabled.
Security: debug_dump redacts potential PII via existing SDK redaction hooks before persisting; customers can opt out of remote upload and only keep local files.
Rollout plan: feature flag gated for internal canary customers for 2 weeks, then beta program, then public release.
Follow-ups: consider adding time-travel testing harness that injects controlled network jitter for replay-enhanced CI runs.

source.title: kmshub: Terraform orchestrator for KMS/HSM secrets failover in VPC/private deploys
source.creator: Ravi Patel
source.description: Deliver a reusable Terraform module (kmshub) that orchestrates KMS/HSM integrations and secrets management fallbacks for private (VPC) Redwood deployments. The module should: 1) provision KMS connectors (cloud KMS or on-prem HSM proxy) and Secrets Engine bindings (Secrets Manager or Vault) in a deterministic, auditable way; 2) wire health checks and circuit-breaker policies so runtime can automatically failover to a safe secrets provider or cached keys if the primary KMS/HSM becomes unreachable; 3) include an automated upgrade path (minor/major) that preserves key material references and rotates provider bindings with minimal downtime; 4) produce drift-detection outputs and a remediation plan (tf plan-based alerting) for operators; 5) work in air-gapped and VPC-proxied environments, with optional sidecar proxy for hardware key brokers. This ticket scopes the module design, CI tests, and a minimal implementation ready for internal dogfooding.
acceptance_criteria: Terraform module 'kmshub' exists in infra-modules repo with documented inputs/outputs
Module deploys a KMS connector and secrets binding in a sample VPC workspace without manual changes
Healthcheck lambda/probe and circuit-breaker policy templates included; simulated primary KMS outage triggers fallback in our staging harness
Upgrade procedure documented in README (minor upgrades via terraform apply, major via canary plan and state migration steps)
E2E tests: terraform apply/destroy in a CI container (mock HSM + Vault) completes within CI timeout
Security review signoff attached and Terraform plan defaults forbid accidental wide-open network rules
tasks: Design module interface (inputs: primary_kms_provider, hsm_proxy_config, vault_role, secrets_engine, fallback_provider, healthcheck_config)
Implement resource templates for AWS KMS connector, Vault transit/kv binding, and HSM proxy placeholders
Implement healthcheck resources: cloudwatch/scheduler + small lambda that validates sign/sign-verify and KV cache TTL; expose status outputs
Implement circuit-breaker wiring: terraform-managed flag and sample runbook entry for runtime to consult
Add upgrade playbook and terraform state migration notes (versioned module strategy)
Author e2e CI scenario using localstack + mock-hsm container + dev-vault to exercise failover
Add unit tests for input validation and terratest integration tests
Prepare PR and request security and infra review
decisions: Use a single module that composes provider-specific submodules (aws-kms, gcp-kms, hsm-proxy) rather than separate top-level modules
Expose a 'fallback_provider' input that can be 'vault-local-cache' or 'cloud-secrets-manager' and wire recommended defaults per deployment type
Prefer an explicit terraform-managed 'secrets_state' resource to avoid implicit in-place key rewrites during upgrades
Healthchecks will be performed by a small portable lambda (or pod) implementing sign/verify + token fetch to validate both KMS and secrets engine paths
risks: Key-rotation complexity: ensuring canonical key references survive module upgrades without dangerous re-creates
Air-gapped environments require maintainable mock HSMs for CI which increases test maintenance
Terraform state drift in multi-tenant VPCs if operators hand-edit bindings outside the module
Fallback semantics must be deterministic to avoid double-encryption or silent key mismatch across nodes
comments: 2026-02-21 - Ravi Patel: Initial ticket created after ops sync. Spiked two approaches: provider-composite module vs. orchestration wrapper. Leaning composite for easier reuse.

2026-02-27 - Ava Chen: Drafted module interface and posted to infra-modules RFC. Main open question: single statefile per deployment or per-environment sub-state. Preferring per-environment sub-state to reduce blast radius.

2026-03-03 - Security (Maya Singh): Flagged requirement: all network endpoints created by module must be private by default and must integrate with KMS-backed logs. Also requested threat model before PR.

2026-03-05 - SRE (Diego Morales): Pushed for inclusion of an active readiness probe that returns a clear failure code for runtime to use; proposed timeline for adding synthetic FAILOVER test in staging.
2026-03-08 - Ava Chen: Implemented initial healthcheck lambda and added terraform resource outputs (kms_healthcheck_arn, fallback_flag). Created PR-4827 for review.
2026-03-11 - Review (Infra Team): Requested changes to default IAM policy (least privilege) and add terratest scenario for a simulated HSM disconnect. Assigned back to ENG-101234 for follow-up.
2026-03-12 - Ava Chen: Updated IAM policies, added terratest skeleton, and scheduled security review for 2026-03-15. Marking ticket In Progress and blocking 4 points on e2e test flakiness fix.
notes: Prototype uses a 'proxy-keybroker' sidecar pattern for on-prem HSMs that speaks a simplified gRPC protocol; this keeps module resources cloud-agnostic
We should avoid storing plaintext keys in state; use data sources referencing external keystores and only store references/ids in state outputs
Consider adding a 'simulate_failover' boolean input for terraform plan-time validation in CI to exercise fallback wiring without causing prod impact
Follow-on work: integrate with console-2026.04 rollout UI to display per-deployment KMS health status and provide manual failover control

source.title: Hosted SSE client-hang mode diagnostics and staged remediation plan
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Multiple customers reported streaming requests (SSE) that emit a subset of tokens then appear to 'hang' without a final event or EOF. Clients either wait indefinitely or reconnect and observe duplicate/overlapping tokens. Issue surfaces across Hosted and several Dedicated tenants after the 2026-02-14 edge rollout. This ticket contains triage notes, an interim mitigation, and a staged fix + rollout plan with QA and monitoring guidance.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Create a long-running generate call via /v1/streams with streaming SSE enabled and a prompt that produces >1024 tokens.
2) Observe token stream emitted for ~30-90s then SSE stops sending events without a final 'event: done' sentinel.
3) Client keeps connection open; after reconnect, partial repeats of last 16-64 tokens observed in some cases.
4) Repro rate higher when requests are routed through edge gateway nodes in eu-west-1 pool after traffic migration.
source.impact: High - multiple customers experienced stuck streaming sessions causing UI freezes, automated backoff loops, and elevated support escalations. Affected customers include FinStream (enterprise), ChatFlow (startup), and internal dogfood tenants. Approx 0.4% of streaming requests during peak windows started exhibiting hang behavior after edge rollout.
timeline: 2026-02-14: Edge gateway rollout (eu-west-1) completed.
2026-02-18: First customer report received - ChatFlow (support ticket #SF-881).
2026-02-20: Oncall triage found increased 'sse_connection_idle' rate in edge nodes. ENG-102345 opened.
2026-02-24: Reproduced locally with gateway configured to idle-timeout=65s. Observed suppressed finalization event in session handoff codepath.
2026-02-27: Hotfix patch (gateway keepalive and guard) deployed to canary; reduced repro rate on canary tenants.
2026-03-05: Delivered follow-up fix to runtime to make finalizer idempotent and emit explicit 'done' sentinel on graceful or forced disconnect.
2026-03-08: Canary rollouts for fix-stage 2 (5% tenants) -> monitoring green for 72h.
2026-03-12: Rolling plan to progressively increase to 50% traffic and schedule Dedicated tenant opt-in for next week.
investigation_notes: Symptom set: SSE stream halts without final 'done' event; client TCP connection often still ESTABLISHED; no upstream 5xx.
Edge gateway change introduced more aggressive keepalive multiplexing and a per-connection idle threshold (65s) to reclaim sockets under high load.

Regression in the streaming session lifecycle: when gateway initiated a half-close due to idle heuristics, the runtime's session finalizer path sometimes considered the session already finalized and suppressed the finalization event to avoid duplicate commits.
On reconnect, client replay logic invoked and server-side ephemeral KV cache merged prefixes, occasionally leading to 16-64 token duplication because last flush checkpoint was missing.
Telemetry gaps: we lacked a distinct metric for 'finalizer_emitted' and relied on inferred end-of-stream via connection close. Added explicit finalizer counter in debug builds.
source.root_cause: Combination of (A) new edge gateway idle/keepalive behavior that triggers an upstream half-close without propagating a graceful shutdown signal, and (B) a defensive regression in runtime finalizer that suppressed emitting the final sentinel if a session state flag indicated 'already committed'. The interaction of both caused silent stream termination without an explicit client-visible event.
source.workaround: Increase client-side timeout/backoff to detect missing final event after 10s of inactivity and proactively reconnect. For Hosted customers we rolled back gateway idle reclaim policy for affected node pool and enabled a temporary per-tenant bypass for streaming routes. Support provided a patch for ChatFlow to treat lack of final sentinel as ephemeral and fallback to polling the status endpoint.
fix_plan: Hotfix (already deployed to canary): Make gateway send 'session-terminate: graceful' header before half-close so runtime can flush finalizer.
Immediate runtime patch: Make finalizer idempotent and ensure it always emits a 'event: done' sentinel even when commit flag present.
Medium-term: Add explicit session heartbeats and 'finalizer_emitted' metric; gatestream will fail open to allow reconnection without duplicates.
Long-term: Implement deterministic sequence-number handoff and server-authored EOF token with monotonic sequence to avoid replay/duplication on reconnects.
Documentation: Update SDKs with client-side resilience guide and recommended reconnection handshake.
source.rollout_strategy: Canary 5% tenants -> monitor 72h -> 25% next day if no regression -> 50% then full. Dedicated tenants will receive opt-in instructions and Private packages will include runtime patch in 2026.03 on-prem bundle. Provide canary list to SRE and PMs ahead of each step.
qa_plan: Synthetic test harness: replay long-stream scenarios across gateway versions with induced idle timeouts and assert final 'done' sentinel is received within 5s of stream completion.
Integration tests: end-to-end tests using Linux clients behind proxy that simulate half-close behavior.
Load tests: validate no increased token duplication at 2x typical QPS for streaming routes.
Canary verification checklist: consumer-facing error rate (client 5xx/streaming hangs) < 0.1% and duplicate token rate < 0.01% over 72 hours.
customers_impacted: Internal-dogfood - multiple product teams observed hangs
source.notes_for_support: Provide customers a short-term client patch: treat 10s of SSE silence as terminal and reconnect to status endpoint. If customer runs Dedicated, schedule an opt-in to the canary runtime patch; record tenant IDs in the canary_allowlist. Escalate any private on-prem clusters that can't be patched to Solutions for offline upgrade planning.

source.title: Unify SDK retry semantics + streaming ergonomics and add changelog automation for v2 deprecations
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Background: v1 SDKs evolved independently and exhibit subtle differences in retry behaviour (default backoffs, idempotency assumptions) and streaming ergonomics (callback-heavy APIs, inconsistent cancellation/backpressure patterns). This has led to user confusion when migrating between languages, flaky integrations, and duplicated docs.

Goal: Define and implement a single, cross-language contract for retry semantics and streaming ergonomics in SDK v2, provide clear migration docs and language-specific examples (Python/TypeScript/Go), and build a small changelog automation tool that detects deprecated APIs across SDKs and generates a consolidated deprecation changelog entry for releases.

Scope:
- Design and implement the retry policy API surface across Python/TS/Go SDKs (RetryPolicy object / options, idempotency marker, standard backoff presets).
- Replace ad-hoc streaming callbacks with a uniform ergonomic surface: async iterators in Python/TS, channel-based pattern for Go, explicit cancellation tokens and early-close helpers.
- Update examples and migration guide pages in docs for each language with pattern conversions (v1 -> v2).
- Implement a changelog automation script that parses SDK public API annotations (deprecation decorators/tags), collects PR metadata, and emits a release-ready deprecation section.
- Add unit/integration tests that validate cross-language parity for a selected subset of behaviours (retry triggers, max attempts, streaming cancel propagation).

Out of scope: full rewrite of every example in sitewide docs (we will migrate main, canonical examples only).
acceptance_criteria: A unified RetryPolicy API is documented and implemented in Python, TypeScript, and Go SDKs with consistent default behaviour across languages
Streaming ergonomics updated: async iterator + cancel semantics in Python/TS and channel + context cancel in Go, with code samples for each
Migration guide created that maps common v1 patterns to v2 (retries, streaming, error handling) with copy-pasteable snippets
Changelog automation script generates a single deprecation list for a release by scanning SDK repositories and PR labels
Tests cover retry backoff correctness and streaming cancellation propagation with CI signals for regressions
Documentation pages approved by Docs and Developer Experience (PRs linked)
comments: 2026-02-16 - Ava Chen: Kickoff meeting notes posted to Confluence: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SDK/Unified+Retries+%26+Streaming

2026-02-20 - Liam Patel: RFC submitted (internal): https://drive.redwood.ai/drive/folders/sdk-unified-retries-rfc (high-level API shapes and migration mapping). Requesting review from @Sofia Martinez (Docs) and @Noah Kim (Runtime).

2026-02-27 - Noah Kim: Runtime acceptance: confirmed we can expose idempotency key hook for non-idempotent endpoints. Need to update server-side idempotency header to be optional for v2 compatibility; opened ENG-12902.
2026-03-02 - Sofia Martinez: Docs feedback: prefer `retry_policy` named param over `retries` for clarity; examples should show backoff presets ("fast", "default", "conservative").

2026-03-04 - Liam Patel: Prototype Python API implemented in branch github.com/redwood/sdk-python/tree/feat/unified-retries (PR: https://github.com/redwood/sdk-python/pull/412). Tests added for exponential backoff + jitter.

2026-03-06 - PR review: TypeScript implementation raised concern about changing default streaming API shape (breaking). Decided to keep adapters for v1 ergonomic patterns and mark them deprecated in favour of async iterators. See PR: https://github.com/redwood/sdk-js/pull/278

2026-03-10 - Security note from Rajiv Desai: Ensure changelog automation does not leak internal-only PR notes. Script will only include public PR titles and explicit changelog labels. Added security_review_required flag.

2026-03-12 - Liam Patel: Initial changelog automation PoC merged to tools repo: https://github.com/redwood/tools/pull/83. It scans repo tags and PR labels `changelog:deprecation` and compiles deprecation lines into CHANGELOG-DEPRECATIONS.md
source.notes: Migration approach: provide compatibility adapters in each SDK that translate v1 call patterns to v2 defaults; adapters are deprecated and will be removed in a future minor. Changelog automation will tag each found deprecated symbol with suggested migration text when available.

- Add unit/integration tests that validate cross-language parity for a selected subset of behaviours (retry triggers, max attempts, streaming cancel propagation).

Out of scope: full rewrite of every example in sitewide docs (we will migrate main, canonical examples only).

source.title: Guided POP routing with quantile-differential and link-safety score
source.creator: Ethan Park
source.description:
Problem statement: We need a region/POP (Point-of-Presence) assignment policy that is resilient to transient spikes that inflate p95 while preserving tail SLOs, and that can also rapidly avoid links showing degraded packet-loss or high retransmit rates. Existing approaches overreact to short-lived p95 excursions or ignore cross-metric correlation.

Proposal summary: Implement a 'quantile-differential with link-safety' guider that computes a composite preference score per POP combining: (1) smoothed p50 and p95 (EWMA windowed), (2) the p95-p50 delta (as a sensitivity factor), and (3) a network link-safety penalty derived from loss/rtx/icmp-failure telemetry. The guider will provide ranked POP choices and a binary failover trigger when safety thresholds breach. It prioritizes stable low-latency POPs but gracefully shifts when tail-risk or network degradation is sustained.

Algorithm (high level):
- Maintain two EWMAs per POP: EWMA50 (p50) and EWMA95 (p95) with different alphas (alpha50 >> alpha95).
- Compute quantile_delta = max(0, EWMA95 - EWMA50).
- Normalize latency_score = sigmoid((target_p50 - EWMA50) / sigma) to map lower-latency to higher score.
- Compute stability_penalty = clamp(quantile_delta / q_threshold, 0, 1).
- Compute link_safety_penalty = clamp( loss_rate*Lw + retransmit_rate*Rw + icmp_error*Iw, 0, 1 ). Weights (Lw,Rw,Iw) configured per-region.
- Composite_score = latency_score * (1 - stability_penalty) * (1 - link_safety_penalty).
- If link_safety_penalty > failover_cutoff OR EWMA95 > hard_latency_cutoff for duration T_fail, mark POP as degraded and exclude for N_cooldown seconds.

Why this is different: Instead of raw percentiles or single-threshold circuit breakers, this approach uses the quantile gap as an explicit signal of tail instability and blends it with independent network health metrics to avoid routing towards POPs with packet-level problems. It also provides tunable sensitivity so SREs can choose how aggressive failovers are for different customer tiers.
source.acceptance_criteria:
1) Deployed guider returns stable ranking for a synthetic workload with injected transient p95 spikes (no flapping within 2m).
2) When packet loss or retransmit rate exceeds configured thresholds, composite_score for affected POPs drops and traffic shifts within configured failover windows.
3) Integration tests cover EWMA tuning, quantile_delta computation, and cooldown logic.
4) Observability: dashboard shows per-POP components (EWMA50, EWMA95, quantile_delta, link_safety) and an alert fires if failover_counter > 3 in 10m.
5) Backfill simulation: Using last 30 days of telemetry, policy should reduce tail-exposure (measured p95 weighted by traffic) by >= 12% for the selected canary service.
source.tasks:
- Prototype scoring function in routing-sim repo (PR: GH/routing-sim#draft)
- Add telemetry extraction job to compute EWMA50/EWMA95 (job: infra/telemetry/ewma-pop)
- Implement gating and cooldown in region-orchestrator with feature flag (rollout: canary then 25% then 100%)
- Create console dashboard tiles: pop-scoring, failover-events, safety-metrics
- E2E canary against staging services, monitor for route flaps and latency regressions
- Run backfill analysis and publish results to Confluence: /confluence/display/INFR/quantile-differential-guided-routing
- Update runbook with mitigation steps and revert flag
source.comments:
2026-02-10 Ethan Park: Created ticket after SRE postmortem showed repeated brittle failovers during a short-lived regional maintenance event. Suggested explicitly factoring in quantile gap.

2026-02-18 Priya Patel: Initial prototype implemented in routing-sim; found alpha choices matter. Added parameters to configuration: alpha50=0.2, alpha95=0.05, q_threshold=30ms, sigma=40ms.

2026-02-24 Liam O'Connor (SRE): Reviewed penalties; suggested weighting link_safety higher for enterprise customer tiers. Added customer-tier multiplier.

2026-03-03 Design note (Maya Chen): Requested dashboard visual showing stacked components so PMs can understand why a POP was deprioritized.

2026-03-09 Engineering: Ran backfill on last 60 days for service 'inference-api' traffic. Early results: composite-driven routing reduced weighted p95 by ~14% in test window and prevented 6 unnecessary cross-region failovers. Full backfill PR: GH/routing-analysis#42 (draft).

2026-03-12 Priya Patel: Switched feature-flag to canary (10% traffic) in us-west region. Monitoring for flapping and customer errors active. No regressions observed in first 48h. Next steps: expand to 25% and run chaos test to inject packet loss on one POP.

Links: GH prototype: https://github.com/redwood-inference/routing-sim/pull/153 (draft); Backfill analysis: https://github.com/redwood-inference/routing-analysis/pull/42; Confluence spec (draft): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/INFR/Quantile-Differential-Guided-Routing
source.last_note: Canary active in us-west (10%) as of 2026-03-12. Watch for alert RU-RTE-POP-FLAP and console metric 'pop.quantile_delta' for unexpected behavior. If failover_count > 5 in 1h, revert feature flag.

source.title: Precision lane traffic controller for demand-driven FP8/INT8 dispatch
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: Dequantization overhead and mismatched kernel precision choices are a dominant cost for many streaming/long-sequence workloads. Current heuristics select INT8 vs FP8 at model-load or route time, which misses opportunity to route on per-request latency/quality characteristics and to amortize dequant costs across microbatches.

Goal: Implement a Precision Lane Traffic Controller (PLTC) that dynamically admits requests into precision lanes (INT8 lane, FP8 lane, FP16 fallback) at runtime based on a short-term admission window. The controller should: (1) reduce per-token dequant overhead by grouping compatible requests, (2) avoid high tail latency by gating slow/misaligned requests to higher-precision lanes, and (3) provide telemetry for per-lane quality/latency tradeoffs.

What this ticket delivers: design, prototype, and a staged rollout of the admission controller and a lightweight lane adapter in the serving runtime. Includes metrics, backpressure behavior, canary rollout plan and integration with the Console telemetry.

Non-goals: retraining quantization scales, model-level quantization conversions, or changing kernel implementations beyond the admission adapter and lightweight batching hooks.
acceptance_criteria: PLTC can admit requests into INT8 or FP8 lanes with an admission latency < 200us
Microbatch coalescing across admitted INT8 requests reduces measured dequant CPU/GPU cost per token by >= 20% on benchmark workloads
Tail (p95/p99) decode latency improves or is unchanged for 90% of canonical prompts compared to current router
Console shows per-lane token counts, lane hit rates, and quality deltas linked to eval prompt sets
Canary rollout to 5% of Hosted traffic with automatic rollback on quality regression > 1.5% or latency SLO breach
notes: Key idea: short admission windows (2-8ms) to collect compatible requests and align KV cache shapes to avoid per-request dequant churn.
Admission decisions use: estimated output length, current KV cache occupancy, prompt prefix similarity (hash buckets), and recent per-customer lane success history.
Will expose a lightweight lane policy DSL to Console so SRE/PL teams can tweak gating without code deploys.
Risk: over-eager coalescing could increase amplification for latency-sensitive requests; plan includes strict SLO-based escape hatch to immediate FP16 pass-through.
design_decisions: Use a 3-lane model: INT8 (fast path), FP8 (quality-preserving fast path), FP16 fallback (safety) rather than boolean quant decide to enable mid-request adjustments.
Admission controller implemented as a runtime front-door module that runs before batching and kernel selection to tag requests with a lane label.
Microbatch coalescer groups only requests with identical sequence-length bucket and compatible prefix-hash to keep KV cache layout aligned.
Telemetry: emit per-request lane tag and pre/post-dequant timing spans to tracing and per-route histograms for Console aggregation.
implementation_plan: Phase 1 (prototype): runtime admission module + in-process lane label; synthetic perf harness against long-context benchmarks; PR: github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/4822
Phase 2 (robustness): integrate escape hatch, backpressure handling, and per-customer policy; add unit tests and fuzzing; PR: github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/4889
Phase 3 (canary): deploy to canary host pool, gather Console telemetry and human evals; rollout to 5% Hosted on success; PR: github.com/redwood-inference/infra/pull/214
Phase 4: iterate on heuristics with telemetry and add kernel-level micro-optimizations if hotspots found.
benchmarks: Initial synthetic run (gpt-like 8k seq): baseline latency p95=210ms, with PLTC prototype p95=188ms (10% improvement); p99 unchanged within noise
Dequant CPU time per 1M tokens reduced from 42s to 31s in INT8 microbatched lane (26% reduction) in microbatch-friendly workloads
Memory overhead of admission window < 1% of serving heap; extra tracing adds ~0.6% allocation in hotpath
tradeoffs: Stricter admission windows increase microbatching benefits but raise risk of increased latency for cold requests; mitigate via dynamic window tuning and escape hatch.
Prefix-hash bucketing improves KV cache alignment but can fragment small-volume customers; provide policy exceptions per-customer.
Adding an admission step increases code complexity in the front-door fastpath; we keep the module minimal and well-tested.
rollout_plan: Internal alpha: run on dedicated perf pool with synthetic traffic for 2 weeks and validate metrics
External canary: route 5% of Hosted traffic, monitor lane quality delta via eval set and automated checks for 48 hours
Gradual increase: 5% -> 20% -> 50% over 2 weeks if metrics stable; emergency rollback toggles in Console
Post-rollout: run 2-week observation window, then mark complete and schedule follow-ups for kernel-level fusions if telemetry indicates.
links: Related tickets: ENG-27191 (streaming quant path planner), ENG-61201 (lazy dequant dispatch)
comments: 2026-02-11 - Ava Chen: Kickoff meeting notes added to Confluence. Decided on 3-lane model after discussing FP8 quality numbers; prototype scope agreed.
2026-02-18 - Mateo Ruiz: Prototype admission controller basic path implemented. Microbatch coalescer uses sequence-length bucketing; initial harness run attached in Drive.
2026-02-22 - Priya Nambiar (PM): Requested per-customer override and Console toggles added to Phase 2 scope to address enterprise needs.
2026-03-02 - Bench results: INT8 lane shows 26% reduction in dequant CPU for long-context benchmark; p95 improved ~10% (see bench report link).
2026-03-05 - Code review feedback (ENG): need clearer invariants for KV cache alignment when merging cross-prefix requests. Mateo to add assertions and test cases.
2026-03-09 - SRE note: Canary infra PR needs an escape route that bypasses admission controller for emergency traffic shaping; infra PR updated.
2026-03-12 - Mateo Ruiz: Merged Phase 1 prototype to canary branch. Preparing canary with 5% traffic tomorrow; monitoring dashboards prepopulated.

source.title: Billing attestation: chain-of-custody, retention schedule, and least-privilege access matrix
source.creator: Sofia Chen
source.description: Create a repeatable attestation package for billing and metering evidence that satisfies SOC-style evidence requests and internal compliance checks. Deliverables include: a documented chain-of-custody for usage events, a retention schedule mapped to legal and commercial requirements, an explicit least-privilege access matrix for services and people, and automated artifacts (signed snapshots, tamper-evident manifests) that can be exported to auditors.
acceptance_criteria: Defined retention schedule covering raw usage events, aggregated billing ledgers, and derived reconciliation artifacts with justification for each retention window.

Chain-of-custody documented end-to-end: event generation -> ingestion -> transformation -> billing ledger -> archival. Each hop includes who/what has write/read access and what cryptographic protections are applied.
Access matrix (service & human) implemented in IAM + documented mapping to least-privilege roles; sample attestations for two customer accounts produced.
Automated weekly snapshot job produces signed, immutable manifests (hash + signature) stored in a tamper-evident bucket with retention and access audit logs.
A SOC-style evidence bundle (PDF + manifest + access logs) can be assembled for a given time window within 24 hours.
E2E regression test to validate billing reconciliation against snapshots in CI and a runbook for dispute investigations.
PRs implementing the snapshot job, RBAC changes, and retention policies merged to main and rollout plan documented.
tasks: Inventory current billing/metering touchpoints (producers, pipelines, transforms, ledgers)
Define retention TTLs and map to legal/commercial requirements
Design chain-of-custody doc with diagram and per-hop controls
Implement signed snapshot job (lambda/cron) that writes manifest (SHA256) and signs with KMS key
Create IAM role-to-permission matrix and update Terraform policies
Add access logging and alerting for any writes to billing buckets/ledgers
Add export endpoint to build SOC evidence bundle (PDF generator + artifact packager)
Add CI e2e test that replays synthetic usage and validates reconciliation against stored snapshots
Write runbook for internal audits and customer dispute investigations
Schedule security review and compliance walkthrough with Legal/Trust
progress_updates: 2026-03-02: Kickoff meeting with PM, Security, and Legal. Scoped required artifacts and agreed on 3 retention tiers (30d, 1y, 7y) based on data type and region. Action: draft retention rationale.

2026-03-04: Prototype snapshot job created (internal branch: github.com/redwood-inference/billing-snapshots#pr-142). Initial manifest format: {version, window_start, window_end, ledger_keys, sha256, kms_sig}.

2026-03-06: Security review flagged KMS key scoping: decided to use a dedicated attestation key with rotation policy and restricted decrypt only to build agents. Follow-up: add key policy to Terraform.
2026-03-08: PM reviewed access matrix; requested a separate reviewer role for SOC bundles to avoid cross-privilege exposure. Implemented reviewer role in staging IAM.
2026-03-10: Integration test added to CI that runs snapshot job on synthetic data and verifies manifest signature (CI job: ci/billing-attestation/e2e).
2026-03-12: Draft Confluence doc posted for internal review: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/INF/Billing+Attestation+and+Chain+of+Custody (feedback requested from Legal and Trust)
2026-03-12: Raised follow-up ticket for long-term cold archival cost analysis (PM to own).
audit_steps: Establish write-once manifest format with canonical JSON canonicalization and SHA-256 digest
Sign each manifest with dedicated KMS signing key; store public certificate for auditor verification
Keep ordered append-only ledger snapshots with incremental sequence numbers and window timestamps
Maintain immutable archival copies in a bucket with Object Lock (WORM) for 7-year tier where applicable
Record IAM policy changes in the change-log service and surface related entries in the evidence bundle
Capture all access logs (bucket, DB, KMS key usage) for the relevant window and include them in the audit bundle
Run a weekly reconciliation job comparing ledger tallies to snapshot digests and produce a regression report
Preserve provenance metadata (producer id, ingestion node id, transform version, model/agent version) alongside each ledger record
decisions: Retention tiers: 30 days for raw transient telemetry, 1 year for aggregated billing ledgers (default for EU customers), 7 years for legal hold/enterprise contracts. Tradeoff: storage cost vs. regulatory/commercial risk.
Use KMS signing (asymmetric) for manifests instead of custom HSM to reduce operational burden; acceptable per Security after key policy scoping.
Implement reviewer-only role for evidence bundle assembly to reduce blasts of privilege; auditors receive exported bundle out-of-band rather than live read access.
Prefer S3 Object Lock (governance mode where required) over proprietary append-only DB for archival to leverage cloud durability and existing tooling.
Include synthetic end-to-end e2e test in CI rather than relying solely on periodic QA runs to ensure regression detection.
Default snapshots cadence: daily deterministic end-of-day snapshots and hourly incremental manifests for high-volume customers (configurable per account).
Scope of SOC bundle: include manifests, access logs, KMS usage logs, reconciliation report, and runbook; exclude PII by design (masking at ingestion).
follow_ups: Create ENG ticket to implement long-term cold storage cost model and billing signal for retention tier changes
PM to coordinate with Legal to finalize retention rationale per jurisdiction
Schedule compliance tabletop exercise with external auditor using a dry-run SOC bundle
Add telemetry-based alerts for any unsigned manifest generation or failed reconciliation
Update onboarding docs for SRE and Support on how to request evidence bundles for customers
comments: Aisha Kapoor (Legal) - 2026-03-03: Confirmed that 7-year retention should only apply when contractually required; otherwise 1-year default is preferable.
Marco Liu (Security) - 2026-03-05: Approved KMS asymmetric signing approach once key policies and rotation schedule are documented.
Priya Rao (PM) - 2026-03-09: Please add an example evidence bundle for a synthetic customer (we need one for sales demos).
Design Review - 2026-03-11: Diagram needs to clearly show separation between attestation pipeline and live billing processing to avoid confusion.

source.title: Concilium: token-coalescer for quantized kernel alignment
source.creator: Aisha Raman
source.description: Implement a token-coalescing runtime layer (Concilium) that reorganizes prefills and microbatches to maximize reuse for INT8/FP8-friendly blocked kernels. The goal is to reduce dequantization overhead and improve kernel alignment for attention and KV prefill paths without changing model semantics. Concilium will: 1) coalesce adjacent token slices across streams where safe, 2) introduce a headwise remap metadata layer so blocked kernels can operate on a coalesced memory layout, 3) provide lazy dequantization views to avoid materializing full FP tensors when not needed, and 4) coordinate prefill scheduling so that coalesced blocks are filled and decoded with minimal synchronization overhead.
acceptance_criteria: P95 decode+prefill latency reduced by >=7% on 1K concurrent streams benchmark (baseline: runtime-1.20 INT8 path)
Average GPU token compute time reduced by >=10% on internal microbatch traces for large-chat workloads
No quality regressions >1% measured on the canonical eval prompt set (text-coherence and next-token accuracy)
Unit-level and integration tests for headwise remap and lazy dequant pass in CI
New telemetry shows correct cache reuse (KV reuse metric increase) and no memory leaks
Canary rollout on dedicated cluster with rollback plan verified
decisions: Use lazy dequantization views instead of eager FP materialization to minimize memory pressure and copy overhead
Expose a compact headwise-remap descriptor to kernels (no change to public API) so kernels can accept coalesced stride maps
Implement coalescing as a best-effort runtime optimization gated by heuristics (sequence alignment, head-count match, per-request privacy tags)
Schedule coalesced prefill as a separate microbatching pass to ensure alignment with KV caching and to allow fallbacks to per-request prefill when policies conflict
comments: 2026-02-25 Aisha Raman: Initial spec created. Proposal emphasizes lazy dequant and headwise remap to avoid full FP captures. Added acceptance criteria and benchmark plan.

2026-03-02 Mateo Alvarez: Implemented prototype of coalescer that groups tokens across 4-stream boundary for the large-chat workload. Observed ~9% token compute time reduction in synthetic run. Need to validate memory behavior.
2026-03-05 Priya Nair (PM): Confirmed that privacy labels must block coalescing for certain enterprise customers. Added policy gating requirement.

2026-03-08 Linus Berg (Eng reviewer): Reviewed kernel API. Suggested moving remap descriptor into a compact 64-bit table to reduce per-call overhead. Also flagged issue with transient CUDA memcpys in current prototype.

2026-03-10 Mateo Alvarez: Ran full eval suite — quality regressions within noise (<0.4%). Found a pathological layout leading to extra CUDA kernel launches; mitigation is to add a batching coalescer flush threshold.
2026-03-12 Aisha Raman: Merged initial kernel shim PR to feature branch. Next: add more heuristics and CI perf gating. Plan canary in dedicated cluster week of 2026-03-22.

source.title: Stratified prefetch sentinel with tiered denylist guards
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Problem: We need a safe, per-tenant and per-route mechanism that recommends adaptive prefetch (KV/prefix) and batching configurations to reduce per-token cost while preserving tail-latency SLOs. Existing policy runners produce global suggestions that either under-optimize low-volume tenants or risk quality regressions for high-priority customers. This ticket designs and implements a \"Sentinel\" layer that fences automated config changes with tiered denylist controls (deny, allow, soft-allow), per-tenant segmentation, and an offline simulation + dry-run pipeline before live apply.
acceptance_criteria: Agent can produce per-tenant and per-route batching + prefetch recommendations with estimated cost delta and latency impact for 90th/99th percentiles
Offline simulator validates recommended config against historical traces and reports regressions >2% for model quality or >10ms tail latency
Safe apply pipeline performs staged canary (1% -> 10% -> 100%) with automated rollback triggers based on telemetry thresholds
Tiered denylist controls reject automated changes for 'enterprise' customers, require opt-in for 'sensitive' routes, and permit soft-allow for 'low-risk' tenants
UI/Console exposes per-tenant recommendation, rationale (tokens saved / latency delta), and one-click dry-run scheduling
Integration tests cover simulation accuracy, rollback trigger logic, and denylist enforcement policy
implementation_notes: Component: 'prefetch-sentinel' microservice that consumes policy suggestions and runs simulation; written in Go to reuse runtime telemetry clients
Store per-tenant profiles in ConfigStore (use existing tenant-profile schema from ENG-40211) with additional fields: risk_level, denylist_phase, preferred_canary_window
Simulation: replay historical request traces to estimate KV cache hit-rate and batching efficiency; reuse the 'route-sim' harness from ENG-263412 and extend for per-tenant context
Recommendation algorithm: combine heuristic rules (model length, avg tokens/customer, route latency sensitivity) with a simple cost model; include uncertainty band to drive conservative suggestions
APIs: POST /recommendations -> returns list of candidate configs and simulation results; POST /apply -> triggers safe-apply pipeline (requires an approver for high-risk tiers)
Console: new 'Recommendations' tab under Customer -> Optimizations showing score, expected savings, and 'Schedule Dry-run' action
rollout_plan: Phase 0 (dev): unit tests + local sim harness (2w)
Phase 1 (internal dry-run): run against 10% of internal traffic for low-risk tenants, no autopilot apply (1w)
Phase 2 (canary): automated safe-apply for eligible low-risk tenants with 1% then 10% ramp, monitor 24h windows (2w)
Phase 3 (gradual expand): expand to additional tenants after verification and product signoff; enterprise denylist remains enforced until manual opt-in
Phase 4 (GA): enable one-click recommendations in Console with guardrails and audit logging
rollback_plan: Primary triggers for auto-rollback: +5% error rate for inference path, tail latency increase >15ms 99th, model quality regression >3% on production prompts
telemetry_checks: Latency: compare canary vs baseline for p50/p90/p99 every 5m; alert if p99 delta > 15ms for 3 consecutive windows
Errors: absolute and relative error counts; rollback if error rate increases by >5 percentage points or doubles baseline
Quality: sample generator outputs compared against baseline prompt set; if quality metric drops by >3% trigger immediate pause
Cost: expected token cost reduction vs actual; surface delta in Console and require human review if savings underperform by >20%
activity: 2026-03-01: Ticket created by Ava Chen with initial design stub and success criteria
2026-03-03: Engineering design review - agreed on Go microservice + reuse of route-sim harness (notes linked)
2026-03-05: Data team provided 30 days of trace logs scoped to 500 tenants for simulation; added to links
2026-03-08: Proto API implemented and unit tests added; initial simulation shows 12% median cost reduction for low-risk tenants
2026-03-10: PM feedback (Priya) - add explicit enterprise denylist behavior and manual opt-in flow to Console
2026-03-12: Canary scaffolding completed; security reviewed audit logging paths (no KMS changes required)
Next: schedule internal dry-run and create PR against runtime for safe-apply hooks

title: Stratified prefetch sentinel with tiered denylist guards
preview: Problem: We need a safe, per-tenant and per-route mechanism that recommends adaptive prefetch (KV/prefix) and batching configurations to reduce per-token cost while preserving tail-latency SLOs. Existing policy runners produce global suggestions that either under-optimize low-volume tenants or risk quality regressions for high-priority customers. This ticket designs and implements a \"Sentinel\" layer that fences automated config changes with tiered denylist controls (deny, allow, soft-allow), per-tenant segmentation, and an offline simulation + dry-run pipeline before live apply.

Existing policy runners produce global suggestions that either under-optimize low-volume tenants or risk quality regressions for high-priority customers. This ticket designs and implements a \"Sentinel\" layer that fences automated config changes with tiered denylist controls (deny, allow, soft-allow), per-tenant segmentation, and an offline simulation + dry-run pipeline before live apply.

source.title: Investigate and hotpatch edge-case latency regression affecting customer workloads across Hosted/Dedicated/Private
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Summary:
A small subset (~2%) of long-session requests from two paying customers began exhibiting severe initial token latency (TTFT) regressions and reduced tokens/sec on 2026-03-09 after a rollout of runtime-1.25 -> runtime-1.26. The impact spans Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments but is most visible for: (a) long system prompts (>2k tokens), (b) models with KV-quant variants, and (c) routes using the Sherpa scheduler hint for lower-cost paths.

Observed behavior:
- TTFT increases from median 180ms to 1.2s for affected requests; tokens/sec drops by ~60% in steady-state for those sessions.
- Regression correlates with requests that trigger cross-worker KV prefetch and also hit a recently-introduced Sherpa hint path that defers kernel selection for up to 100ms while probing variant warm state.
- Customer workload traces (see dashboards) show a high rate of repeated KV cache misses leading to hot-path oscillation between quant and non-quant execution kernels.

Root-cause hypothesis (current):
1) Sherpa scheduler introduced a deferred probe window to prefer cheaper variants; under certain long-context patterns this probe overlaps with KV-prefetch timing, causing duplicate prefetch—and a race with kernel warmup.
2) Duplicate prefetch + affinity shuffle produces transient eviction of KV shards, amplifying misses and emitting repeated cold-start penalties (both TTFT and tokens/sec).
3) Quantized model variant selection path does not early-bypass the heavy probe when context length > 2k, so probe costs are incurred per first-token.

Immediate mitigations applied (timeline):
- 2026-03-10 02:15 UTC: Rolled emergency feature-flag to disable deferred Sherpa probe for all Hosted clusters (flag: sherpa.probe.defer=false).
- 2026-03-10 03:05 UTC: Applied configuration override on Dedicated control-plane to force immediate kernel pick for routes tagged with route:priority-customer.
- 2026-03-10 05:30 UTC: Issued hotpatch to Private release notes and customer playbook recommending: set runtime_hint:force_warmup=true and upgrade to runtime-1.26-patch.

Status and next actions:
- On Hosted the emergency flag dropped customer TTFT back to baseline for >95% of affected sessions (monitoring: hosted-ttft-incident-708182 dashboard).
- Dedicated clusters show partial improvement but some nodes still exhibit 2x median TTFT; suspected stale prefetch policy in older node images.

Planned immediate work (this ticket):
- Implement and ship a 72-hour hotpatch that: (a) short-circuits Sherpa probing when context_length > 1500, (b) deduplicates concurrent KV-prefetch requests per-session, and (c) adds a light-weight kernel-warm probe that does not evict KV shards.
- Orchestrate Dedicated and Private rollout with canary of 10% -> 50% -> 100% over 12 hours with automatic rollback on TTFT > 2x baseline.
- Add telemetry to flag:prefetch_dups and sherpa_probe_latency; create alert with runbook (pager duty escalation to SRE lead).

Testing and validation:
- Synthetic replay harness created (link below) reproduces the TTFT amplification on a local 8-GPU dev cluster. Test cases include long system prompts, alternating quant/non-quant variant switches, and Sherpa hints.
- Load test plan: 10k synthetic requests across permutations; threshold: median TTFT <= 300ms and tokens/sec >= 75% of baseline before broad rollout.

References & artifacts:
- Customer tickets: CS-4391 (Company A), CS-4412 (Company B)
- Dashboard (internal): https://metrics.redwood.ai/dashboards/hosted-ttft-incident-708182
- Confluence runbook draft: https://redwood.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/OPS/pages/22334455/Edge+Latency+Sherpa+Incident
- Repro harness: https://github.com/redwood-inference/inference-loadtests/tree/main/sherpa-ttft-repro
- GitHub PR (hotpatch prototype): https://github.com/redwood-inference/inference-runtime/pull/5482

Acceptance criteria:
- Hotpatch merged and deployed to Hosted with validation run showing <=1.2x baseline TTFT for 99th percentile and tokens/sec within 90% of baseline for affected routes.
- Dedicated/Private rollout coordinated with SE and Customer Success teams; documented in release notes.
- Post-mortem including root-cause analysis and follow-up work items completed within 7 days of rollback/closure.
comments: 2026-03-10 02:00 UTC — Ava Chen: Triage call done. Scoped to Sherpa probe + KV-prefetch interaction. Assigned Diego to run repro and craft hotpatch.
2026-03-10 02:15 UTC — Diego Martinez: Flag rolled on Hosted (sherpa.probe.defer=false). Early telemetry good for Company A. Still seeing residuals on Dedicated clusters (some node images at 1.24).
2026-03-10 04:10 UTC — Liz Patel (SRE): Canary on hosted showed 98% reduction in TTFT spikes. Creating alert for prefetch_dup_rate > 0.5%.
2026-03-11 09:22 UTC — Diego Martinez: Prototype dedupe logic implemented in PR #5482. Running local 8-GPU repro; reproduces 3x TTFT without fix, baseline with fix.
2026-03-12 14:03 UTC — Maya Brooks (Solutions): Contacted Company B with mitigation steps; they applied runtime_hint override and reported 70% improvement.
2026-03-12 15:30 UTC — Diego Martinez: Preparing hotpatch release candidate for runtime-1.26.1 with QA checklist. ETA to merge: 2026-03-13 02:00 UTC.
follow_up_actions: Backfill unit+integration tests that assert no duplicate prefetch for concurrent session requests
Investigate longer-term change: make Sherpa probe cost-aware and context-length-aware in scheduler heuristics
Add per-route instrumentation in Console to show prefetch_dup_rate and sherpa_probe_latency
Coordinate with Dedicated infra to update older node images (1.24 -> 1.26) and re-run canaries

source.title: KV cache eviction regression & poison-susceptibility follow-up
source.creator: Aisha Khatri
source.description:
Summary: During a production capacity run on 2026-02-28 we observed repeated latency spikes and degraded throughput across several hosted clusters. Root cause analysis identified a compound failure mode in the KV/prefix cache: an eviction heuristic regression combined with an edge-case in cache key normalization allowed stale/duplicated entries to remain referenced, causing memory growth and periodic cache poisoning (incorrect KV lookup results for hot prefixes). This ticket tracks follow-ups and action items from the postmortem aiming to fully remediate the regression, add guardrails, and close monitoring gaps.

What happened: A change in the eviction score calculation (PR #1984) altered LRU aging for small shard segments. Under typical workloads this increased cache churn, but under a specific prefix distribution we saw double-insert races where identical logical keys were stored with different internal representations (normalization mismatch). Eviction then removed the newer entry while the older, semantically incorrect entry remained reachable through a stale pointer in the prefix index. Over hours this caused memory to grow and some requests to return mismatched KV payloads (cache poisoning).

Impact: Intermittent elevated tail latency, errors in a small subset of requests returning outdated embeddings for cached prompts, and elevated memory usage leading to several autoscaler-triggered node restarts in affected Dedicated clusters. Customer-facing impact was limited but reproducible in internal load tests; severity classified as P0 for platform stability.

Mitigations already applied: rolled back PR #1984, disabled the new normalization path behind a feature flag, and deployed a short-term GC sweep job to reclaim orphaned entries (runbook: https://confluence.redwood.ai/runbooks/kv-cache-orphan-sweep). Immediate pressure subsided after rollback but the underlying correctness and observability gaps remain.
action_items: Audit and unify key normalization: add canonicalizer utility and exhaustive unit tests; owner=Mateo; due=2026-03-15
Fix eviction heuristic: revert heuristic changes and implement deterministic eviction metrics for small shards; owner=Priya Rao; due=2026-03-18
Implement entry versioning and validation: store per-entry checksum and timestamp to detect stale pointers at lookup time; owner=Ethan Chu; due=2026-03-22
Extend CI to include heavy prefix-distribution fuzz tests that simulate double-insert races; owner=CI Team; due=2026-04-01
Add memory-watermark alerting and automated sweep: create Datadog dashboard + a non-disruptive reclaim job with safety windows; owner=Ops (Samira); due=2026-03-19
Backfill telemetry for cache mutations: instrument write/evict/normalize counters and sample diffs to S3 for forensic replay; owner=Telemetry; due=2026-03-20
Run a controlled canary for the fixed path on staging-dedicated with 72h soak and verified metrics; owner=Release Eng; due=2026-03-26
Security review for cache poisoning vector: threat model and mitigation signoff; owner=Security (Liam); due=2026-03-17
source.resolution_summary: Short-term rollback and sweep stabilized clusters. Long-term plan focuses on canonical key normalization, eviction determinism, defensive entry validation, and improved CI/monitoring. No customer PII exposed; security review required due to potential poisoning vector. Most action items have clear owners and timelines; progress tracked in this ticket and cross-linked PRs.
comments: 2026-03-01 | Aisha Khatri: Created ticket after postmortem review. Added initial mitigation notes and prioritized canonicalization as highest risk for correctness.
2026-03-02 | Mateo Alvarez: Rolled back PR #1984 at 03:15 UTC. Deployed orphan-sweep job v0.1 (run id: sweep-20260302-01). Memory usage returned to baseline over ~4 hours.
2026-03-04 | Priya Rao: Investigated eviction codepath; identified two heuristics that degrade with small-shard cardinality. Draft PR: github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/2041.
2026-03-06 | Ethan Chu: Prototyped per-entry checksum and a fast validation step that costs ~0.5% latency on writes. Open PR: github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/2060. Running microbenchmarks.
2026-03-08 | Samira (Ops): Set temporary Datadog monitors for heap-growth > 85% per shard and alert paging to on-call. Starting automated reclaim job tests on staging.
2026-03-10 | Security (Liam): Started threat modeling; identified two high-risk scenarios if poisoning persisted. Requested design doc before release to prod.
2026-03-12 | Mateo Alvarez: Merged normalization canonicalizer tests (github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/2089) into feature branch. Next: integrate with eviction path and run staging soak.
2026-03-12 | Aisha Khatri: Updated due dates and added ENG-7410/ENG-7422 as orchestrator and CI dependencies. Scheduling a cross-team sync Friday 2026-03-13 16:00 UTC.

source.title: Workload-temporal coalescer: heuristics, profile-driven parameters, and safe apply
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Goal: implement a workload-temporal coalescer that recommends continuous batching parameters and safe config apply/rollback policies based on multi-dimensional workload profiles. This ticket covers the profiling pipeline, heuristic parameterization (window sizes, hold thresholds, micro-coalescing probabilities), the shadow-apply + canary rollout strategy, and automated rollback triggers.
comments: 2026-02-16 - Ava Chen: Kickoff notes added. Alignment with Optimize-Batching-2026 OKRs. Decided profiling will run on 7-day sliding windows and emit per-route signatures (arrival distribution, median seq length, tail seq percentiles, inter-arrival autocorrelation).

2026-02-22 - Diego Patel: PoC of temporal window sampler done (branch: feat/temporal-coalescer-sampler). Early sims show potential 10-16% token-cost reduction for conversational workloads with 95th p99 latency increase <5ms in our cluster sim. Added benchmark artifacts in Confluence.

2026-02-28 - PM review by Laila Morris: Asked for stronger rollback criteria and customer-facing opt-out. Agreed to add an immediate rollback if error rate delta >0.3% or if p99 latency increases by >20% relative to baseline for 5m aggregated window. Also added opt-out label for routes flagged as latency-sensitive.

2026-03-04 - Design feedback by Marco Ruiz: Suggested clarifying UX in Console: show expected cost delta, safety margin, and conservative confidence band. Will file a follow-up DES ticket for Console card mockups.

2026-03-09 - Diego Patel: Implemented shadow-run mode and metric comparison harness. Running internal A/B shadow tests for 48 hours on 10% sampled traffic. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/2891

2026-03-12 - SRE review by Priya Singh: Raised concerns about KV cache priming causing transient write amplification; suggested adding a priming cooldown and a max-cache-writes-per-minute limiter. Added to implementation notes.
source.implementation_notes:
Profiling: ingest 7-day request traces and compute per-route signatures: inter-arrival CDF, sequence-length histogram, user-session affinity, kv-reuse-rate. Heuristics: 1) window_size = f(p25_interarrival, p50_seq_len) using a small lookup table learned from offline sims; 2) hold_thresholds proportional to expected KV reuse and latency SLO headroom; 3) micro-coalesce probability decays with predicted tail sensitivity.

Apply flow: 1) compute recommended config and confidence score; 2) create config in staging as 'shadow' and mirror sampled traffic (10%) for 48h; 3) analyze shadow vs baseline using uplift tests on cost-per-token, request-latency p50/p95/p99, error-rate, and model output divergence score; 4) if safety checks pass, schedule gradual rollout: 1% -> 5% -> 20% -> 50% -> 100% over configurable windows with HALT checks at each step.

Rollback triggers: immediate rollback if error_rate_delta > 0.3% OR p99_latency_increase > 20% for a 5m rolling window OR model-output-divergence exceeds calibrated threshold (seen in eval harness). Soft rollback (pause rollout) if cost savings below expected band but metrics stable.

Safety mitigations: conservative defaults, per-tenant opt-out, max-coalesce-window hard cap, KV-priming cooldown, and rate-limiting cache writes. All parameter changes are recorded in audit logs for Private deployments.
acceptance_criteria: Profiling pipeline emits per-route signatures daily and retains 7-day windows
Recommendation engine produces deterministic config suggestions given a route signature
Shadow-run harness compares baseline and shadow with automated statistical tests
Rollout automation implements staged traffic ramps with HALT checks and automatic rollback
SRE-approved priming cooldown and write-rate limiter are in place
Console surfaces recommended config, confidence, expected cost delta, and safety rationale
source.rollout_plan: Phase 0 (internal): run offline sims across 50 representative workloads and validate against the benchmark harness (2 weeks). Phase 1 (shadow): mirror 10% sampled traffic for 48h on non-critical routes (2 weeks). Phase 2 (canary): canary rollout to <5% traffic with 30m observation windows and health gates (1 week). Phase 3 (gradual): incrementally increase to 100% across safe segments with automated HALT/rollback. Post-rollout: 30-day monitoring and weekly regression alerts. Each phase requires sign-off from SRE and PM.
telemetry_queries: BigQuery: sql/queries/batching_profile_signatures.sql - aggregates request inter-arrival and seq length histograms
Monitoring: alert rule 'batching-rollout-halt' - triggers on error_rate_delta > 0.3% or p99 latency increase > 20%

source.title: Go SDK: schema-first decoder patterns and streaming ergonomics
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Goal: Design and implement a schema-first decoder set for the Go SDK that provides strong compile-time structs, ergonomic generics-based helpers for common patterns, and streaming-friendly decoders that interoperate with the SDK's retry/backpressure model. This ticket covers design, a prototype library, integration tests, and an example app demonstrating structured streaming + resumable retries.
acceptance_criteria: A documented API for declaring schema-driven decoders that map streaming tokens to typed Go structs
Generics-based helper functions for common decoding patterns (envelopes, arrays, fallback unions) with examples
Streaming combinator primitives (MapStream, FilterStream, ChunkedDecode) that compose with the existing SDK stream interface
Retry-safe streaming checkpoint API allowing resumed reads without duplicating semantic side effects
Unit/integration tests covering JSON framing, partial-object streaming, and error-recovery flows
An example in /examples/streaming-decoder demonstrating live decode, backpressure, and a canary retry scenario
PR merged to go-sdk repo and release note entry in release-2026.03
progress_updates: 2026-02-11: Spike completed. Prototype uses a small set of generic Decoder[T] interfaces and a streaming Tokenizer that yields JSON fragments. Chose to keep decoder semantics pull-based to fit Go idioms.

2026-02-18: Internal demo to SDK guild. Feedback: prefer explicit envelope validation helpers and clearer distinction between "atomic" vs "incremental" decode paths. Added EnvelopeValidator helper to next iteration.

2026-02-25: Built streaming combinators (MapStream, WindowedBatch, FlatMapFragment) and wired a minimal retry checkpoint that records last-accepted offset and KV cache key. Local benchmark shows 12% overhead vs raw decode on 16k token traces (acceptable).
2026-03-03: Opened PR #1183 with core decoder types and combinators. Seeking design review from infra + runtime teams for checkpoint semantics.

2026-03-10: Addressed review comments: switched from implicit EOF semantics to explicit Close/Finalize on Decoder, added backpressure integration hook to stream reader. Added more exhaustive integration tests.

2026-03-12: Running e2e on dedicated capacity; discovered ordering issue when mixing envelope validation with KV cache warmup. Added a short-term workaround (serialize warmup before validation) and created follow-up for a race-free warmup API.
decisions: Adopt schema-first approach: users declare Go struct tags or a simple schema type and the helper returns Decoder[T]. This keeps generated types readable and statically typed.
Expose minimal runtime surface: Decoder[T], Stream[T], and Checkpoint. Avoid heavy code generation in the initial release to reduce maintenance burden.
Retry semantics: checkpoint records byte-offset + logical message-id. On resume, SDK asks server for resumption token; client verifies idempotency token before replaying side effects.
Backpressure: stream reader implements a bounded buffer with an explicit Request(n) API to integrate with Redwood runtime batching. This avoids unbounded memory growth in consumer code.
notes: Trade-offs considered: full codegen (fast, ergonomic) vs runtime generics (flexible, simpler rollouts). We opted for runtime generics to unblock faster and avoid adding a codegen step to CI.
Edge cases: partial JSON objects split across frames. Decoder.Finalize must be called to validate incomplete objects; combinators will surface a specific ErrPartialObject.
Follow-ups created for: docs/examples, telemetry hooks for decode failure rates, and a small runtime change to expose resumable stream tokens in the hosted API (backend change required).

This ticket covers design, a prototype library, integration tests, and an example app demonstrating structured streaming + resumable retries.

source.title: Lane escrow for throughput handover and quota reconciliation
source.creator: Priya Natarajan
source.description: Introduce a tenant lane escrow mechanism that reserves short-duration throughput credits, enables graceful handover between autoscaler modes, and provides an auditable quota reconciliation ledger to mitigate noisy neighbor effects and enforce throughput guarantees on Dedicated capacity.
source.background: Customers have requested stronger throughput guarantees on Dedicated capacity while avoiding brittle hard-slicing that wastes GPU utilization. Existing soft-quota approaches let tenants burst, but noisy neighbors still cause tail latency spikes and produce billing disputes. We need a mechanism that: 1) allows predictable short-term throughput guarantees; 2) reduces noisy neighbor interference via fast handover and preemption; 3) provides an auditable reconciliation path so billing and support can explain deviations.
source.proposed_solution: 1) Implement a Lane Escrow service that issues short-lived throughput credits (escrow tokens) to tenant requests. Credits are consumed by the serving runtime to prioritize batching and kernel placement. 2) Autoscaler cooperates with escrow by pre-allocating credits on predicted workload ramps based on a lightweight workload signature classifier. 3) Introduce a reconciliation ledger that records issuance, consumption, and forced reclaim events for post-hoc audits and chargeback. 4) Add a graceful handover flow for credit preemption: notify tenant agent, allow a configurable grace window, then apply probabilistic preemption to minimize binary failures.
design_decisions: Escrow token TTL set to 30s by default to balance responsiveness and issuance overhead
Workload signature classifier runs in control-plane every 5s; uses simple histogram features to avoid heavy ML dependencies
Preemption is probabilistic with exponential backoff to avoid synchronized eviction across tenants
Ledger is append-only and stored in the control-plane database with indexing by tenant+window for fast queries
Make enforcement opt-in per customer initially (flag: tenant_lane_escrow) to avoid disruptive changes
acceptance_criteria: Control plane exposes escrow issuance API and tenant flag toggles
Serving runtime honors escrow tokens and uses them to influence batch prioritization in >=95% of test scenarios
Autoscaler demonstrates improved 95th and 99th percentile tail latency for bursty tenants in simulation (>=20% reduction) while maintaining >=95% GPU utilization vs baseline
Reconciliation ledger contains complete issuance/consumption/reclaim records for every test traffic run and can be queried within 2s for a 24h window
Rollout plan documented and SRE runbook created for fallback and incident response
implementation_steps: Control plane: escrow API, token minting, TTL enforcement, and ledger schema
Runtime: token-aware prioritization hooks in batcher and kernel scheduler
Autoscaler: workload signature module + pre-allocation controller
Dashboard: per-tenant escrow view, credit consumption timeline, and reconciliation query UI
Integration tests: end-to-end simulation harness and noisy neighbor synthetic workloads
Canary rollout: internal tenants -> beta customers -> GA
testing_plan: Unit tests for token issuance, TTL expiry, and ledger writes
Integration tests exercising handover with different grace windows and preemption rates
Scale simulation with 500 synthetic tenants including long-tail spike patterns to measure fairness and utilization
Chaos experiments: delayed ledger writes, control-plane partition, and sudden autoscaler disconnects
Observe and validate metrics: escrow_minted, escrow_consumed, escrow_reclaimed, preemptions_count, tenant_tail_latency
rollout_plan: Phase 0: Implement feature gated behind tenant flag and run internal integration tests
Phase 1: Canary with internal infra tenants for 2 weeks, monitor latency, utilization, error rates
Phase 2: Invite 3 beta customers with different workload profiles (embeddings, chat, batching) for 4 weeks
Phase 3: Gradual enablement for new Dedicated purchases; provide opt-in for existing customers
Rollback: disable tenant flag and drain ledger writes; runtime falls back to existing soft-quota behavior
metrics: 95p_latency_change: target -20% vs baseline in burst windows
99p_latency_change: target -20% vs baseline in burst windows
gpu_utilization_delta: no more than -5% compared to baseline during steady state
false_positive_preemptions: <1% of preemptions causing client-visible failures
risks: Increased control-plane load from high-frequency token minting and ledger writes
Customer confusion about temporary preemption leading to support tickets and SLA disputes
Potential for denial-of-service if a tenant tricks classifier to get extra credits (needs rate limits)
Complexity in autoscaler coordination can introduce regressions; must be feature-gated
mitigations: Batch ledger writes and use a write-behind buffer to reduce DB pressure
Expose clear per-tenant telemetry and a 'why-preempted' reason code in logs and dashboard
Rate-limit classifier inputs and validate signatures against historical baselines
Start with opt-in customers and provide a zero-downtime rollback path
comments: 2026-03-10 Priya Natarajan: Created initial proposal after customer call with FinTech tenant that reported burst contention
2026-03-11 Marco Alvarez: Prototype runtime hooks implemented; observed ~15% tail latency improvement in microbenchmarks
2026-03-12 Sophie Chen (PM): Confirmed prioritization with billing team; reconciliation ledger required for chargeback audits
