source.title: Routing decision audit trail and per-hop telemetry enhancements
source.creator: Aisha Patel
source.description: Scope: Add machine-readable decision logs and sampled distributed traces for the smart routing layer that performs region selection, tier routing, and fallback to model variants. Provide per-hop metrics (latency, queue depth, token count) emitted at each routing hop (edge proxy -> router -> model-host) and correlate them by a trace_id and decision_id. Deliverables: 1) decision_log schema and storage plan (low-cost cold storage + short-term hot index), 2) cross-service trace propagation and instrumentation, 3) per-hop metrics with aggregation keys for route_policy, model_variant, region, tier, and customer_tier, 4) dashboards and alerting for anomalous routing patterns, and 5) sampling & redaction policy to maintain privacy and SLOs. Non-goal: full request payload retention; only metadata and short decision fingerprints retained per policy.
acceptance_criteria: Decision log schema published and approved by privacy/security
Router emits decision_log entry for 100% of routed requests in staging and at 20% sample in prod
Traces propagated across edge -> router -> host with <=5ms added tail latency in P95 measured in lab
Per-hop metrics exposed to metrics backend and dashboarded by model_variant/region/tier
Searchable index retained for 14 days and cold storage for 1 year as per storage plan
Sampling and PII redaction configurable per customer and enforced in the router
work_log: 2026-03-01: Kickoff meeting with routing, infra, and security. Agreed to use trace_id + decision_id as primary correlation keys.

2026-03-03: Drafted decision_log schema (fields, types, retention tiers) and posted to Confluence. Key fields: trace_id, decision_id, timestamp, input_fingerprint, route_policy, selected_region, selected_tier, selected_model_variant, fallback_reason, cost_estimate, predicted_latency_ms, decision_scores.
2026-03-05: Prototype trace propagation implemented in routing-service; uses existing x-rdw-trace header. Benchmarked latency impact in perf lab (P95 +1.2ms).
2026-03-08: Security review requested; added PII redaction step in router. Decided to store only input_fingerprint (sha256) for retraceability.
2026-03-10: Metrics schema for per-hop exports defined (per_hop_latency_ms, queue_depth, tokens_emitted, tokens_consumed). Added labels: route_policy, model_variant, region, tier, customer_id_hash.
2026-03-12: Integration tests added; PR opened for router changes (see links). Running small-scale canary in staging next week.
comments: Tomás Müller (2026-03-04): Review - prefer using existing tracing library to avoid duplication; confirm sampling decisions live in control-plane to avoid hot-path checks.
Ruth Chen, Security (2026-03-06): Request - ensure input_fingerprint is irreversible and document retention windows. Recommend encryption-at-rest for hot index.
Priya Nair (Product, 2026-03-09): Request - add dashboard tile to show % of requests that hit fallbacks and median added latency when fallback occurs.
Ethan Park (2026-03-11): Note - trade-off: full 100% decision logs in prod would increase storage cost by ~6x; proposing 20% sampling with triggered full-capture on anomalies (ship detection rule).
Aisha Patel (2026-03-12): Follow-up - prepared rollout plan: 1) staging smoke, 2) 5% prod sampling for one week, 3) metrics/alerts validation, 4) 20% sampling + anomaly-triggered full capture.

source.title: Separate policy gatekeeper and async lease pool to reduce routing hotpath contention
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Large portions of the smart routing hotpath still execute synchronous policy checks and lease handling under a global mutex. This ticket extracts a lightweight policy gatekeeper (read-only decision cache + observer pipeline) and an asynchronous lease pool (per-region evicting lease queue) to remove contention, reduce p99 tail latency, and make fallback decisions non-blocking.
acceptance_criteria: Policy checks on the hotpath should be reduced to a single non-blocking cache lookup + fast-path observer that never allocates per-request mutexes.
Region lease acquisition moves to an async lease pool: callers get a 'lease token' immediately and are informed via callback/await when the backend is allocated or fallback should trigger.
End-to-end p99 routing latency for small requests decreases by >= 18% in the staging cluster under realistic traffic replay.
No regression in correctness: canary traffic shows identical routing decisions compared to the baseline for 99.9% of requests; mismatches must be captured and triaged.
Migration toggle (feature flag) to flip between legacy synchronous path and new async gatekeeper for progressive rollout.
Automated integration test harness that replays regional surge scenarios and asserts no deadlocks or unbounded goroutine growth.
Documentation for the new gatekeeper/lease pool interfaces and a brief migration guide for plugins that implement custom policy adapters.
technical_notes: Design: split routing decision responsibilities - Gatekeeper (policy cache + observer emitter) and LeasePool (per-region async allocator). Gatekeeper implements an LRU TTL cache keyed by route_signature and tenant; observers subscribe to decision events for metrics and audit logs.
Implementation language: Go 1.22. Introduce an allocator interface used by the lease pool so Dedicated/Private backends can plug custom allocation logic without touching hotpath.
Concurrency model: use lock-free ring buffers for observer dispatch (github.com/ourorg/lockfree-ring) and a fixed-size worker pool for lease fulfillment. Avoid global mutex in router.Resolve().

Fallback policy: when lease acquisition is delayed beyond threshold, LeasePool returns a soft-failure token and Gatekeeper triggers fallback resolution via configured fallback adapter (region-priority or model-variant).
Telemetry: add per-stage timers (gatekeeper_lookup, lease_enqueue, lease_fulfill_latency) and counts for early-fallbacks. Ensure metrics emitted with route and tenant tags but without PII.

Dependency upgrades: bump google.golang.org/grpc to v1.56.0 for improved connection backoff and netpoll fixes; upgrade our internal metrics client to metrics-go v0.9.2 to pick up histogram improvements. Validate build CI and container image size.
Risk: observer backlog can grow under sustained high rps - mitigation: bounded ring buffers + drop strategy with 'dropped_observer' metric and opt-in durable event sink for audit-critical tenants.

Trade-offs: added complexity in async error paths; we'll implement clear invariants and end-to-end tests to minimize correctness risk. The design favors availability and latency over synchronous consistency in rare edge cases.
rollout_plan: 1) Feature branch with unit + integration tests. Open PR to core/router. Reviewers: @marcus, @elaine, @riley.
2) Deploy gatekeeper + lease pool behind feature flag in staging (20% traffic). Run routing-replay and internal chaos scenarios for 72 hours.
3) Promote to canary (5% prod) for 48 hours, validate p95/p99 and compare decision dumps to baseline. If mismatches > 0.1%, pause rollout and investigate.
4) Gradually increase to 50% then 100% over 24-48 hour windows, monitoring early-fallback and dropped_observer metrics.
5) Post-rollout: remove legacy sync path in a follow-up cleanup ticket after 2 weeks of stable metrics.
testing_notes: Create a new integration scenario in infra/replay: regional surge with 3x nominal rps and mixed short/long requests.
Add unit tests for LeasePool eviction and backpressure behavior (simulate worker starvation).
Add fuzz tests for Gatekeeper cache keys to ensure canonicalization matches legacy router and surface key collisions.
comments: 2026-02-12 Ava Chen: Spiked a prototype extracting observer dispatch to a ring buffer. Observed 12% reduction in p99 on a small synthetic load. Next step: formalize LeasePool API and add per-region worker pool.
2026-02-22 Marcus Patel: Reviewed initial API. Suggested adding a 'soft-fail' token to make fallback decisions deterministic when allocation is delayed. Also flagged telemetry gaps.

2026-03-01 Riley Gomez: Implemented LockfreeRing and basic LeasePool. Unit tests for eviction pass. Observed a minor goroutine leak in stress tests; root cause was canceled contexts not propagated to workers - fixed in PR.
2026-03-05 Elaine Park: Design review approved. Requested documentation for plugin authors and a short migration checklist (add Gatekeeper.ReadCache hook).
2026-03-09 CI: Upgraded grpc and metrics-go in the feature branch; CI flake in integration related to ephemeral port exhaustion; adding retry in test harness.

2026-03-12 Riley Gomez: Deployed to staging behind flag. Replay run shows p99 down ~19% and early_fallbacks increased by 0.03% (within expected). Observers dropped metric at 0.002% - acceptable for now. Moving to canary next.
Follow-ups: create ENG-997776 to remove legacy sync path post-stable and ENG-997777 to add durable audit sink for high-compliance tenants.

source.title: Contingency choreography for active-passive divergence and staged resynchronization
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Objective: Create and validate an operational choreography for active-passive regional divergence windows that ensures safe partial promotions, deterministic KV reconciliation, and SLO-gated rollback. Scope: multi-region L2 services (inference gateway + tokenstore), KV snapshot orchestration, circuit-breaker fencing, staged traffic siphon, and end-to-end restore verification. Success criteria: (1) ability to perform a staged promotion from passive -> active with <5% request drop and median p95 latency regression <20% vs baseline in canary slice; (2) automated snapshot checksum compare and amber/gold/green reconciliation gates pass without manual data patching; (3) documented rollback path and automated fence (circuit-breaker) that prevents split-brain for 60m; (4) drill runbook exercised twice with postmortem and checklist ticked. Out-of-scope: full cold-start recovery from expired snapshots (handled by ENG-24001).
acceptance_criteria: A runbook with clear operator commands exists and is stored in Confluence
Automated snapshot-and-checksum tool produces matching manifests before promotion
Two scheduled drills completed against staging and one against a canary region with metrics recorded
Circuit-breaker test harness can trigger progressive load-shedding and demonstrate graceful degradation with SLO gating
runbook_steps: Pre-drill: announce window, snapshot KV + index state (T0), diff against last good manifest, capture traffic profile (qps, 99th latency)
Pre-flight checks: consumer ingress quotas, reserved capacity in passive region, telemetry alerts suppressed to maintenance mode, pager rota confirmed

Phase 1 - Quarantine and Snapshot Verify: activate read-only fencing on passive region; take live incremental snapshot; compute SHA256 manifests; run checksum-compare; if mismatch -> abort and escalate
Phase 2 - Canary Promote (1% traffic): enable routing policy to siphon 1% to passive->active path; verify KV lookups consistent; monitor SLOs for 15 minutes
Phase 3 - Progressive Promotion (10/30/100): step traffic by percentage waves with 15m observation windows and automated health gates (error rate, p99 latency, tokenstore miss rate)

Phase 4 - Reconciliation Wave: if drift detected within keyset, run reconciliation job (idempotent writes with tombstone-safepoint) and verify secondary checksum; if persistent drift, engage data-sync run (manual gating)
Phase 5 - Post-promotion validation: run end-to-end sample prompts, embedding consistency checks, audit trace verification, and billing/tokens sanity
Rollback: activate region circuit-breaker, switch routes back to previous active, roll back any configuration flags, and validate consumers are back at baseline within 10 minutes
Post-mortem & artifact capture: capture snapshots, traffic traces, timeline of decisions, and a short annotated diff for the incident log
validation_checks: KV read-after-write consistency checks against golden keys
Paged trace consistency: a sample of 200 requests shows identical outputs across regions
SLO gate: error budget burn rate under 5% and p95 latency within 1.2x of baseline during canary
Circuit-breaker functional test: ability to shed 40% traffic without cascading failures
updates: 2026-02-21 - Ava C: Draft spec created and shared with SRE/Platform. Identified primary risk: KV snapshot latency under load.
2026-02-26 - Marcus R: Added checksum-compare step and proposed using immutable manifests in GCS. Assigned ENG-31721 as dependency for fencing APIs.
2026-03-02 - Priya N: Design review feedback - suggested adding a canary slice for heavy-context requests to validate KV cache warmup behavior.
2026-03-04 - Marcus R: Implemented the canary traffic siphon in the routing layer; integration tests passing locally (see PR #5819).
2026-03-07 - Security review (Liam O'Connor): confirmed KMS envelope usage for snapshot manifests; noted retention policy review required.

2026-03-10 - SRE Ops: Dry-run in staging executed. Observations: snapshot compute spiked by 18% causing delayed canary start. Mitigation: throttle snapshot job and prefetch KV partitions. Follow-up: schedule probe window to prime cache 10m before promotion.

2026-03-11 - Marcus R: Updated runbook to include cache-priming step and added automated alarm to abort if snapshot duration > 2x baseline. Next: schedule first full drill against staging (2026-03-18).
risks_and_mitigations: Risk: Split-brain if network partition resolves asymmetrically. Mitigation: strict fencing via circuit-breaker TTL and operator-confirmed quiesce before promotion.
Risk: Snapshot job overloads control-plane. Mitigation: stagger snapshots by partition and implement backpressure and soft-deadline aborts.
Risk: Data drift requiring manual patch. Mitigation: build reconciliation tooling that applies idempotent compensating writes and tracks LSNs for audit
next_steps: Schedule two drills: staging (2026-03-18) and canary-region dry-run (2026-03-25)
Finish PR #5819 kernel: add snapshot manifest metadata and checksum endpoint
Ops to rehearse rollback path and validate circuit-breaker TTL behavior under simulated partitions
Create short-run training doc for oncall with the 9 operator commands and a decision tree

source.title: Schematic Stability Guardian: Stream-safe constrained-decoder + graceful schema-fallback test harness
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: Streaming model outputs occasionally violate strict schemas in live traffic (partial JSON fragments, trailing tokens, or tool-call markers inserted mid-object). Current validators either fail the request (causing user-visible errors) or run expensive post-processing that increases latency. We need a test harness and decoder shim that validates constrained decoding behavior under streaming conditions and exercises graceful fallback logic used by the platform when strict schema adherence cannot be asserted mid-stream.

Goal: Build a reproducible test harness (unit + integration + synthetic traffic) and an engineering prototype 'Schematic Stability Guardian' - a stream-safe constrained-decoder adapter that: (1) enforces constrained sampling where possible, (2) detects and classifies schema violations in-flight, and (3) triggers deterministic fallback behaviors (best-effort repair, model variant swap, or safe-structured-plaintext) according to policy. The ticket covers test harness design, baseline experiments, the shim prototype, and automation to run regression suites across model variants and sequence lengths.
source.background: We've seen intermittent schema drift in production long-tail prompts and when tool calling interleaves with structured outputs. Existing tickets focus on decoder constraints and schema fuzzing, but fewer tests cover streaming partial-chunk scenarios and how fallback policies interact with routing and caching. This ticket unifies those gaps into a single orchestrated CI experiment and a small runtime shim to exercise fallback policies deterministically.
source.scope: In scope: streaming partial-output fuzz scenarios, constrained-decoder parameter sweep (top-k/top-p/temperature), sentinel insertion heuristics, policy evaluation for fallback behaviors, CI job that runs nightly across curated prompts and model variants. Out of scope: full production rollout of the shim; extensive UI/Console work (separate EPIC).
source.proposal: 1) Design a test harness that can replay streaming outputs at various chunk sizes and interleave simulated tool calls. 2) Implement a 'stream-safe' adapter that monitors token stream, keeps a lightweight incremental parse state for expected schema, and flags violations. 3) Define deterministic fallback actions: a) attempt incremental repair (closing braces, re-requesting tail tokens via short re-roll), b) fall back to a schema-tolerant model variant, or c) emit a structured error with safe-plain fallback payload. 4) Run a matrix of experiments measuring latency, successful-parse-rate, and repair rate across models and decoder settings.
source.tradeoffs:
- Incremental parsing increases CPU overhead per stream; we must benchmark to keep latency cost within SLOs.
- Aggressive repair heuristics can mask real model failures and can produce incorrect semantics if not conservative.
- Fallback to tolerant model variants may increase cost and slightly degrade output fidelity.
- Longer tests increase CI time; we will prioritize representative prompt families for nightly runs and run the full matrix weekly.
implementation_plan: Design harness: define prompt corpus, chunk-size schedule, and interleaved tool-call scenarios (owner: Diego)
Prototype stream-safe adapter: incremental parser + rules engine (owner: Diego)
Build constrained-decoder parameter sweep runner (owner: SRE team, contact: Lina Patel)
Integration tests: run adapter + decoder sweep against open models and Redwood-verified variants (owner: QA)
Cost & latency benchmarks: measure overhead on dedicated GPU nodes and hosted API (owner: infra measurement)
CI automation: nightly job + regression alerting to Slack channel #structured-output-tests (owner: CI)
Docs & runbook: write operator-facing runbook for fallback policies and post-incident steps (owner: Diego)
Postmortem & decision: capture findings and recommend rollout strategy (owner: Ava)
source.testing_plan:
- Unit tests for the incremental parser with edge cases (truncated arrays, nested objects split across chunks).
- Property tests that mutate boundaries and insert tool-call tokens mid-JSON.
- E2E integration that verifies the chosen fallback policy triggers and produces expected outputs (repair / variant-swap / safe-plain).
- Performance tests measuring added CPU and tail latency at 5p95 and p99.
- Run against subset of production prompts anonymized in partnership with privacy team.
acceptance_criteria: Nightly harness runs execute and report pass/fail (no regressions) for chosen prompt corpus
Prototype adapter correctly classifies violation type and triggers configured fallback in >=95% deterministic cases for synthetic violations
End-to-end latency overhead introduced by the adapter is <=10ms at p95 on dedicated test nodes (configurable threshold)
Runbook with clear operator actions and error classification is published and reviewed by SRE and Security
Metrics dashboard tracking parse success rate, repair attempts, fallback rates, and cost delta is live in Grafana
source.metrics:
- parse_success_rate (per-prompt family)
- repair_attempt_rate
- repair_success_rate
- fallback_trigger_rate
- additional_cpu_ms_per_stream
- p95/p99 latency delta
- cost_delta_per_1M_tokens
source.rollout_plan: Phase 0: internal-only tests in CI and dedicated infra. Phase 1: shadow mode on small percentage of hosted-API traffic (no user-visible fallback). Phase 2: enable conservative fallback policies for PW-flagged customers and Dedicated clusters. Phase 3: broader rollout after 2 weeks of no regression signals.
comments: 2026-03-02 - Ava Chen: Kickoff meeting notes: agreed on prioritized prompt families: billing/contact forms, webhook tool-calls, and multi-step instructions. Lina to provide infra baseline metrics by 2026-03-05.

2026-03-04 - Diego Morales: Prototype adapter implemented a small incremental JSON state machine. Early results: detects truncated arrays 92% of the time on synthetic truncation. Need to tune for nested objects. Opening draft PR.
2026-03-06 - Review from Lina Patel: flagged CPU overhead concern when concurrent streams > 120 per GPU. Suggested sampling approach for hosted API (shadow 1%). Added to the plan.

2026-03-08 - Security review: noted that anonymized production prompt corpus must be validated. Security requested confirmation that repair heuristics never submit extra tokens to external tools; awaiting signoff.
2026-03-10 - QA: Integration tests passing on local runners. Nightly CI job skeleton established (job: nightly-schema-guard). Next: add Grafana panels and alerting thresholds.
source.notes: This ticket intentionally favors a conservative repair-first policy in prototypes. If experiments show high false-repair rates, we'll prefer deterministic variant-swap with explicit operator visibility. Follow-up tickets will split runtime integration into a small shim library vs. a deeper runtime kernel change depending on perf tradeoffs.

title: Schematic Stability Guardian: Stream-safe constrained-decoder + graceful schema-fallback test harness
preview:
Problem: Streaming model outputs occasionally violate strict schemas in live traffic (partial JSON fragments, trailing tokens, or tool-call markers inserted mid-object). Current validators either fail the request (causing user-visible errors) or run expensive post-processing that increases latency. We need a test harness and decoder shim that validates constrained decoding behavior under streaming conditions and exercises graceful fallback logic used by the platform when strict schema adherence cannot be asserted mid-stream.

Goal: Build a reproducible test harness (unit + integration + synthetic traffic) and an engineering prototype 'Schematic Stability Guardian' - a stream-safe constrained-decoder adapter that: (1) enforces constrained sampling where possible, (2) detects and classifies schema violations in-flight, and (3) triggers deterministic fallback behaviors (best-effort repair, model variant swap, or safe-structured-plaintext) according to policy. The ticket covers test harness design, baseline experiments, the shim prototype, and automation to run regression suites across model variants and sequence lengths.

source.title: Automated Health Gate: telemetry-driven circuit thresholds for Private VPC upgrades
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Purpose: Add an automated, telemetry-driven health gate to our Terraform deployment pipeline for Private/VPC upgrades. The gate evaluates short-window SLOs (latency, error-rate, saturation, token throughput) and enforces an automated rollback or traffic quarantine when degradation exceeds configured thresholds. This is an infra-level safety harness to make upgrades (runtime, kernel, model variant changes) across private clusters predictable and reversible without manual intervention.

Scope: Build a reusable Terraform module (redwood/vpc-health-gate) and integration patterns for: (1) pre-upgrade probes and dry-run checks, (2) continuous post-deploy evaluation windows (1m/5m/15m), (3) policy-driven automated rollback or traffic quarantining, (4) observability dashboard and runbook links in PRs, and (5) integration with Dedicated/Private routing policies for graceful fallback to compatible model variants.

Non-goals: This ticket does not rework kernel selection logic or implement new model-level quality regression checks (those are separate eval pipelines). It also does not modify customer-facing routing decisions beyond the controlled canary/quarantine workflow described below.
acceptance_criteria: Terraform module redwood/vpc-health-gate published in infra-modules with README and usage example
Module exposes configurable SLO windows and thresholds for: request_latency_p95_ms, error_rate_5m, token_throughput_per_gpu, cpu_util_percent
Upgrade pipeline (concourse/ci) calls the module pre-upgrade to register probes and post-upgrade to evaluate 1m/5m windows
Automated rollback triggers when two or more SLO signals breach configurable severity within the same evaluation window
Playbook and console dashboard links included in the PR template and in rollout summary events
End-to-end test harness validates both success and rollback paths using traffic shadowing in staging
steps: Design Terraform module interface: inputs for thresholds, evaluation windows, alert severities, rollback actions, and IAM role bindings
Implement module resources: CloudWatch/Prometheus alert rules, SLO policy CRD, and a small controller webhook that reads policy state and emits rollout actions
Add lifecycle hooks to existing vpc upgrade pipeline: pre-check (synthetic), deploy, post-check windows, finalization or rollback
Create a lightweight ‘health-gate’ controller (single binary) to run as a job in the control plane to evaluate metrics windows and call the orchestration API
Wire module outputs to console instrumentation: include links to dashboards and last-evaluation snapshots
Author unit/integration tests: terraform plan validations, controller e2e with simulated metric streams
Coordinate with security to review IAM scopes and KMS usage for any webhook secrets
Roll out to staging, run two staged upgrades to validate both pass and rollback behaviors, then schedule canary to production
comments: 2026-02-16 Diego Morales: Initial design doc uploaded; recommending a conservative default policy: require two independent signals before rollback to avoid flapping. See attachment.

2026-02-20 Priya Kapoor (PM): Customer-facing requirement: provide a 'quarantine' action instead of immediate rollback for Dedicated customers who prefer degraded but available service. Added as a configurable action in the module.

2026-02-25 Design review: Ava Chen: prefer thresholds as percentile and absolute values (e.g., p95>250ms AND p50>120ms) to detect both latency spikes and median shifts. Also add token_throughput per GPU lower-bound check to detect saturation.

2026-02-28 Engineering sync: implement health-gate controller as opt-in (flag in module) so customers with strict compliance can disable automated rollbacks and require manual approval. Diego to add feature flag and document behavior.

2026-03-03 QA: Found flapping in simulated network jitter tests — increased evaluation window to 2m for the primary trigger and require sustained breach over two consecutive windows before action. Added exponential backoff suppression for repeated rollbacks.

2026-03-07 Security review: requested KMS-wrapped webhook tokens and least-privilege IAM for the controller. Diego updated module to produce an IAM role and KMS alias. Security approved on 2026-03-09.

2026-03-10 Diego Morales: Implementation branch created and PR opened. e2e staging harness passing for success path; rollback path needs one more synthetic test for token-throughput saturation scenario. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra/pull/4821

source.title: Staged heterogeneous GPU dependency upgrade with runtime ABI adapters and graceful fallbacks
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Background: Our GPU-serving fleet runs a mix of CUDA versions, Triton builds, and multiple CUTLASS profiles across cloud and on-prem nodes. Past upgrades caused a spike in cold-start latency and two incidents where symbol resolution failures triggered partial node evictions. Goal: Implement a staged upgrade orchestration that (1) ships lightweight ABI adapter shims instead of inplace library swaps, (2) provides runtime probing and per-node capability stenciling, (3) exposes an operator-controlled canary + automated rollback policy, and (4) adds end-to-end telemetry and unit/regression tests to prevent regressions. Scope: runtime loader changes, new signed shim artifacts, CI performance benchmarks, canary pipeline in deployer, and updated rollout docs. Acceptance criteria: canary rollout to 5% of prod fleet without >5% increase in tail latency, zero symbol resolution errors in canary, automated rollback triggers on metric breaches, and successful upgrade runbook completed. Out of scope: full CUTLASS recompile of all models (handled separately). Risks: shim regressions, increased memory overhead on older nodes, and SLO blips during initial canary. Mitigations: tight regression thresholds, lower-memory shim variants, and staged traffic ramping.
comments: 2025-11-05 - Kickoff: Alignment meeting with infra, runtime, and QE. Decided to prefer adapter shims over aggressive in-place library replacements. Action: Diego to prototype shim loader by 2025-11-20.
2025-12-02 - Prototype check-in: initial shim loader (abi-adapter-v1) submitted. Observed 1.8ms average extra startup time on p3 nodes; memory overhead ~14MB. Added microbench harness: GH #5821.

2026-01-14 - QE regression tests: small but consistent 2% tail-latency uplift with abi-adapter-v1 on long-sequence workloads. Trade-off discussion: accept small uplift for stability; iterate on warmup heuristics to recover tail.

2026-01-28 - Design update: introduce dual-path loader (fastpath: existing symbols; adapterpath: shim-resolve) and per-host capability stamp in /var/lib/redwood/gpu-cap.json. Design doc: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENG/Heterogeneous+GPU+Upgrade
2026-02-11 - PRs: runtime loader + capability stenciler landed in feature branch. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/5821. Requesting infra review for canary rollout orchestration.

2026-02-25 - Canary plan: stage A (internal test cluster), stage B (5% prod non-critical customers), metrics to monitor: 99th-per-token-latency, kernel-symbol-errors, eviction-rate, memory-usage-per-proc. Dashboard: https://console.redwood.ai/dashboards/gpu-init-latency

2026-03-03 - Canary 5% started. Early telemetry: no symbol errors, 3% increase in 99th-per-token-latency for large-context models on older instance types. Triggered warmup-offload experiment (move larger kernels to prelinked hotpool) which recovered ~60% of regression.

2026-03-09 - Post-canary retrospective: canary met stability thresholds after warmup-offload. Remaining tasks: sign shim artifacts in CI, add signed checksum verification in loader, document operator rollback commands, and add k8s rollout job with automatic traffic-shift rollback rule. Plan to promote to 20% on 2026-03-15 if no regressions.

2026-03-10 - Action items assigned: (1) Diego - implement signed shim verification (ETA 2026-03-18), (2) Priya Rao - add automated rollback runbook to ops playbooks (ETA 2026-03-14), (3) QE - extend perf tests to mixed-vnode scenarios (ETA 2026-03-20).

source.title: VPC Observability Terraform module: Prometheus/Grafana/OTel + log forwarder, upgrade & healthcheck plan
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Scope: Build a reusable Terraform module for VPC/private deployments that provisions a minimal observability stack opinionated for Redwood Private customers: Prometheus (federation-compatible), Grafana (provisioned dashboards + org config), OpenTelemetry Collector (receiver/processor/exporter pipeline), and a log-forwarder (Vector/Fluent Bit) that reliably ships container logs to our central logging tier or customer-managed endpoints.

Goals:
- Single Terraform module with clear inputs/outputs so tenants can opt in to managed observability when deploying Private.
- Support upgrade paths for collector, Prometheus, and Grafana without data loss or long downtime.
- Built-in healthchecks and self-healing signals (K8s probes, Prometheus rules, Grafana datasource tests) surfaced in Console.
- Small TF surface for platform teams: sensible defaults + override hooks.

Constraints & decisions:
- Target K8s deployment model for private package (helm_release + k8s resources). We will not attempt to build a non-K8s variant in this iteration.
- Prefer OpenTelemetry Collector as the canonical telemetry gateway; allow pluggable exporters via module variable.
- Use Prometheus in a single-instance HA pattern (ReplicaCount + PersistentVolume) with optional federation block for hosted ingest.
- Grafana will be deployed with provisioning files (dashboards, datasources) baked into the module; admin password stored in KMS-backed secret.

Terraform interface (examples):
Inputs (selected):
- name_prefix (string, required)
- namespace (string, default: "redwood-observability")
- enable_prometheus (bool, default: true)
- prometheus_retention_days (number, default: 7)
- prometheus_storage_size (string, e.g., "50Gi")
- enable_grafana (bool, default: true)
- grafana_provisioned_dashboards (list)
- otel_config_map (string, optional: custom collector config)
- log_forwarder_type (string, values: ["vector","fluentbit"], default: "vector")
- shipping_endpoint (string, optional: customer or Redwood ingest URL)
- healthcheck_alerts_enabled (bool, default: true)

Outputs (selected):
- grafana_url
- prometheus_url
- otel_collector_service
- log_forwarder_daemonset_name

Upgrade strategy summary:
1) Schema-safe Terraform changes only (additive inputs/outputs) in minor releases.
2) Collector upgrades: perform a canary upgrade to a single node/replica, validate telemetry flow and traces, then roll out cluster-wide.
3) Prometheus upgrades: use Thanos or remote-read-compatible approach for large upgrades if needed; for minor patch upgrades we will roll pod-by-pod respecting scrape and WAL thresholds and waiting for compaction.
4) Grafana upgrades: use blue/green if major version bump; minimize dashboard reprovisioning during patch upgrades.

Healthchecks and observability signals (what we monitor):
- Collector: heartbeat metric from collector exporting to our ingest, exporter success rate, queue size, receiver lag. Alert if exporter failures > 1% over 5m.
- Prometheus: target scrape success rate, TSDB head block growth, WAL corruption metrics, persistent volume free space. Alert on scrape success < 95% for 10m.
- Grafana: datasource health check, provisioning error logs, alert if frontend 5xx rate > 1% for 5m.
- Log forwarder: shipping success rate, backpressure/oom events, file rotation errors.

Deliverables:
- Terraform module in repo: infra/modules/observability-vpc
- Helm charts or upstream chart references + K8s manifests generated by module
- CI jobs (plan/apply gating, module acceptance tests)
- Runbook for upgrades + rollback checklist
- Prometheus alert rules and Grafana dashboards provisioned

Implementation notes and example usage are below in technical_notes and test_plan sections.
acceptance_criteria: Module provisions Prometheus, Grafana, OTel Collector, and log forwarder in a test VPC with default inputs without additional manual steps
Grafana URL and Prometheus URL are exposed via module outputs and validated by an automated smoke test
Collector upgrade canary flow completes: new collector version receives traces/logs for canary pod and no dropped spans/errors are observed
Healthcheck alerts fire in staging when we simulate failures (e.g., block disk write to Prometheus PVC, stop collector exporter) and clear after remediation
Documentation and runbook covering upgrade and rollback steps checked into infra/docs and linked from module README
technical_notes: Use helm_release resource to manage upstream Prometheus/Grafana charts and override values via chart_values var.
Provision Prometheus with persistent volumes and set retention to a conservative default; expose remote_write hooks to allow customers to configure remote ingest.
OTel Collector config will be templated with module variables; support hot-swap exporter configuration via Kubernetes ConfigMap update with readiness gating.
Log forwarder: default to Vector for richer transforms; allow switching to Fluent Bit for minimal footprints via variable. Use node-level daemonset for log collection.
Add tf validate/tfsec checks in module CI; include a terratest suite to assert K8s resources and simple scrape/export behavior.
Plan for backward-compatible outputs: never remove an output without deprecation cycle and migration guide.
test_plan: Create a throwaway cluster in CI (K8s v1.26) and run `terraform apply` with module defaults; run smoke tests to hit /metrics and Grafana login
Simulate failure scenarios: kill collector exporter, fill Prometheus PVC to 95% and assert alerts in Alertmanager or Console, stop log-forwarder daemonset and assert log backlog metrics
Run upgrade test: deploy collector vX, upgrade to vX+1 in canary, validate telemetry continuity for 15 minutes, then roll cluster-wide
E2E: ingest a sample trace from a sample app and assert it appears in traces view (otel -> exported path) and in metrics via instrumentation.
rollout_plan: Phase 1 (staging): land module in infra/modules and run full acceptance tests in staging VPC
Phase 2 (pilot): enable module for 2 private customers with limited retention and increased monitoring; run manual upgrade and monitor for 72h
Phase 3 (general availability): enable `enable_observability` flag in private deploy templates and publish module registry entry
Rollback strategy: restore previous helm_release revision for Grafana/Prometheus; revert collector image to previous tag; if PVC corruption suspected, restore from snapshot following runbook.
comments: 2026-03-01 - Ava Chen: Created ticket to unify VPC observability module. Initial scope and goals outlined. Assigned to Luca.
2026-03-03 - Luca Martinez: Drafted Terraform inputs/outputs and recommended helm-based approach for Prometheus/Grafana. Opted for Vector as default log forwarder due to transformation support.

2026-03-04 - Priya Kapoor (SRE): Suggested adding explicit Prometheus WAL and retention checks and an automated snapshot step before major upgrades. Added as dependency for backup automation (ENG-12999).

2026-03-06 - Security (Mateo Ruiz): Requested KMS-based secret provisioning for Grafana admin credentials and review of outbound egress when shipping logs to hosted ingest. Marked security_review_required.
2026-03-08 - Luca Martinez: Added upgrade canary flow and test plan; created initial CI acceptance job in infra/ci/modules-observability.yml (WIP).
2026-03-10 - Ava Chen: Reviewed acceptance criteria and requested documentation checklist be added to README. Updated due_date to 2026-04-08.

source.title: Temporal reservoir batcher for skewed turn lengths and aggressive KV compaction
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem statement:
Many production workloads contain a heavy skew between short prompts (1-2 turns) and long multi-turn dialogues. The current continuous-batching scheduler aggregates by arrival time and length buckets, but we observe repeated KV fragmentation and suboptimal GPU slab utilization when a small number of long requests intermingle with high-volume short requests. This leads to transient OOMs, higher p50/p95 latency for long responses, and degraded token-per-dollar efficiency.

Proposal summary:
Introduce a Temporal Reservoir Batcher (TRB) that maintains short-term reservoirs per-GPU weighted by observed turn-length histograms and recent KV residency. The TRB will: 1) allocate a soft-reservation of KV slots for long-turn pipelines (reservoir windows) based on tail-frequency forecasts; 2) perform opportunistic prefetching with a stride-aware policy to pull KV shards for imminent long responses; 3) use an aggressive background KV compaction pass (slab-level compaction) during low-utilization windows to reduce fragmentation; 4) extend the scheduler's admission policy to allow controlled preemption of micro-batches to free contiguous slab space for long-turn allocations. The design targets preserving short-request latency while reducing long-request tail latency and overall GPU memory waste.

Key innovations compared to current designs:
- Temporal reservoir sizing derived from short-term exponential smoothing of per-route length histograms (not static length buckets).
- Stride-aware prefetch: prefetching focuses on contiguous KV ranges expected for a run, minimizing scattered allocations and cache misses.
- Background slab compaction tuned to be I/O- and kernel-friendly (run at configurable low CPU/GPU priority, triggered by slab fragmentation metrics).
- Soft preemption policy where short micro-batches can be coalesced or deferred by at most X ms to accommodate a long-turn allocation when it would reduce total OOM events.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Increased scheduler complexity could regress short-request p50 latency. Mitigation: conservative default reservoir sizes, canary rollout on a subset of GPUs, and circuit-breaker to disable preemption if p50 increases by >5%.
- Risk: Compaction overhead causing GPU stalls. Mitigation: compaction only runs in low-utilization time slices and yields to inference kernels; tune compaction chunk size and throttle.
- Risk: Forecast misprediction causing wasted reservations. Mitigation: use decay on reservoirs and immediate reclamation path after TTL elapses.

Acceptance criteria:
- End-to-end improvement: p95 latency for long-turn responses reduced by at least 15% in our synthetic mixed workload benchmark, with p50 for short requests degraded by no more than 3%.
- GPU memory fragmentation metric (percent fragmented slabs) reduced by at least 20% during steady-state mixed workloads.
- No increase in OOM incident rate in canary cluster; stable or reduced token-per-dollar cost for mixed workloads.
- Feature gated behind runtime-1.20 flag and can be disabled dynamically via routing policies.

Implementation notes:
- Scheduler changes live in scheduler/tactical_reservoir.cc and scheduler/reservoir_policy.h.
- KV compaction implemented as a background task in kv/compactor.cc with per-slab compaction state and throttles exposed as flags.
- New telemetry: slab_fragmentation_ratio, reservoir_utilization, prefetch_hit_rate, preemption_count. Expose on Console under per-model memory panel.
- Config knobs: reservoir_ttl_ms, reservoir_soft_fraction (percent of GPU slab), prefetch_stride_hint, compaction_chunk_mb, compaction_throttle_pct.

Rollout plan:
- Phase 0 (dev): unit tests + integration tests on dev cluster using synthetic skewed-dialogue generator.
- Phase 1 (canary): enable TRB on 5% of GPUs in staging; traffic from two internal customers that represent heavy skew. Monitor metrics for 48-72 hours.
- Phase 2 (limited prod): ramp to 20% of production GPUs for 7 days; run canary checks daily; enable circuit-breaker thresholds.
- Phase 3 (full): default enabled for runtime-1.20 with opt-out route policy, and documentation for tuning knobs.

Observability & dashboards:
- Add a "Reservoir" tab under Console -> Model -> Memory that shows reservoir size, utilization, reclamation events, and compaction activity by GPU.
- Add anomaly alerts: reservoir_utilization > 90% for 10m, compaction_throttle > 70% for 5m, preemption_count spike for route.

Dependencies and related work:
- Relies on slab-level compaction primitives from ENG-42109 and improved histogram telemetry from ENG-142580.
- Interacts with batch admission flow refactor in ENG-612345 (coordinate preemption API).
acceptance_criteria: p95 reduction >= 15% for long-turns on synthetic benchmark
short-request p50 degradation <= 3%
fragmentation metric improvement >= 20%
no increase in OOM rate during canary
feature gated and dynamically disable-able
implementation_steps: Prototype reservoir allocator and prefetch policy in scheduler unit tests
Implement slab compaction background worker with throttle controls
Expose telemetry and dashboard tiles for reservoir and compaction metrics
Integrate preemption hook with admission controller and add circuit-breaker flags
Run integrated e2e tests on synthetic and recorded production traces
Canary rollout and iterate on knobs
Document tuning guidance and add Console controls
test_plan: Unit tests for reservoir sizing and prefetch stride calculation
Simulated mixed workload (short:long ratio 1000:1, 10k qps) in perf cluster
Record-and-replay test using customer A traces to validate prefetch correctness
Fuzz tests that inject very large contiguous KV ranges to validate compaction stability
Failure injection: force compaction to run during 90% utilization window and verify throttle behavior
monitoring_and_alerts: Create Console alert: preemption_rate spike (baseline+3 sigma) for a route
Daily canary healthcheck report during rollout
comments: 2026-02-12 Marco Ruiz: Spike complete. Basic reservoir works in unit tests; prefetch stride reduces scattered KV allocations in synthetic run.
2026-02-20 Priya Nair (PM): Prioritize canary with Customer A and Customer B traffic; add telemetry for prefetch_hit_rate.
2026-02-28 Design review (Luca Moretti): Suggest reducing compaction chunk size to avoid long GPU kernel stalls; added note to implementation.
2026-03-03 Performance: first staging run shows p95 long-turn down 18% but short p50 up 1.7% — within target. Will proceed to limited prod after safety checks.
2026-03-08 Security: reviewed - no secrets involved. Confirmed gating and RBAC for compaction controls (ops-only).
2026-03-10 Marco Ruiz: Starting canary rollout on 5% of GPUs. Monitoring hooks pushed in PR #8342.
TODO: follow-up ticket to surface recommended knob defaults per model family (ENG-276445)

source.title: Tool parameter spec style guide and runtime recovery patterns
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Purpose: define a single, pragmatic JSON parameter specification and a set of runtime compatibility + recovery behaviors for function/tool calling. Scope: param schema style (shape conventions, primitive coercions, optional vs required), three validation modes (strict/lenient/coerce), canonical error payloads and mappings, and a lightweight shim strategy for older clients and model variants. Non-goals: full JSON Schema v2020 implementation and client-side SDK changes beyond minimal helpers.
acceptance_criteria: A machine-readable parameter format is documented and approved by API and runtime teams
Runtime accepts requests under three validation levels with deterministic outcomes
Error payloads use canonical fields (code, message, path, suggestion) and are stable for SDKs
Compatibility shim transforms at most two forms (snake_case <-> camelCase, numeric-string coercion) and logs transform events to Console
design_decisions: Use a small, opinionated subset of JSON Schema: types (string, number, integer, boolean, object, array), enum, properties, required, and format hints (email, uri, iso8601). Avoid complex keywords (oneOf/anyOf/allOf) at first rollout
Declare a schema version field top-level: '__param_spec_version' with semantic versioning for the spec (initial value '1.0')
Introduce three validation modes: 'strict' (reject on mismatch), 'lenient' (reject only for missing required fields and gross type errors), 'coerce' (attempt safe coercions then validate)
Default for hosted API is 'coerce' for first 90 days post-RTM to minimize breakages, then move default to 'lenient' unless customer opts into 'strict'
Runtime returns a stable error envelope and numeric internal codes (1000-1999 tool validation) so SDKs can programmatically map behaviors
validation_levels: strict: exact type and shape match required; any deviation => 400 with code 1001
lenient: missing non-required props tolerated; minor type mismatches (e.g., int vs float) tolerated; unknown props preserved under 'extra_args'
coerce: runtime will attempt safe conversions (string->number if numeric string, 'true'/'false'->boolean, numeric->string) and then validate; logs coercions
canonical_error_payload:
Example payload (runtime returns HTTP 400/422 based on error): {
  "code": 1001,
  "http_status": 400,
  "message": "Invalid parameter: 'temperature' must be number between 0.0 and 1.0",
  "path": "/params/temperature",
  "suggestion": "Cast string '0.8' to number or send a numeric literal"
}
Field semantics: code: internal numeric id; http_status: numeric HTTP; message: short human text; path: JSON Pointer-ish string showing param path; suggestion: actionable remediation text
error_codes: 1100: compatibility shim applied (informational, runtime returns 200 but logs 1100)
runtime_recovery_flow: Step 1: Validate incoming payload against declared param spec version and requested validation_level
Step 2: If validation_level == 'coerce', attempt conversions and record events for each coerced field
Step 3: If validation fails and mode == 'lenient', strip unknown optional fields into 'extra_args' and continue if required fields present
Step 4: If mode == 'strict' or required fields are missing, return canonical error payload and halt execution

Step 5: When an in-flight fallback occurs (e.g., model variant doesn't support a param), attempt configured fallbacks in order: alternate param mapping -> default value -> model fallback -> runtime-level error
compatibility_shims: Case normalization: auto-convert snake_case and kebab-case keys to camelCase for historical clients when 'compatibility' header present
Numeric coercion: accept numeric strings for number/integer fields under 'coerce' mode (log event with code 1100)
Boolean coercion: accept 'true'/'false' strings and 0/1 for boolean fields when mode permits
Unknown keys: move into top-level 'extra_args' object so the tool handler can access legacy fields without runtime failure
Schema downgrades: if client param spec version < runtime supported version, apply a downgrade transform table (maintain small, explicit transforms per minor version)
migration_notes: Notify SDKs: publish migration guide with examples for coercion and strictness changes two weeks before switching default validation mode
Telemetry: emit metrics for coercion rates, shim usage, and error-class frequencies to identify upgrade friction
Rollout plan: enable strict validation as opt-in per-customer, then rollout to new API keys after 60-day opt-in window
Backfill: implement a console dashboard to show top 20 shimmed fields and how often they triggered for each customer
comments: 2025-11-06 - Ava Chen: Initial draft, proposed three-mode approach to balance reliability with backward compatibility.
2025-12-01 - Design reviewed: Amanda Ruiz: prefer human-readable 'suggestion' field to help Product with error messaging UX.
2026-01-14 - Runtime: Julian Park: implemented a prototype coercion pass; observed 12% of requests required at least one coercion in staging.
2026-02-20 - PM review: Kemi Adeyemi: requested explicit guidance on telemetry and customer communication; added migration notes.
2026-03-10 - Security: Priya Nair: approved, asked to ensure transforms do not leak sensitive keys; added checks to strip 'password' and 'secret' fields before logging.

source.title: Route consistency window during peering promote causes transient resolver-binding drift
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Summary: During a peering promote (when a secondary peering session is promoted to primary to balance load or recover capacity), some Dedicated tenant routes can appear to be consistent at the control plane level while dataplane bindings (egress resolver affinity and next-hop for SNAT) lag by a small window (1-30s). The lag can cause tenant traffic to be forwarded to an egress path that no longer has the correct resolver mapping, producing transient DNS misresolution and connection resets for long-lived client sessions. Several customer escalations reported 5-30s spikes of 500-level proxy errors and DNS NXDOMAINs coincident with peering promotions during scheduled maintenance or autoscaler-driven reparent events.
symptoms: Short-lived spikes of DNS NXDOMAIN or incorrect A records for tenant hostnames
Client TLS handshakes failing mid-session (shortly after peering change)
Increased 5xx rate on routes served by dedicated nodes during peering promotions
Resolver affinity in control plane still points to old egress after route table has been updated
repro_steps: 1) Create a Dedicated tenant with active VPC peering to an external customer VPC and long-lived client connections (keepalive) to Redwood-hosted endpoints.
2) Trigger a peering promote: simulate by failing the current primary peering session or running the promote workflow used for capacity rebalancing.
3) Observe control plane route/peering state flips within seconds but dataplane resolver bindings (SNAT mapping, egress selection) persist for an extra 1-30s window.
4) Capture packet traces on egress nodes and resolver processes to see stale resolver IPs returned to tenants while route next-hops have already changed.
source.root_cause_hypothesis: Race between peering promote workflow that atomically updates route tables and a separate async reconciliation process that updates resolver affinity and SNAT allocation. The reconciliation uses tenant-level caches with TTLs and backoff that were tuned for lookups during normal churn, not an order-of-ops where the route table flips first. Hence the dataplane keeps forwarding based on stale cached resolver entries until the cache expiry/reconciliation runs.
mitigations: 1) Hotfix: add a short blocking reconciliation step in the peering promote path to synchronously flush and rebind resolver affinity for affected tenant prefixes before the peering promote is declared complete.
2) Short-term: increase monitoring alerts to detect resolver/control-plane divergence and auto-page on >5s divergence for any tenant during peering events.

3) Customer communication: notify impacted customers of the root cause and the planned maintenance window for hotfix rollout; provide mitigation steps (retries, shortened keepalive) while we roll the fix.

4) Rollback plan: the hotfix is additive; if issues appear we will revert the blocking step and instead roll a targeted config change to increase reconciliation frequency (but that may increase load).
source.proposed_fix: Implement a peering-promote hook that performs a deterministic dataplane flush: (a) mark affected tenant prefixes as 'in-transition', (b) invalidate resolver affinity and SNAT assignments for those prefixes, (c) force immediate reconciliation of bindings on peering target nodes, then (d) complete promote. Add tracing tags so we can correlate promote events with per-tenant resolver versions.
implementation_notes: - Modify peering controller codepath (control plane) to call egress-binding-service.invalidate(prefixes[]) synchronously.
- Ensure invalidate is implemented as idempotent and returns only after the local and peer caches confirm new bindings.
- Add unit and integration tests that simulate promote and verify no stale resolver responses are returned post-promote.
- Limit scope to Dedicated peering promotes for now; do not change Hosted peering behavior until validated.
- Performance: measure latency added by the synchronous step and add a non-blocking fallback if exceed threshold (with explicit alerting).
rollout_plan: 1) Create a feature-flagged rollout in runtime-1.22 branch. Default: off.
2) Deploy to staging and run reproduction test harness (automated promote every 30s with load) for 48h.
3) Canary: enable flag for 5% of Dedicated tenants (low-risk tenants) for 24h, monitor resolver/control-plane divergence, 5xx rates, and CPU impact.
4) Expand to 25% for 24h, then 100% if no regressions.
5) After 100% rollout, backfill additional tracing and set an SLA alert to detect similar regressions for 30d.
6) Notify support and CS teams before each expansion wave; provide rollback steps.
monitoring_and_alerts: - Add metric: resolver_binding_version_delta (control_plane_version - dataplane_version) aggregated per tenant
- Alert: page if resolver_binding_version_delta > 0 for >5s for any tenant during peering_promote_inflight_count > 0
- Dashboard: correlate peering events, resolver invalidation calls, and 5xx spikes by tenant
- Postmortem: collect packet captures and egress logs for first canary tenants for incident review
comments: 2026-02-25 - Ava Chen: Triage summary attached; initial customer reports from two enterprise customers: support-2319 and support-2332. Both observed ~10s DNS failures coincident with peering maintenance.
2026-02-27 - Mateo Alvarez: Reproduced in staging with synthetic promote harness. Captured trace showing resolver affinity not invalidated for ~12s after route update.
2026-03-01 - SRE: Adding additional logging to egress-binding-service; will collect live traces during next maintenance window.
2026-03-04 - Engineering: Proposed synchronous invalidate hook drafted; opened PR #4987 targeting runtime-1.22-rc.
2026-03-06 - QA: Integration test added to promote_harness that asserts no stale answers after promote; test failing intermittently due to race in cache invalidation (investigating).
2026-03-08 - Mateo Alvarez: Hotfix PR updated to add exponential backoff on peer confirmation; seeking review from Control Plane and SRE owners.
2026-03-10 - Support: Notified affected customers of planned canary on 2026-03-12; requested monitoring thresholds they want us to notify on.
2026-03-10 - Ava Chen: Marking as In Progress; next steps: finish code review, schedule canary, and prepare postmortem template.

source.title: Token fingerprinting harness for per-model throughput + p95 regression detection
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Create and maintain a reproducible microbenchmark harness that fingerprints tokens/sec and p95 latency for our core served model variants (float32, int8/quantized, and compiled-kernel builds). This harness will be the canonical source-of-truth for per-model throughput baselines used by CI, rollout decisions, and billing guidance. It must exercise streaming & non-streaming paths, warm KV cache behavior, and sequence-length sensitivity.
source.acceptance_criteria: 1) Harness can run against any model in the catalog with a single CLI invocation and emit JSON artifacts (median tokens/sec, p50, p95, p99 latencies, and raw token timelines) to s3://redwood-benchmarks/eng/ENG-35789/<date>/.
source.acceptance_criteria_2: 2) Benchmarks include three canonical sequences (short=64, mid=256, long=1024) and two traffic modes: single-request throughput and steady-streaming concurrency; each target reports median and p95.
source.acceptance_criteria_3: 3) CI integration: a regression detector job consumes the artifact and flags PRs when median throughput drops >7% vs baseline OR p95 latency increases >15% OR statistical test (Wilcoxon) yields p<0.01 for user-impactful distributions.
source.acceptance_criteria_4: 4) Results visible in Console per-model dashboard and hooked into alerting channel (slack #runtime-bench-regressions) with actionable links to raw artifacts and diffs.
source.implementation_notes: - Language: harness in Python for quick iteration; critical runner components in Go micro-binary for parallel load generation.
source.implementation_notes_2: - Use runtime gRPC endpoint when available for lower client overhead; fallback to REST path for parity tests.
source.implementation_notes_3: - Warmup protocol: 3x warmup runs with same prompt length to populate KV cache; separate cold-run mode for cache-less baselines.
source.implementation_notes_4: - Sampling: capture per-token timestamps at 1ms resolution; compress timeline to newline-delimited JSON and upload.
source.test_plan: 1) Unit tests for result parsing & baseline diff logic. 2) End-to-end nightly job that runs on the smallest Dedicated pool (dev-dedicated-smoke) and writes artifacts. 3) Canary: run harness on every runtime-1.22 canary build and compare to last successful baseline. 4) Statistical validation harness that generates synthetic noise to ensure false-positive rate <1%.
progress_updates: 2026-03-02: Spike prototype completed — basic single-request runner implemented and validated against local docker runtime.
2026-03-04: Integrated per-token timestamping and S3 artifact upload; created first artifact set for model 'open-7b' quantized-int8.
2026-03-06: Observed large variance on p95 for streaming workloads when batch size autoscaling is enabled; opened follow-up ENG-35802 to stabilize autoscaler interaction.
2026-03-09: Regression detector design doc posted; threshold defaults proposed (7% median, 15% p95). Planning CI gating after one-week of nightly runs.
2026-03-10: Initial Console integration POC implemented; webhook posts summary card to #runtime-bench-regressions.
source.notes: Design decision: we will treat median tokens/sec as primary SLO metric for throughput regressions and p95 as the safety metric for tail latency. For models with known cold-start penalties (large kv) we store both cold and warm baselines.
source.risks_mitigations: Risk: noisy artifacts from multi-tenant infra. Mitigation: run nightly on isolated dev-dedicated pool and annotate artifacts with instance topology. Risk: false positives in PR gating. Mitigation: use two-stage gate — warn on first failure and block only on persistent regressions across 3 runs.
source.next_steps: 1) Finish Go runner shim and add retries/backoff, 2) Add baseline management service to mark canonical baselines and roll forward, 3) Wire regression detector to PR checks and implement auto-muted exceptions for experimental models.

source.title: Streaming token ingress lane: low-latency preprocessing and hot-prefix cache
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: The prefill/prompt ingestion stage is a measurable hotspot for short-prompt workloads: per-request tokenization, normalization, and prefix handling add ~8–30ms tail latency in our hosted path and amplify CPU pressure on Dedicated nodes. This is particularly visible for streaming requests and small-batch decode lanes where decode-side compute is tiny compared to prefill overhead.

Goal: Reduce per-request prefill overhead by 40–60% for common short prompts (<=64 tokens) while preserving correctness for complex inputs and ensuring CPU usage scales safely under bursty traffic. Secondary goals: reduce p50/p95 of prompt preprocessing, reduce cold-start tokenization cost, and provide knobs to trade CPU vs. memory for Dedicated customers.

Proposed approach: Implement a dedicated "streaming token ingress lane" in the runtime that combines three techniques: (1) hot-prefix microcache (LRU with prefix hashing + length buckets) for normalized prompt prefixes that occur frequently across requests; (2) a fast-path tokenizer: an optimized single-pass UTF-8 scanner + limited normalization that skips heavy unicode folds for ASCII-dominant inputs; (3) async prefill scheduling that coalesces incoming prompt preprocessing operations for the same connection/client into a shared microbatch when latency budget permits.

Design details:
- Hot-prefix cache: store tokenized results for prefix lengths 8/16/32 tokens keyed by normalized text signature and model tokenizer config. TTL and max-resident entries configurable per deployment. Cache is sharded by serving-thread and uses eviction-aware metrics to avoid cross-thread contention.
- Fast-path tokenizer: implement a branchless ASCII fast lane that performs byte-level token boundary detection for common byte ranges and defers full tokenizer only on non-ASCII or ambiguous cases. Provide feature flag to enable fast-path per-model.
- Async prefill scheduling: introduce a short (configurable) scheduling window (1–4ms) in the streaming ingress that will coalesce tokenization tasks for concurrent requests on the same worker; falls back to eager tokenization if request requests streaming-first-chunk or low-latency flag set.
- Integration with kernel selection/attention scheduling: annotate prefill tokens with downstream sequence-length hints so kernel scheduler can choose early decode kernels or KV-cache warmup kernels ahead of decode. This reduces model-side stalls when prefill and decode overlap.

Trade-offs and risks:
- Correctness: fast-path must be 100% consistent with full tokenizer for accepted inputs. We will assert equivalence for a large fuzz corpus; fallback to full tokenizer on mismatch.
- Memory: prefix cache increases memory per-worker; provide cluster-level caps and charge memory to Dedicated customer quotas.
- Latency vs throughput: scheduling window can add ms to p50 but reduces tail and CPU; default 2ms is conservative.
- Security: cached tokenizations must be sanitized for customer isolation; per-tenant namespacing will be used for Hosted and Dedicated.

Metrics and acceptance criteria:
- Short prompts (<=64 tokens): median preprocessing latency reduced >=50%, p95 reduced >=30%.
- End-to-end user-visible latency for streaming-first-token not regressed (>5% increase) for any RBAC tier.
- CPU cycles spent on tokenization per 10k requests reduced by >=40% in benchmarks.
- No tokenizer-equivalence failures in production rollout (>=1M evals).

Implementation plan (phases):
1) Prototype fast-path tokenizer as a library module and run local equivalence fuzz tests (owner: Diego). (Wk1)
2) Hot-prefix cache PoC behind feature flag with synthetic workload harness; measure hit rates and eviction behavior. (Wk2)
3) Async coalescing scheduler integrated into streaming ingress with microbench harness. (Wk2–Wk3)
4) End-to-end integration tests, kernel hinting hook, and trace spans for observability. (Wk4)
5) Canary rollout to a fraction of traffic (2–5%) on Hosted; Dedicated opt-in via config. Monitor metrics for 48–72h. (Wk5)
6) Full rollout and postmortem. (Wk6)

Testing plan: unit tests for fast-path, equivalence testing vs full tokenizer, load tests with short-prompt workloads (chat startups, search queries), chaos testing for cache misses and eviction storms.

Monitoring and dashboards: add ingestion-latency, tokenize-cpu, prefix-cache-hit-rate, token-equivalence-failures, per-model fast-path enable metric, and scheduling-coalesce-rate. Correlate with downstream kernel selection metrics to validate warmup benefits.

Rollout controls: feature flags at model and deployment level, per-customer memory caps, and a safe-mode that disables async coalescing when p99 decoding latency regresses.

Follow-ups: expose cache diagnostics in Console, provide CLI for cache flush on config changes, and propose a Dedicated tenant setting to move prefix cache to a separate process to limit interference.
comments: 2026-03-02 Diego Ramirez: Initial prototype of ASCII fast-path tokenizer implemented. Local fuzz runs show 0 mismatches on 100k random ASCII examples.
2026-03-04 Ava Chen: Added hot-prefix cache PoC. Observed ~18% CPU reduction in synthetic traffic with 50% repeated prefixes; need to tune shard count to reduce lock contention.
2026-03-06 SRE (Maya Patel): Concern about per-worker memory on high-concurrency Dedicated nodes. Suggested adding strict per-shard caps and sampling-based eviction metrics.
2026-03-08 Diego Ramirez: Async coalescing scheduler integrated. End-to-end benchmark shows p50 preprocess down 46% and p95 down 28% for short prompts on test harness when coalesce-window=2ms.
2026-03-09 Product (Liam O'Neill): Please add a Console flag so support can toggle per-customer fast-path for Dedicated customers during onboarding.
2026-03-10 QA (Nina Gupta): Equivalence fuzz corpus expanded to 2M cases including unicode edge cases; fast-path falls back correctly on mismatch. Ready for canary after infra config for cache caps.

source.title: Tokenwise requant budgeter and kernel provenance harness for dovetailed attention scheduling
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: Mixed-precision pipelines (INT8/FP8) introduce per-token dequant/requant overhead that is disproportionately paid during prefill and early decode windows, increasing P95 latency and token cost for short-turn conversations. Current kernel selection lacks fine-grained token-aware budgeting and provenance telemetry, making it hard to decide when to incur a dequant vs. pick a slower FP32/FP16 kernel.

Proposal: Implement a "tokenwise requant budgeter" in the runtime scheduler that tracks a sliding budget across the active token window and decides, per token/head-group, whether to route to an INT8 fastpath with a requantization at the epilogue or to a safer FP8/FP16 kernel. Complement this with a "kernel provenance harness": lightweight telemetry attached to each kernel decision that records the features used to decide the path (sequence length, kv-hit ratio, headwise entropy, batch shape, latency budget remaining, cache hits) and the observed cost/latency after execution.

Core deliverables:
- Scheduler change that maintains a per-request token budget (configurable by route/model) and makes tokenwise routing decisions during prefill and decode. Budgeting policy should support: fixed token budget, sliding-window proportional budget, and conservative holdback for canary traffic.
- Kernel provenance harness: a compact event emitted per token-chunk with labels (kernel_variant, policy_decision, reason_codes). Events routed to an internal perf stream for post-hoc analysis and automatic policy tuning.
- Dovetailed attention staging: schedule dequant/requant epilogues to overlap with subsequent attention projection compute when safe, reducing visible tail latency.
- Simulation tool: a batch-mode simulator that ingests historical traces (Confluence link below) and runs candidate budget policies to estimate delta cost and latency distribution before deployment.

Acceptance criteria:
- Perf: P95 token-latency for conversational workloads reduced by >= 7% on canary traffic compared to baseline; median latency unchanged or improved.
- Cost: token compute cost (estimated vGPU-seconds normalized) reduced by >= 4% for INT8-friendly workloads.
- Quality: zero significant regressions on guard evals (top-k sampling similarity, logprobs) — measured via existing evaluation harness.
- Observability: kernel provenance events show >= 95% coverage for canary traffic and feed into a dashboard.

Rollout plan:
1) Merge scheduler + harness behind a feature flag (config per-route).
2) Canary on low-volume customers and internal traffic, collect telemetry for 7 days.
3) Run simulation with collected traces to tune budget thresholds.
4) Gradual ramp to 20%/50%/100% with automatic rollback if P95 worsens > 5% or quality checks fail.

Testing & Validation:
- Unit tests for budget math and token accounting.
- Integration tests exercising prefill+decode dovetail where dequant epilogue overlaps next attention.
- Load tests with synthetic short-turn and long-context traces.
- A/B experiments with cost and quality metrics.

Telemetry & Dashboarding:
- Add provenance stream consumer that aggregates counts by kernel_variant, policy_decision, reason_code, and headwise breakdown.
- Dashboard widgets: tokenwise budget burn rate, kernel selection heatmap, delta-latency per kernel variant, and automatic anomaly alerts.

Risks & Mitigations:
- Risk: incorrect budgeting could cause more dequants, increasing cost. Mitigation: conservative default budgets and simulation tuning before ramp.
- Risk: telemetry overhead. Mitigation: provenance events must be compact (single-line protobuf) and sampled (configurable sampling rate) for high-volume routes.
- Risk: subtle quality regressions when preferring INT8 fastpath. Mitigation: automatic eval gating and canary quality checks.

Artifacts:
- Trace bundle (internal drive): https://drive.redwood/example/token-traces-q1-2026
- Design doc (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood/display/RUNTIME/Tokenwise+Requant+Budgeter
- Perf sim (repo): github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/perf-sim/pull/42
- Initial perf dashboard: https://grafana.redwood/internal/d/quant-budgeter-canary

Follow-ups:
- Policy auto-tuner that uses provenance events to do closed-loop threshold optimization.
- Headwise microkernel experimentation to enable mixed head precision within a single attention pass.
comments: 2026-03-02 - Diego Martinez: Initial spike shows token-budgeting math is tractable. Microbench shows 3-6% P95 improvement for short (<=40 token) traces when dovetail overlap succeeds.
2026-03-04 - Priya Nair (PM review): Request adding explicit guardrail to ensure model output quality doesn't degrade beyond eval thresholds; added to acceptance criteria.
2026-03-06 - Samir Patel (Eng review): Noted that provenance stream must be binary-compact and sampled to avoid IO pressure. Suggested per-route sampling factor.
2026-03-09 - Design feedback from Lina Gomez: UX in Console needs a simple toggle and a visualization of budget burn — added to dashboard requirements.
2026-03-10 - Diego Martinez: Feature flag proto merged behind runtime.feature.token_requant_budgeter. Next: simulation run over two weeks of internal traces.

source.title: Plan-driven runtime rollouts: Terraform-gated staged migration with preflight priming and reversible snapshot restore
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Motivation: We need a reproducible, plan-driven way to migrate private (VPC) data-plane runtimes with zero-downtime guarantees and a safe, deterministic rollback path. This ticket defines a playbook + Terraform gating mechanism that uses the plan output (diff), automated preflight validation, lightweight priming probes, and a reversible snapshot workflow for KV/state to enable fast, low-risk rollbacks.

Scope:
- Add a terraform-plan gate step that annotates planned resource changes and computes a risk score (instance churn, network changes, storage moves).
- Implement a preflight controller that runs in the target VPC slice: applies ephemeral terraform in a staging namespace to create a mirrored runtime slice (no public traffic).
- Run the following validations against the mirrored slice: Terraform diff assertion, synthetic traffic replay (rho-based workload sampled from recent production traces), priming of KV cache prefixes, tail-latency & error-rate SLO checks, and continuous health event checks for 5 minutes.
- If preflight passes, orchestrate a staged traffic shift using weighted L4 routing (no DNS TTL changes) combined with session-lease awareness to ensure sessions are not dropped.
- Prior to first traffic shift, take a consistent snapshot of KV/state (S3 snapshot + metadata manifest). Snapshot becomes the rollback anchor.
- On any health regression above thresholds (configurable: 5% 5xx, 2x p95 latency, or >10% error budget burn in 10m), perform an automated revert: immediate traffic shift back to previous slice, restore snapshot into a new warm slice, keep degraded slice isolated for postmortem.

Acceptance criteria:
- Terraform pipeline can block a rollout when plan risk score >= configurable threshold.
- Preflight mirrored slice runs synthetic traffic and reports pass/fail with telemetry attached to the plan.
- Rollout can be aborted or rolled back via a single operator command that: (1) shifts traffic, (2) triggers snapshot restore, (3) runs smoke checks on restored slice.
- Clear runbook in Console and a CI job that demonstrates the full cycle in staging (including revert).

Design decisions / trade-offs:
- We choose to rely on weighted L4 routing (faster, less noisy) instead of L7 proxy rewrites to minimize application-layer coupling. Drawback: some tenant-aware routing logic has to be leaked into the orchestrator.
- Snapshot-and-restore is slower than in-place hotpatching but provides deterministic, reproducible rollback paths for stateful tenants; acceptable for high-risk private customers.
- We opt for an ephemeral preflight slice rather than only synthetic probes against an existing slice to avoid priming interference and to validate infra changes (security groups, subnets).

Implementation notes:
- Terraform: add a new module path modules/runtime/migration-gate with a plan-tagger script that uploads plan artifacts to build storage and posts a summary to the pipeline. Use terraform 1.6+ planfile output and the plan-extractor utility.
- Preflight controller: simple K8s job/operator that spins up mirrored hosts using a tenant-safe instance profile. Controller runs a workload runner (python) which replays traces via the runtime gRPC ingestion endpoint.
- Priming: capture top-N prefixes from production KV hot keys, prefetch into mirrored runtime KV cache via a priming API (POST /admin/prime).
- Health checks: integrate with Datadog dashboards (dashboard id: dd-priv-runtime-rollout), publish rollouts as traces to X-Ray-compatible tracing for latency correlation.
- Rollback: snapshot manifest stored at s3://redwood-private-rollbacks/<tenant>/<timestamp>/manifest.json; restore job verified by running the same smoke-suite used during preflight.

Open questions:
- How long should we keep ephemeral preflight slices to balance cost vs. validation fidelity? (Triage: 10-30 minutes default).
- Should the terraform risk scoring be customer-configurable per deployment (some customers accept higher risk for faster upgrades)?

Deliverables:
- Terraform module and pipeline gating step (PR)
- Preflight controller binary + priming API stub
- Runbook (Console & oncall) and a demo CI job in staging showing abort + rollback
- Telemetry dashboard and pager policy

Links & artifacts:
- Design notes: https://confluence.redwood.ai/private/plan-driven-rollouts
- Terraform PR (WIP): https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra/pull/6120
- Preflight controller repo: https://github.com/redwood-inference/private-rollout-controller (branch: plan-gate)
- Datadog dashboard: https://app.datadoghq.com/dashboard/dd-priv-runtime-rollout
