title: Plan-driven runtime rollouts: Terraform-gated staged migration with preflight priming and reversible snapshot restore
preview:
Motivation: We need a reproducible, plan-driven way to migrate private (VPC) data-plane runtimes with zero-downtime guarantees and a safe, deterministic rollback path. This ticket defines a playbook + Terraform gating mechanism that uses the plan output (diff), automated preflight validation, lightweight priming probes, and a reversible snapshot workflow for KV/state to enable fast, low-risk rollbacks.

Scope:
- Add a terraform-plan gate step that annotates planned resource changes and computes a risk score (instance churn, network changes, storage moves).
- Implement a preflight controller that runs in the target VPC slice: applies ephemeral terraform in a staging namespace to create a mirrored runtime slice (no public traffic).
- Run the following validations against the mirrored slice: Terraform diff assertion, synthetic traffic replay (rho-based workload sampled from recent production traces), priming of KV cache prefixes, tail-latency & error-rate SLO checks, and continuous health event checks for 5 minutes.
- If preflight passes, orchestrate a staged traffic shift using weighted L4 routing (no DNS TTL changes) combined with session-lease awareness to ensure sessions are not dropped.
- Prior to first traffic shift, take a consistent snapshot of KV/state (S3 snapshot + metadata manifest). Snapshot becomes the rollback anchor.
- On any health regression above thresholds (configurable: 5% 5xx, 2x p95 latency, or >10% error budget burn in 10m), perform an automated revert: immediate traffic shift back to previous slice, restore snapshot into a new warm slice, keep degraded slice isolated for postmortem.

Acceptance criteria:
- Terraform pipeline can block a rollout when plan risk score >= configurable threshold.
- Preflight mirrored slice runs synthetic traffic and reports pass/fail with telemetry attached to the plan.
- Rollout can be aborted or rolled back via a single operator command that: (1) shifts traffic, (2) triggers snapshot restore, (3) runs smoke checks on restored slice.
- Clear runbook in Console and a CI job that demonstrates the full cycle in staging (including revert).

Design decisions / trade-offs:
- We choose to rely on weighted L4 routing (faster, less noisy) instead of L7 proxy rewrites to minimize application-layer coupling. Drawback: some tenant-aware routing logic has to be leaked into the orchestrator.
- Snapshot-and-restore is slower than in-place hotpatching but provides deterministic, reproducible rollback paths for stateful tenants; acceptable for high-risk private customers.
- We opt for an ephemeral preflight slice rather than only synthetic probes against an existing slice to avoid priming interference and to validate infra changes (security groups, subnets).

Implementation notes:
- Terraform: add a new module path modules/runtime/migration-gate with a plan-tagger script that uploads plan artifacts to build storage and posts a summary to the pipeline. Use terraform 1.6+ planfile output and the plan-extractor utility.
- Preflight controller: simple K8s job/operator that spins up mirrored hosts using a tenant-safe instance profile. Controller runs a workload runner (python) which replays traces via the runtime gRPC ingestion endpoint.
- Priming: capture top-N prefixes from production KV hot keys, prefetch into mirrored runtime KV cache via a priming API (POST /admin/prime).
- Health checks: integrate with Datadog dashboards (dashboard id: dd-priv-runtime-rollout), publish rollouts as traces to X-Ray-compatible tracing for latency correlation.
- Rollback: snapshot manifest stored at s3://redwood-private-rollbacks/<tenant>/<timestamp>/manifest.json; restore job verified by running the same smoke-suite used during preflight.

Open questions:
- How long should we keep ephemeral preflight slices to balance cost vs. validation fidelity? (Triage: 10-30 minutes default).
- Should the terraform risk scoring be customer-configurable per deployment (some customers accept higher risk for faster upgrades)?

Deliverables:
- Terraform module and pipeline gating step (PR)
- Preflight controller binary + priming API stub
- Runbook (Console & oncall) and a demo CI job in staging showing abort + rollback
- Telemetry dashboard and pager policy

Links & artifacts:
- Design notes: https://confluence.redwood.ai/private/plan-driven-rollouts
- Terraform PR (WIP): https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra/pull/6120
- Preflight controller repo: https://github.com/redwood-inference/private-rollout-controller (branch: plan-gate)
- Datadog dashboard: https://app.datadoghq.com/dashboard/dd-priv-runtime-rollout

source.title: Short-term amplification zones and weighted overdraft invoicing for Dedicated fleets
source.creator: Mateo Alvarez
source.description: Problem: Customers on Dedicated fleets occasionally need short, high-throughput windows (e.g., product launches) that exceed committed throughput. Today we either reject requests, throttle aggressively, or let requests succeed and then issue blunt overage line items that are hard to explain. Goal: implement 'amplification zones' — controlled, temporary capacity lifts that are 1) meterable at sub-minute granularity, 2) governed by per-tenant weighting and caps, and 3) produce a transparent, prorated overdraft invoice line (weighted overdraft invoicing) instead of opaque overage charges. This ticket covers design and implementation of the allocation algorithm, autoscaling hooks, telemetry, UI flags, and the billing attribution surface for invoices.
acceptance_criteria: A1: System can enable a tenant-level amplification window (duration, max uplift %, weighted priority) that increases available throughput for the tenant without violating fleet-wide SLOs.
A2: Amplification allocation respects operator-configured emergency pools and per-tenant caps; requests beyond configured uplift are either queued or token-limited with clear error codes.
A3: Billing records include a weighted overdraft line with per-minute usage, uplift factor applied, and a human-readable explanation suitable for invoices and support.
A4: Console exposes amplification activation, remaining uplift budget, and cost estimate in the Dedicated UI; operators can revoke or throttle active windows.
A5: End-to-end test demonstrates a simulated launch traffic pattern where amplification windows reduce tail latency and produce the correct prorated invoice entries.
A6: Monitoring and alerts for amplification exhaustion, cross-tenant interference, and anomalous uplift usage are wired into existing SLO dashboards.
A7: Documentation (runbook + developer guide) is linked from the ticket and reviewed by PM and billing.
A8: Feature toggles allow staged rollout: operator-only, customer opt-in, and full-auto modes.
A9: Updated changelog and migration notes for customers who previously received flat overage invoices.
tasks: Design allocation algorithm: weighted fair-share with per-tenant boost ceilings (owner: Jordan)
Extend autoscaler to accept temporary uplift policies and preemptive scale signals (owner: Priya Raman)
Create billing pipeline changes: per-minute overdraft computation and invoice line generation (owner: Nikhil Patel)
Console: add Amplification panel with budget, estimate, and revoke controls (owner: Elena Vo)
Telemetry: new metrics (amplification_uptime, uplift_consumed, uplift_cost_estimate) and dashboard tiles (owner: SRE)
E2E integration test harness and synthetic workload scenario (owner: QA)
Operator runbook and incident playbook update (owner: Ops)
API surface: enable amplify endpoint with validation and idempotency (owner: API team)
Security review of billing data export format (owner: Security)
Customer communication copy for billing change (owner: Growth)
Create migration script for historical overage reconciliation (owner: Billing)
Feature gate implementation and gradual rollout plan (owner: Product)
Post-launch cost analysis job to validate invoicing accuracy (owner: Data Eng)
notes: Background: Customers ask for short windows of elastic throughput but dislike unpredictable invoices; finance wants clearer, prorated lines tied to observed uplift.
Design constraints: Must not allow a single tenant to starve fleet-critical services. Allocation should be bounded both by absolute GPU vcores and by per-tenant budget tokens.
Billing: We will compute overdraft price = base_rate * uplift_multiplier * minutes_used, where uplift_multiplier derives from tenant weight and time-of-day premiums.
Autoscaling hooks: autoscaler must treat amplification as soft headroom; it can trigger ephemeral scale-up if fleet capacity is available, otherwise enforce queueing/limits.
UI: Estimate must be shown before activation and as a running meter while active. Operators need a force-revoke that gracefully drains in-flight requests with 30s warning.
Alternative considered: instant credit pools assigned per-tenant (rejected due to accounting complexity and long-lived reconciliation).

Related artifacts: Confluence design draft - https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/DED/Amplification+Zones, billing spec - https://docs.redwood.ai/billing/overdrafts, prototype PR - https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra/pull/9876
Edge cases: network partitions where uplift continues to be consumed without billing visibility; plan to add local buffering and reconciliation jobs.
Performance target: while amplification active, p95 latency must remain within 1.25x baseline for the tenant and fleet-wide degradation must be <5%.
Regulatory: For customers in EEA with separate billing regimes, apply regional price overrides (handled in billing pipeline).
Future: Consider marketplace for purchased uplift credits; out of scope for initial rollout.
comments: 2026-02-26 - Jordan Kim: Initial spike test results attached. Amplification reduced tail latency by ~18% for the simulated 10x burst. See artifact: https://graphs.internal.redwood/launch-sim/amp-2026-02-26
2026-02-28 - Priya Raman: Autoscaler prototype can accept a 'soft_boost' flag; needs enhancement to respect per-tenant weight windows and to report back available capacity before committing nodes.
2026-03-02 - Nikhil Patel: Billing schema proposal posted. We need legal sign-off on invoice language; tagging @Legal. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/billing/pull/231
2026-03-04 - Elena Vo: Console mockups uploaded. UX asks for a two-state button (Estimate / Activate) and granular revoke options. Mockup link: https://drive.redwood.ai/amp-console-mock.png

2026-03-06 - SRE (Automated): Integration test failed in staging - uplift accounting drifted by 0.8% over 24h. Root cause: clock skew between uplift agent and billing aggregator. Action: add heartbeat reconciliation job.
2026-03-08 - Mateo Alvarez: Updated the design to include heartbeat reconciliation and a capped reconciliation tolerance (0.1%). Added an operator alert if drift >0.5%.
2026-03-09 - Legal (Ana Morales): Invoice language approved with two small edits; added requirement to surface a link to the detailed usage CSV in all overdraft invoice lines.
2026-03-10 - Jordan Kim: Ready for review. All core components have PRs open: infra (#1021), autoscaler (#988), billing (#231). Requesting review from SRE and Billing leads.
2026-03-11 - PM (Riley Chan): Please confirm rollout cadence. Recommend operator-only for two weeks, then opt-in, then auto default after 60 days if no issues.
2026-03-12 - Ops (Samira Gupta): Runbook draft added. Includes force-revoke play and customer communication templates. Link: https://runbook.redwood/amps

source.title: Isolate transitive crypto deps with runtime policy gate and link-time hiding
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Goal: eliminate accidental exposure and symbol collisions from transitive cryptographic dependencies across the inference runtime and control plane services. We will introduce a runtime policy gate enforced at build/link time (symbol hiding + consumption whitelist) and a CI binary-scan to detect exported crypto symbols. This reduces attack surface, prevents mixed-OpenSSL ABI issues, and provides a safe upgrade path for OpenSSL/JW* libraries used transitively by third-party dependencies.
acceptance_criteria: All runtime microservices in 'inference' and 'auth' teams have no exported OpenSSL or libcrypto symbols in production builds (verified by nm/objdump or scan job)
CI includes a failing job that flags binaries with exported crypto symbols or multiple linked crypto providers
OpenSSL bumped to 3.2.1 for all services that directly link crypto; transitive users are either isolated or pinned
Staged rollout: 10% canary for two weeks with latency and error budgets within 5% of baseline
Operator docs updated (build flags, sanitizer, and emergency rollback steps) and runbook added to Confluence
tasks: Audit (depgraph) to enumerate packages pulling in OpenSSL/JW* transitive deps (owner: Diego)
Design link-time symbol hiding flags and linker scripts for linux builds (owner: Priya Nair)
Implement build changes in Bazel/Make to enable -fvisibility=hidden / --version-script where needed (owner: infra-team)
Create CI binary-scan job that runs nm/objdump and a small python matcher to detect exported crypto symbols (owner: CI)
Upgrade direct crypto consumers to OpenSSL 3.2.1 and pin transitive ones where isolation is not yet possible (owner: runtime-team)
Replace or patch JWT/OAuth libraries that FFI-load native crypto (owner: auth-team)
Run canary rollout with A/B routing and collect console metrics (owner: Diego)
Coordinate security review and ship runtime-1.22 release notes (owner: Security)
Post-mortem and follow-up tickets for any services requiring static linking or vendor forks (owner: Diego)
decisions: Prefer dynamic linking + symbol hiding over static-for-everything to preserve fast CVE patching and image size
Introduce a conservative whitelist policy for services that legitimately export crypto symbols (e.g., packaging/testing tools) rather than a blanket allow
Delay forced replacement of high-risk transitive libs until vendor forks or patches are available; use pinned versions as interim mitigation
risks: Linker script changes can break plugins that rely on symbol visibility (risk to third-party telemetry plugins)
Upgrading OpenSSL may expose ABI differences causing subtle runtime failures under high-load kernels
Canary could reveal QoS regressions due to different memory/layout from symbol hiding; need capacity for rollbacks

2026-03-01 - CI Team: Added binary-scan job in 'ci-security' that fails on exported OpenSSL symbols. Initial run flagged 5 images. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/ci-config/pull/212

2026-03-03 - Diego Morales: Upgraded inference-runtime to OpenSSL 3.2.1 behind feature flag. Canary deployment PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/7890 (WIP). Observed 1.8% latency variance at 5% traffic during smoke tests.
2026-03-05 - Security (Lena Park): Requested threat-modeling review for runtime policy gate and a checklist for release. Security review ticket created: SEC-2026-45

2026-03-08 - Diego Morales: Canaries scheduled to start 2026-03-10 for 10% traffic. Added rollback playbook and emergency patch instructions to Confluence runbook. Noted follow-up: investigate telemetry-agent plugin breakage in staging

Keep compatibility shim for SAML SSO adapters until we verify all orgs are on patched packages
Coordinate with solutions-engineering before rolling changes to Dedicated/Private images used by customers

source.title: session-pool-ballooning-and-prefix-cache-pointer-race-runtime-fix
source.creator: Sofia Martinez
source.description:
Summary: Multiple customers (Hosted burst pools and a Private deployment for a regulated customer) reported sudden GPU OOM spikes followed by degraded throughput. Investigation shows a previously unobserved race between session pool growth (we've named it 'ballooning') and the prefix-store tombstone sweep which caused stale pointer chains to accumulate in the prefix cache metadata, preventing GC and causing page saturation on GPU arenas. The failure surface included increased allocator retention, long-lived session objects pinned by expired prefix entries, and cascading OOMs under bursty traffic patterns.

Impact: Incident affected three customer environments intermittently over the past 36 hours: two hosted burst pools (short latency spikes then OOMs) and one private cluster where a single tenant experienced sustained OOMs and service degradation for ~45 minutes during peak. Traffic patterns were short, high-concurrency chat bursts with high prefix reuse (system prompts + user history).

Root cause hypothesis: A loose invariant in the prefix-store's tombstone reconciliation path allowed a pointer to a session's suffix metadata to survive a concurrent compaction path. When session eviction ran concurrently with prefix-store sweep under a race window, the allocator's free list got inconsistent references to session-block ranges. Those references prevented coalescing of adjacent free spans in the GPU allocator and created small, long-lived fragments that accumulated until a critical page-saturation threshold was hit on certain GPU instance types. The underlying contributing factors were: (1) the lazy-evict path deferring metadata unlink, (2) an optimistic prefix index reconcile that didn't acquire the session handle lock, and (3) a kernel scheduling pattern under high concurrency that elongated the race window.

Temporary mitigations applied:
- Rollout of a fast mitigation to Hosted pools: reduced burst concurrency on affected pools and enabled aggressive prefix TTL truncation for the last 24 hours to limit pointer lifetime (hosted gate patch rolled to 2 AZs).
- For the Private customer: we applied a targeted restart of inference daemons (graceful draining + restart) and enabled a temporary node-level eviction policy to force coalescing.
- Emergency telemetry: increased sampling on allocator free-list sizes, prefix-store tombstone counts, session-handle contention metrics, and GPU page utilization; alerted at lower thresholds.

Permanent fix (planned):
1) Immediate runtime patch (this ticket) to make prefix-store tombstone reconciliation acquire the session handle and perform a small memory barrier + pointer nulling sequence before completing unlink. This removes the race window and ensures allocator sees consistent free spans.
2) Add a defensive validator that scans short-lived tombstones on heal to detect and repair stale pointers (low-overhead, opt-in via runtime flag).
3) Improve allocator coalescer to tolerate transient dangling references by verifying block ownership before rejecting coalescing (safeguard against future races).
4) Add an e2e stress test in CI that reproduces the session-evict + prefix-sweep interleaving under high concurrency and short TTLs to prevent regressions.

Rollout plan:
- Create PR with small locking + memory fence sequence and unit tests (target: runtime-1.22.0 patch branch).
- Canary deploy to one hosted AZ (non-customer) with synthetic load mirroring bursty chat traffic, monitor allocator metrics for 4 hours.
- If green, expand to affected hosted pools (2 AZs) and coordinate with Private customer for a maintenance window to apply patch + restart.
- Full rollout to runtime fleet within 72 hours contingent on canary results.

Testing & validation:
- Unit tests cover pointer nulling and lock acquisition order.
- Repro harness: added a deterministic interleaving simulator to our stress repo and a long-run job that reproduces the allocator fragmentation signature.
- Add smoke-check script to verify no regression in steady-state throughput and tail latency.

Customer comms:
- Notified the Private tenant’s SRE and account team with a mitigation summary and a scheduled maintenance window (2026-03-10 02:00 UTC).
- Hosted customers on affected pools received an incident advisory and workaround note for reduced concurrency.

Follow-ups (post-release):
- Postmortem documenting timeline, decision points, and RCA (owner: incident lead).
- Backfill telemetry dashboards to include tombstone age percentile and allocator coalesce rate.
- Review whether prefix TTL defaults and lazy-evict heuristics need tuning for high prefix-reuse workloads.

Files & links:
- GH PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/1289 (WIP)
- Stress harness: https://github.com/redwood-inference/stress/tree/ballooning-repro
- Incident notes (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/INC/2026-03-02+Session+Pool+Ballooning
comments: 2026-03-02 - 09:12 UTC - Sofia Martinez: Triage - multiple customer escalations reported; initial heap/allocator traces captured by on-call. Assigned to Ethan. Marking P0 due to private tenant impact.

2026-03-02 - 14:05 UTC - Ethan Park: Collected full GPU resident set maps and allocator histograms from affected nodes. Observed growing free-list fragmentation correlated with prefix tombstone counts. Repro attempt in lab started.

2026-03-03 - 02:41 UTC - Ethan Park: Repro succeeded in stress harness when introducing artificial scheduling preemption; confirmed race between prefix-sweep and session-evict. Drafting minimal locking patch. Filing PR stub.
2026-03-04 - 18:23 UTC - Priya Rao (SRE): Canary plan approved. Alert thresholds lowered for allocator saturation. Private customer maintenance scheduled.
2026-03-06 - 07:10 UTC - Design Review by Wei Zhang: Reviewed telemetry dashboards and recommended adding percentiles for tombstone age and block-coalesce latency. Moved to follow-up workstream.
2026-03-08 - 11:55 UTC - Ethan Park: WIP PR pushed (link above). Running CI and extended stress jobs. Will initiate canary upon green.

title: session-pool-ballooning-and-prefix-cache-pointer-race-runtime-fix
preview:
Summary: Multiple customers (Hosted burst pools and a Private deployment for a regulated customer) reported sudden GPU OOM spikes followed by degraded throughput. Investigation shows a previously unobserved race between session pool growth (we've named it 'ballooning') and the prefix-store tombstone sweep which caused stale pointer chains to accumulate in the prefix cache metadata, preventing GC and causing page saturation on GPU arenas. The failure surface included increased allocator retention, long-lived session objects pinned by expired prefix entries, and cascading OOMs under bursty traffic patterns.

Impact: Incident affected three customer environments intermittently over the past 36 hours: two hosted burst pools (short latency spikes then OOMs) and one private cluster where a single tenant experienced sustained OOMs and service degradation for ~45 minutes during peak. Traffic patterns were short, high-concurrency chat bursts with high prefix reuse (system prompts + user history).

Root cause hypothesis: A loose invariant in the prefix-store's tombstone reconciliation path allowed a pointer to a session's suffix metadata to survive a concurrent compaction path. When session eviction ran concurrently with prefix-store sweep under a race window, the allocator's free list got inconsistent references to session-block ranges. Those references prevented coalescing of adjacent free spans in the GPU allocator and created small, long-lived fragments that accumulated until a critical page-saturation threshold was hit on certain GPU instance types. The underlying contributing factors were: (1) the lazy-evict path deferring metadata unlink, (2) an optimistic prefix index reconcile that didn't acquire the session handle lock, and (3) a kernel scheduling pattern under high concurrency that elongated the race window.

Temporary mitigations applied:
- Rollout of a fast mitigation to Hosted pools: reduced burst concurrency on affected pools and enabled aggressive prefix TTL truncation for the last 24 hours to limit pointer lifetime (hosted gate patch rolled to 2 AZs).
- For the Private customer: we applied a targeted restart of inference daemons (graceful draining + restart) and enabled a temporary node-level eviction policy to force coalescing.
- Emergency telemetry: increased sampling on allocator free-list sizes, prefix-store tombstone counts, session-handle contention metrics, and GPU page utilization; alerted at lower thresholds.

Permanent fix (planned):
1) Immediate runtime patch (this ticket) to make prefix-store tombstone reconciliation acquire the session handle and perform a small memory barrier + pointer nulling sequence before completing unlink. This removes the race window and ensures allocator sees consistent free spans.
2) Add a defensive validator that scans short-lived tombstones on heal to detect and repair stale pointers (low-overhead, opt-in via runtime flag).
3) Improve allocator coalescer to tolerate transient dangling references by verifying block ownership before rejecting coalescing (safeguard against future races).
4) Add an e2e stress test in CI that reproduces the session-evict + prefix-sweep interleaving under high concurrency and short TTLs to prevent regressions.

Rollout plan:
- Create PR with small locking + memory fence sequence and unit tests (target: runtime-1.22.0 patch branch).
- Canary deploy to one hosted AZ (non-customer) with synthetic load mirroring bursty chat traffic, monitor allocator metrics for 4 hours.
- If green, expand to affected hosted pools (2 AZs) and coordinate with Private customer for a maintenance window to apply patch + restart.
- Full rollout to runtime fleet within 72 hours contingent on canary results.

Testing & validation:
- Unit tests cover pointer nulling and lock acquisition order.
- Repro harness: added a deterministic interleaving simulator to our stress repo and a long-run job that reproduces the allocator fragmentation signature.
- Add smoke-check script to verify no regression in steady-state throughput and tail latency.

Customer comms:
- Notified the Private tenant’s SRE and account team with a mitigation summary and a scheduled maintenance window (2026-03-10 02:00 UTC).
- Hosted customers on affected pools received an incident advisory and workaround note for reduced concurrency.

Follow-ups (post-release):
- Postmortem documenting timeline, decision points, and RCA (owner: incident lead).
- Backfill telemetry dashboards to include tombstone age percentile and allocator coalesce rate.
- Review whether prefix TTL defaults and lazy-evict heuristics need tuning for high prefix-reuse workloads.

Files & links:
- GH PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/1289 (WIP)
- Stress harness: https://github.com/redwood-inference/stress/tree/ballooning-repro
- Incident notes (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/INC/2026-03-02+Session+Pool+Ballooning

source.title: Cost Sentinel: Continuous Workload Profiling and Safe Batch Parameter Synthesizer
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Deliver a service component (Cost Sentinel) that continuously profiles production request streams to synthesize safe batching parameters and caching heuristics. The synthesizer will propose incremental config updates that can be applied automatically under conservative safety checks, and provide an automatic rollback path when SLOs regress or error rates increase. This ticket scopes the initial engine, telemetry contracts, canary rollout plan, and integration points with the Console rollout APIs and the autoscaling subsystem.
source.problem_statement: Manual batching/caching tuning is time-consuming, error-prone, and frequently suboptimal as workload shapes drift. Customers want reliable cost improvements without sacrificing latency SLOs or introducing instability during config changes.
goals: Generate per-workload batching parameter suggestions (target batch size, max latency budget, prefix cutoffs) from continuous production telemetry
Provide a heuristics layer that translates recommendations into safe config patches with guardrails (rate limits, max change per window, dependency checks)
Automate canary apply and rollback using existing rollout APIs with explicit SLO monitoring and blast-radius controls
Expose recommendations and simulated cost/latency tradeoffs in Console for operator approval
non_goals: Replace model-level architecture tuning (quantization/compilation) in this phase
Full autoscaling policy redesign; will integrate with existing autoscaler only for observation and trigger hooks
acceptance_criteria: Cost Sentinel produces a daily recommendation bundle for a sample of 50 active workloads with estimated token-cost delta and latency impact
Automated canary apply path executes a staged rollout (1% -> 10% -> 50% traffic) with automatic pause on 95th latency increase > 10% or error-rate increase > 0.5%
Console shows recommendation diff, simulated cost impact, and a one-click approve/apply option
If applied, the system can rollback to the prior config within 3 minutes of trigger and generate a post-mortem entry
Unit and integration tests cover the heuristics translator and the canary state machine
source.technical_design_summary: The engine will run in a stateless worker pool that consumes aggregated telemetry (per-route token histograms, tail latency, error rates, per-client keys) from the central metrics pipeline. A lightweight profiling job consumes rolling windows (5m, 1h, 24h) and synthesizes candidate parameters using a cost model + safety heuristics module. Recommendations are output as signed JSON patches and sent to the rollout service for staged apply. A monitoring policy monitors configured SLO signals and triggers automatic rollback when thresholds are breached. The heuristics module enforces conservative bounds: max 20% change per 24h, never exceed configured global batch ceiling, and block changes for routes serving <500 requests/day.
implementation_tasks: Define telemetry contract (histogram buckets, cardinality limits) and update metrics pipeline
Build profiler job that computes workload summaries across 5m/1h/24h windows
Implement cost model that maps batch size + latency budget to token-cost and expected latency distribution
Create heuristics translator that converts candidate parameters into rollout-safe JSON patches
Integrate with rollout API to support staged apply and programmatic rollback
Add Console UI panel to review recommendations and simulate impact
Write end-to-end tests including synthetic workload validation
Prepare runbook and post-rollback incident template
risks_and_mitigations: Risk: Over-aggressive batch increases could cause head-tail latency spikes. Mitigation: conservative heuristics, staged canaries, and a strict 95th percentile monitoring threshold
Risk: Metrics cardinality explosion from per-client profiling. Mitigation: cap profiling to top N clients by token volume and aggregate rest to 'long tail' buckets

Risk: False positives in rollback triggering on transient cloud noise. Mitigation: require sustained regression for two consecutive windows (5m + 5m) before rollback; add a short cooldown between rollbacks
Risk: Operators reject automated applies. Mitigation: provide clear simulation numbers and rollback guarantees; enable manual approval mode
rollout_plan: Stage 0: dry-run recommendations in staging cluster and validate against synthetic workloads
Stage 1: produce daily recommendation bundles for 50 production routes (no auto-apply) and surface in Console
Stage 2: enable auto-canary apply for low-risk routes (>=5000 req/day) with staged percentages 1/10/50
Stage 3: expand auto-apply to medium risk routes after 2 weeks of stable metrics
Stage 4: retrospective and policy updates based on observed regressions
notes: Initial experiments in sandbox reduced per-token cost by 11% on average for chat workloads with peak concurrencies between 60-120
We observed a 0.8% 95th latency regression when naive batch doubling was applied — informed the 20% per-24h cap
PM has requested visibility hooks for enterprise tenants to opt-out of auto-apply
updates: 2026-01-25: Kickoff meeting with product and console teams. Agreed on telemetry contract and sample route criteria.
2026-02-10: Profiler POC completed; produces candidate batch sizes and estimated cost deltas. Initial cost model validated against historical billing.
2026-02-18: Heuristics translator implemented; basic guardrails enforced (max change 20%, min traffic threshold). Adding additional dependency checks next.
2026-02-28: Integrated with rollout API; first end-to-end dry-run in staging succeeded. Created rollout metrics dashboard.
2026-03-05: Security review requested for telemetry retention policy. Privacy team requested redaction of request IDs in long-term storage. Working with infra to add aggregation windowing.
2026-03-07: Began Console mockup for recommendation panel. UX review scheduled for 2026-03-12. Planning canary expansion for next cycle.
qa_plan: Unit tests for heuristics translator edge cases (low-traffic, high-error, long-tail buckets)
Integration test that runs synthetic traces through profiler -> translator -> rollout (dry-run) and verifies no config applied
Canary test in staging that measures cost vs latency for controlled traffic patterns
Chaos test that injects latency spikes to validate rollback behavior
postmortem_template: Which recommendation or config patch triggered the change
Metrics before/during/after (p50/p95 latency, error rate, cost delta)

source.title: KV hit allocator and TTL continuous-deployment playbook
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Goal: build a simulator + recommendation engine and a staged-apply playbook that can safely propose per-prefix TTLs, cache-key transforms, and batching-friendly key shaping to reduce per-token inference cost while respecting latency SLOs and rollback constraints.

Scope: offline workload ingest (trace sampler), hit-allocation model (per-prefix hit-rate forecasting with uncertainty), TTL/policy optimizer (cost vs latency trade-off curve), recommendation output (TTL, key rules, expected cost delta, confidence band), and a staged apply/rollback harness integrated with the existing deployment orchestrator and Console.

Model approach: start with a Bayesian hit-allocation model (beta-binomial smoothing for low-traffic prefixes) complemented by a Poisson arrival model for per-prefix request rates. Use hierarchical priors to borrow strength across prefixes with similar token length and route. Compute posterior expected hit-rate uplift from candidate prefix TTL increases and simulate added KV RT savings versus additional cold miss latency (99th percentile). Rank recommendations by expected cost-savings per unit risk (risk = simulated 99p latency delta + percent of traffic exposed to TTL change).

Safety and rollout: recommendations are gated by: 1) a staging canary run at 0.5% traffic for 24h with shadow replay to measure real miss amplification; 2) automated rollback triggers if 99p latency increases by >5% or error rate regression >0.2%; 3) a dry-run budget cap (absolute cost delta) per tenant; 4) backloged staged-apply windows with human approval when risk > threshold. The staged apply job supports progressive ramps (0.5%, 2%, 10%, 50%, 100%) and includes automatic hold-and-notify on signal.

Deliverables: 
- Offline simulator (Jupyter/Notebook + reproducible harness) that produces per-prefix cost/latency trade curves.
- Recommendation engine CLI + REST endpoint that emits TTL suggestions with confidence bands and key-transform patches.
- Staged-apply playbook (automation workflows + runbook) integrated into the deployment orchestrator with rollback probes.
- Console proposal UI wireframe and risk indicators for PMs/SREs.

Success metrics: median expected cost reduction across sampled workloads >=10% with <1% increase in 99p latency; false-positive rollback rate <2% during first month of rollouts.

Linking artifacts: simulator notebook: https://drive.redwood.ai/notes/kv-hit-allocator-sim; design doc: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/OPT/TTL+Allocator+Spec; prototype PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/1234
comments: 2026-02-20 - Ava Chen: Drafted initial proposal after spike. Identified key risk vectors: low-volume prefixes, multi-tenant amplification, and batching interactions. Requested Miguel to prototype posterior estimator.

2026-02-25 - Miguel Rivera: Prototype completed. Early sim on 7-day trace (prod-sample-2026-02) shows median cost reduction 12% and 99p latency delta +0.6% on synthetic workloads. Notebook attached to the PR. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/1234
2026-02-27 - Priya Nair (PM): Reviewed acceptance criteria. PM requested a conservative canary flow and explicit per-tenant budget caps. Added requirement: Console risk indicator for recommendations.

2026-03-01 - Lina Park (SRE): Ran shadow replay on staging with 1% traffic for 6h. Observed two prefixes where miss amplification caused CPU spikes on inference nodes. Action: add per-prefix CPU cost estimate and exclude prefixes flagged with high model size variance.

2026-03-03 - Design review (Felix Huang): UX wireframe for Console proposal page uploaded. Feedback: surface expected savings, confidence band, canary ramp controls, and quick revert button. Added label 'needs-ux-iteration'.

2026-03-05 - Miguel Rivera: Implemented staged-apply job with rollback probes. Integration tests passing locally. Blocker: need rollout policy hook in orchestrator (ticket created ENG-100046). Next: add automated alerting integration with PagerDuty for rollback events.

source.title: Reservation deficit detector and graded throttling ramp for noisy tenants
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Summary: After the 2026-02-11 capacity incident we observed recurring evictions and elevated P99 latencies caused by tenants that rapidly consumed burst tokens and drained node reservations. This ticket implements a lightweight "reservation deficit" detector that continuously estimates when a tenant's effective usage exceeds its reservation over a short rolling window and applies a graded throttling ramp instead of immediate hard ejection. Goals: reduce noisy-neighbor-driven evictions, preserve tail latency for stable tenants, and provide transparent signal for operators to decide reservation reconciliation or customer outreach.
source.background: Postmortem link: https://confluence.redwood.ai/incidents/dedicated-2026-02-11. Observed patterns: (1) tenants that do many short high-token bursts cause reservation sinks when KV cache growth + long sequences combine with batching spikes; (2) hard ejection causes cascading rebalancing and higher churn; (3) the current burst tokenbucket is binary for soft vs hard limits and lacks progressive backpressure. Similar mitigations in prior tickets reduced frequency but not variance. This detector is intended to be minimally invasive and reversible.
acceptance_criteria: Detector emits a time-series metric 'tenant_reservation_deficit' with clear threshold labels (ok/warning/critical)
Throttle ramp applies per-tenant rate limits in 3 grades: warning (small slowdown), restrict (50% token allowance), hard-throttle (queue/deny) and logs rationale
No more than 5% increase in median throughput for unaffected tenants during canary rollout; P99 tail latency decreases for impacted hosts vs baseline within 24h
Automated e2e test that simulates bursty workload and validates detector triggers and ramp behavior
Runbook updated with actions for operator escalation and manual reservation reconciliation
tasks: Design detector signal: sliding 5m window compare cumulative tokens vs reservation allowance (work with telemetry team)
Implement detector service in allocator (golang): ingest per-tenant usage, output metric and reason tags
Add graded throttling policy hooks to request router: warning -> token delay; restrict -> cap token allotment; hard-throttle -> 429 with Retry-After
Create simulation harness for burst patterns and run on staging clusters with synthetic tenants
Add dashboards and alerts: reserve-deficit SLI and host-level aggregation
Prepare canary rollout plan and rollback criteria
Post-deploy soak and calibration phase to tune thresholds
design_notes: Detector algorithm: exponential EWMA for short-term usage plus a simple deficit counter (tokens_used - nominal_reservation) clipped to recent max
Hysteresis: require deficit above warning_threshold for 2 consecutive windows before escalating; require 4 consecutive windows to reach hard-throttle to avoid oscillation
Interaction with autoscaler: detector should not trigger during controlled autoscale events; mark autoscale windows as exempt
KV cache interactions: we add a KV cache growth factor to the reservation baseline for tenants with known long-seq workloads to reduce false positives
Operational visibility: every throttle event must include tenant_id, reason, deficit_value, window_seconds, and active_reservation
tradeoffs_and_risks: False positives: overly aggressive thresholds could throttle healthy tenants. Mitigation: staged rollout with increased logging and honey-tenant tests
Complexity vs benefit: keeps allocator simpler by not reallocating reservations automatically, requiring follow-up tickets for auto-reconciliation
Customer experience: graded throttling introduces some 429s which may surface to clients; we will coordinate with account teams for high-value customers
Performance: detector adds small CPU/ memory overhead in allocator; limit sampling to tenants with >X qps to reduce cost
rollout_plan: Feature gated behind runtime flag 'reserv_deficit_detector'
Canary: enable on 2 staging dedicated clusters with synthetic traffic and one small production shard (low-risk customers)
Calibration: run 72h soak, adjust thresholds to maintain <1% false-positive rate on canary tenants
Production ramp: enable 10% -> 30% -> 60% -> 100% of shards over 7 days with monitoring gates
Rollback criteria: >5% increase in spontaneous evictions, >2x error rate for non-target tenants, or any severe customer complaint escalated to SRE
test_plan: Unit tests for detector math and state transitions
Integration tests in CI that spin up allocator + router and validate throttling grades
Load test in staging: synthetic tenants with burst profiles (short-high, long-moderate, steady-high)
Chaos test: simultaneous autoscale + detector activity to validate exemptions
A/B experiment in prod: control shards without detector vs experiment shards measuring P99 latency and eviction counts
monitoring_and_alerting: SLOs: alert if host-level deficit events > threshold over 1h
Dashboards: 'Reservation Deficit Overview' with top 20 tenants by deficit and histogram of deficit durations
Pager: SRE on-call is alerted for critical-level sustained deficits (>30m) on production shards
implementation_notes: Work will live in repo: github.com/redwood-inference/allocator under /pkg/deficitdetector
Prefer simple in-memory sliding windows per-tenant and emit aggregated metrics to prometheus
Add config knobs: window_seconds, warning_threshold, restrict_threshold, hard_threshold, min_qps_to_sample
Include correlation tags for downstream tracing to tie throttle events to requests
comments: 2026-02-28 - Ava Chen: Initial ticket created after postmortem review. Assigned to Rohit for prototype. Action: determine sampling and threshold defaults.
2026-03-01 - Rohit Patel: Prototype detector implemented in allocator sandbox. Early results show clear separation for synthetic noisy tenant. Next: integrate graded throttle into router hook.
2026-03-02 - SRE: Request exemption window for autoscale events added to design notes; otherwise detector generated several false positives during triggered scaling in staging.

2026-03-03 - PM (Elena Martinez): Coordinate with account ops for communication plan. For a subset of high-value customers, we will start in 'warning' only mode for 2 weeks before enabling restrict grades.
2026-03-04 - Design (Maya Singh): Reviewed UX for tenant dashboards; suggested adding per-tenant recent sequence length distribution to help calibrate KV cache factor. Accepted and added to tasks.
next_steps: Complete router integration for graded throttle (assigned: Rohit)
Push prototype PR and run staging harness (due: 2026-03-10)
Prepare ops runbook and notify account teams for canary window (owner: Elena)
Schedule post-canary retro to decide on automated reservation reconciliation follow-up

Goals: reduce noisy-neighbor-driven evictions, preserve tail latency for stable tenants, and provide transparent signal for operators to decide reservation reconciliation or customer outreach.
text: Summary: After the 2026-02-11 capacity incident we observed recurring evictions and elevated P99 latencies caused by tenants that rapidly consumed burst tokens and drained node reservations. This ticket implements a lightweight "reservation deficit" detector that continuously estimates when a tenant's effective usage exceeds its reservation over a short rolling window and applies a graded throttling ramp instead of immediate hard ejection. Goals: reduce noisy-neighbor-driven evictions, preserve tail latency for stable tenants, and provide transparent signal for operators to decide reservation reconciliation or customer outreach.

source.title: SLO Arbitration Fabric: proportional soft-reservations & transient KV slab leasing for continuous batching
source.creator: Ethan Park
source.description: Problem: The current continuous batching system uses hard preemption heuristics that either over-evict KV cache (hurting long-context workloads) or favor throughput-heavy tenants at the expense of latency SLOs for small interactive tenants. Goals: 1) Implement an SLO Arbitration Fabric (SAF) that enforces per-tenant latency budgets while preserving aggregate GPU utilization; 2) Introduce proportional soft-reservations for batch slots and transient KV slab leases to avoid hard eviction storms; 3) Provide a predictable degradation path that trades small throughput drops for latency guarantees; 4) Integrate monitoring, simulation, and rollout plan to validate safety before full rollout.
acceptance_criteria: SLO Arbitration Fabric implemented in serving runtime with a configurable tenant SLO input surface
Soft-reservation mechanism reserves fractional batch capacity per-tenant and respects global GPU memory headroom
Transient KV slab leases allow short-term KV residency for in-flight requests and expire gracefully under sustained pressure
Measured 95p tail latency for interactive tenants meets configured SLOs under mixed load in staging (>90% of test runs)
End-to-end perf regression < 5% p95 latency for background throughput tenants in default config
Feature gated and rolloutable by region and tenant tier via Console rollout controls
design_decisions: Use proportional soft-reservations: each tenant gets a fractional share of batch admission credits based on weight and recent SLO violations
Introduce transient KV slab leases (timebox + memory cap) instead of immediate KV slab eviction to prevent cascading misses
Admission controller will consider effective latency budget, current KV hit-rate delta, and GPU free memory when deciding to admit or delay a request
Implement a lightweight predictive shrinker that uses short-window statistics (1s-10s) to temporarily reduce soft-reservations for tenants showing sustained low utility
Avoid immediate cross-tenant preemption; prefer voluntary yield signals to long-running requests and in-flight KV writebacks to CPU if applicable
Expose config knobs in runtime and Console: reservation weights, lease durations, shrink thresholds, and spill-to-CPU toggles
rollout_plan: Feature behind runtime feature flag and Console toggle
Canary in controlled region with low-risk tenants for 48h, collect latency/throughput/kv-hit dashboards
If canary stable, expand to non-critical regions for 7 days with automated rollback on SLO regressions
Gradual exposure to premium tenants with opt-in; full rollout after 30 days of stable metrics and signoff from Reliability
Post-rollout: keep conservative defaults for soft-reservations and lease durations for 60 days, then iterate based on telemetry
monitoring: New Console dashboard: per-tenant reservation utilization, KV slab occupancy, lease churn rate, p50/p95/p99 latency overlays
Alert: >3% of premium tenants experiencing 2x configured SLO target for >10 minutes
Synthetic load tests in staging with representative mix (interactive 30%, batch 70%) executed on deployment events
Add histogram-based tracing for admission decisions to allow forensic playback of scheduling decisions
test_plan: Unit tests for admission controller decision logic and KV lease lifecycle
Integration tests: multi-tenant local simulation harness with injected noise to validate fairness properties
Performance sims using existing simulator (link in attachments) to sweep reservation weights and lease durations
End-to-end staging runs with production-like models and prompt distributions to verify SLO preservation and memory behavior
risks: Incorrect reservation tuning could regress throughput for background workloads
KV lease expiry heuristics may still cause misses for very bursty long-context tenants
Telemetry gaps could make diagnosing corner-case preemption hard; invest in tracing early
Spill-to-CPU path may introduce unpredictable latency spikes if invoked frequently on certain instance types
comments: 2026-02-20 Ethan Park: RFC posted to Confluence: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SERV/SAF+RFC. High-level simulation results attached.
2026-02-24 Mariana Ortiz: Proof-of-concept admission controller implemented in branch feat/saf/admissions (github.com/redwood/serving-runtime/pull/1827). Initial unit tests green.
2026-02-26 Reliability WG review: asked to add per-tenant tracing and a decay path for reservations. Noted risk around spill-to-CPU on small instances.

2026-03-01 Load test results (staging): p95 for interactive tenants improved by 18% in median of runs, but throughput for batch tenants dipped by ~6% in worst-case config. Adjusted default weights to favor stability.
2026-03-02 Design review accepted pending dashboard widgets and alert thresholds. Assigned rollout milestones and test ownership to SRE. PR 1827 updated and ready for integration tests.
Pending: finalize knob names in Console and add migration notes for Dedicated customers with strict memory commitments.

source.title: Sinuous regional routing policy: composite latency + capacity steering with safe fallbacks
source.creator: Priya Natarajan
source.description: Design and implement a new multi-region request steering policy (codename: 'sinuous') that combines latency-based routing, geo pinning for affinity, and capacity-aware steering into a single compact decision function. The goal is to improve SLO adherence for 99th pct latency during cross-region incidents while minimizing unnecessary cross-region egress and avoiding capacity hot-spots. The policy must integrate circuit breaker signals and graded load-shedding tiers for graceful degradation.
source.background: We currently have several orthogonal steering heuristics (latency hedging, geo affinity, capacity hints) implemented as separate pluggable steps. In practice edge cases create oscillation between hints, and no single policy enforces safe fallbacks during partial regional failures. Incidents in Q4 2025 and early 2026 showed: (1) noisy hedging causing double-requests in failover windows, (2) cross-region cost spikes due to overly aggressive geo-eviction, and (3) tails that missed SLOs when regional autoscalers lagged. This ticket consolidates research into a unified scoring model with explicit circuit-breaker and load-shed integration.
source.proposal_summary: Introduce a composite score: S = w_lat * normalized_latency_score + w_cap * headroom_score + w_aff * affinity_score + w_cost * cost_penalty. Bind S thresholds to action tiers: local route, soft-hedge (parallel request to fallback), hard-failover (single request to fallback), shed (reject with downgrade or cached response). Circuit breakers (per-region) will raise S by a fixed penalty to force evictions. Create a runtime flag 'sinuous_v1' and a canary rollout to dedicated accounts before broad enabled rollout.
architecture_notes: Score components computed at ingress proxy with lightweight cache of recent regional headroom (sourced from autoscaler metrics) - stale <= 5s
Latency score uses 95th-windowed baseline per-region-per-model, normalized to [0,1] with hysteresis to avoid flapping
Affinity score decays over time if a region reports increased error-rate or decreased headroom
Circuit breaker state is driven by central control-plane with local fast-paths to avoid control-plane unavailability
Load-shedding tiers: T1 (background/non-critical low priority) -> T2 (best-effort degrade: compressed tokens / partial responses) -> T3 (hard reject with fallback)
acceptance_criteria: End-to-end policy implemented behind feature flag 'sinuous_v1'
Unit and integration tests covering score components, threshold transitions, and circuit-breaker interactions
Benchmark: under synthetic regional outage (50% capacity loss) the global 99th latency for transit-critical routes improves by >= 20% vs current baseline
Cost impact: cross-region egress increase must be <= 10% in canary accounts vs baseline (measured over 72h) unless customer-level opt-in disables cross-region fallback
Canary rollout to 10 dedicated customers with monitoring dashboards, followed by progressive ramp to 50% of traffic after 7 days if no regression
Operational docs: runbook, dashboard panels, and SLAs for rollback triggers are published
tasks: Design: finalize score formula and thresholds (owner: Priya) - doc: confluence/redwood/sinuous-design
Simulations: run traffic replay with 10 historical incidents and annotate region-level metrics (owner: analytics)
Runtime changes: implement scoring at ingress proxy and exposure of headroom cache (owner: runtime team)
Control-plane: circuit-breaker state API and safety tokens (owner: control-plane)
Feature flag and rollout pipeline (owner: platform)
E2E tests and canary harness additions (owner: test-infra)
Observability: Grafana panels for score distribution, per-region headroom, hedging count, shed events (owner: observability)
Documentation: runbook, operational playbook, and customer-facing support notes (owner: docs)
milestones: 2026-02-27: simulations complete and threshold candidates proposed
2026-03-26: staged rollout to 50% traffic if no regressions
metrics: Global SLO coverage: 99th latency per route (pre/post comparison)
Cross-region egress (GiB) and cost delta vs baseline
Percentage of requests hedged (soft-hedge count / total)
Shed events by tier and by customer
Regional headroom telemetry (vCPU/GPU utilization, queued requests)
rollout_plan: Enable sinuorous_v1 (flag off) in staging and run 48h smoke tests
Canary: enable flag for 10 dedicated customers with traffic snapshotting and live alerting
Observe for 72h and run regression suite comparing 95/99 latency, error-rate, and egress costs
If stable, progressively enable for 25% of traffic for 72h, then 50% for 72h
Full rollout gated by no P0 regressions and acceptable cost delta
rollback_triggers: Sustained 99th-latency regression > 15% for critical routes over 30 minutes
Increase in customer-facing error-rate (5xx) > 0.5% absolute vs baseline sustained 15 minutes
Cross-region egress spike > 25% in canary wallets or customers flagged as cost-sensitive
Unexpected high rate of shed events (>0.1% of total requests) for critical customers
comments: 2026-02-16 Priya: initial draft posted to confluence and requested design review with runtime and control-plane
2026-02-18 Mark R.: suggest using exponential decay for affinity score; avoids abrupt affinity loss during transient blips
2026-02-22 Analytics: ran replay of 8 incidents — preliminary thresholds show 18-24% improvement in 99th for critical routes under simulated regional capacity loss
2026-02-25 Security (Ana): flagged that circuit-breaker control-plane API needs authenticated service tokens and audit logging; added as dependency
2026-03-01 Liam: opened PR: github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/2147 (ingress scoring core) - needs perf bench
2026-03-02 Observability (Vera): dashboard prototype available at grafana.redwood.com/d/sinuous/sinuous-routing
